The most common issues reported are chronic low back pain, ipsilateral knee degeneration and laxity, and contralateral hip arthritis.

search_keywords: Adjacent joint degeneration after hip arthrodesis

content.title: Hip Arthrodesis Prognosis
content.definition: Hip arthrodesis reliably provides pain relief and good clinical results, but its long-term success is frequently limited by adjacent joint degeneration, most commonly affecting the lumbar spine and ipsilateral knee, which may necessitate eventual conversion to a total hip arthroplasty.
content.summary: Hip arthrodesis provides pain relief and reasonable clinical outcomes in most patients. Success is frequently limited in the long term by adjacent joint degeneration, which affects up to 60% of patients, commonly involving the lumbar spine, ipsilateral knee, or contralateral hip.

content.title: THA Prosthesis Design Introduction
content.definition: The biomechanics of total hip arthroplasty depend on the prosthesis design (cemented, press-fit, or modular stems and cups), the bearing surface (polyethylene, metal, or ceramic) and its lubrication characteristics, and the fixation method.
content.summary: This section provides an essential overview of total hip arthroplasty (THA), detailing how prosthesis biomechanics are influenced by design, fixation method, and bearing surface characteristics. It explores the history of THA while differentiating between cemented and press-fit fixation techniques and comparing materials like metal, ceramic, and polyethylene used in articulation.

content.title: THA Prosthesis Design History
content.definition: The history of hip arthroplasty evolved from Dr. Gluck's use of ivory in 1891 to Austin Moore's metallic hemiarthroplasty in the 1940s, culminating in Sir John Charnley's low friction arthroplasty concept in the 1960s using a small metal head, polyethylene cup, and acrylic bone cement.
content.summary: The history of hip replacement began in 1891 with Dr. Gluck’s use of ivory, followed by Austin Moore's introduction of the metallic hemiarthroplasty in the 1940s. The field was revolutionized in the 1960s when Sir John Charnley pioneered the concept of low friction arthroplasty, utilizing a small metallic head, a polyethylene cup, and acrylic bone cement.

content.title: THA Prosthesis Design Press-fit Femoral Stems
content.definition: Press-fit femoral stems rely on biologic fixation with compression hoop stresses for initial stability, come in tapered, extensively coated, and modular designs, and are uniquely complicated by intraoperative fracture (often from under-reaming), high loosening rates in irradiated bone, and junctional corrosion in modular components.
content.summary: Press-fit femoral stems are uncemented components used in total hip arthroplasty that rely on biologic fixation for long-term integration. Initial stability is achieved through compression hoop stresses applied to the surrounding bone during implantation.

content.title: THA Prosthesis Design Cemented Femoral Stems
content.definition: Cemented femoral stems rely on cement (a grout) for initial and long-term stability with limited remodeling, are preferred for irradiated bone or poor proximal bone stock (Dorr C femurs), and are composed of cobalt-chrome/stainless steel (most common) or titanium (no longer recommended due to crevice corrosion), with the main unique complication being stem breakage due to cantilever bending.
content.summary: Cemented femoral stems are components used in total hip arthroplasty that rely on acrylic bone cement acting as a grout to provide both initial and long-term mechanical stability. This fixation method is often preferred for patients with poor proximal femoral bone stock or limited potential for biological ingrowth, such as in irradiated bone.

content.title: THA Prosthesis Design Bearing Surfaces
content.definition: Metal-on-polyethylene (MoP) bearings have the longest track record, are the lowest cost, and offer the most modularity, but suffer from higher wear and osteolysis rates compared to metal-on-metal and ceramic bearings, and the smaller head size increases the risk of impingement. Metal-on-metal (MoM) bearings offer better wear properties (lower linear wear) and larger femoral heads (increased ROM), but their disadvantages include higher cost, elevated serum metal ion levels (which peak during the "wear in" phase), the risk of forming pseudotumors, and potential for hypersensitivity (T-cell mediated, Type IV), making them contraindicated in pregnant women and those with renal disease or known metal hypersensitivity. Ceramic-on-ceramic (CoC) bearings provide the best wear properties (lowest coefficient of friction) and use inert particles (no cancer concern), but are disadvantaged by being more expensive, having the worst mechanical properties (brittle, low fracture toughness), and the risk of squeaking (due to edge loading, impingement, or loss of fluid film lubrication) and stripe wear. Ceramic-on-polyethylene (CoP) bearings are considered the standard of care because alumina ceramic heads result in less polyethylene wear than MoP bearings, though zirconia heads can undergo undesirable tetragonal to monoclinic phase transformation over time (increased after 8 years in vivo), while titanium-on-polyethylene is not recommended due to high wear rates.
content.summary: Bearing surfaces refer to the specific articulating materials utilized in total hip arthroplasty, such as metal-on-polyethylene, metal-on-metal, and ceramic combinations. These components determine the characteristics of lubrication, friction, and long-term wear properties of the implanted joint.

content.title: THA Templating Introduction
content.definition: Templating is the preoperative process of anticipating implant size and position to reproduce hip biomechanics, minimize limb length inequality, and anticipate difficulties, with an accuracy ranging from 52% to 98% (plus or minus one size) depending on surgeon experience.
content.summary: Templating is the essential preoperative planning process that utilizes standardized radiographs and specialized templates to accurately anticipate the optimal size and position of prosthetic components. This process guides the surgeon in restoring proper hip biomechanics, correcting offset, and minimizing limb length inequality during total hip arthroplasty.

content.title: THA Templating Radiographic Analysis
content.definition: Radiographic analysis requires an AP pelvis centered over the pubic symphysis and an AP hip taken in 10 to 15 degrees internal rotation (to keep the femoral neck parallel to the cassette), often supplemented by a frog lateral hip view, with 20% magnification considered standard for templating.
content.summary: Radiographic analysis is the essential preoperative process of obtaining and interpreting specific radiographs, such as the AP pelvis and internally rotated AP hip, to accurately establish anatomical landmarks, determine magnification, and identify factors like limb length discrepancy and pelvic alignment. This assessment provides the foundational measurements needed for precise implant selection and positioning during surgical planning.

content.title: THA Templating Radiographic Landmarks
content.definition: Key radiographic landmarks for templating include the medullary canal, greater trochanter, and lesser trochanter on the femoral side, and the acetabular roof and teardrop on the acetabular side, along with the ischial tuberosities to establish limb length.
content.summary: Radiographic landmarks are distinct anatomical structures identified on preoperative pelvic and hip radiographs (e.g., lesser trochanter, teardrop, ischial tuberosities) used during templating for total hip arthroplasty. These landmarks are essential for accurately determining limb length discrepancy and optimizing the size and placement of the prosthetic components.

content.title: THA Templating Establish Limb Length Discrepancy
content.definition: To establish limb length discrepancy, one should draw a horizontal reference line connecting the ischial tuberosities (or teardrops) on the AP pelvis and measure the vertical distance from this line to the most proximal point of the lesser trochanters bilaterally.
content.summary: Establishing limb length discrepancy is a vital step in preoperative templating that uses an AP pelvis radiograph to measure the existing difference in limb length between the hips. This is accomplished by measuring the vertical distance between a fixed pelvic reference line, such as one connecting the ischial tuberosities or teardrops, and the proximal-most point of the lesser trochanters on both sides.

content.title: THA Templating Template the Acetabulum
content.definition: To template the acetabulum, place the appropriately sized cup template at roughly 40 degrees of abduction so its medial border approximates the ilioischial line and is close to the teardrop (a medial cup decreases joint reactive forces), ensuring the inferior border is at the level of the inferior teardrop line to mark the new center of rotation.
content.summary: Templating the acetabulum involves placing the appropriate component template, usually at about 40 degrees of abduction, to anticipate the implant size and position. This step ensures the medial cup border approximates the ilioischial line and teardrop, defining the component's center of rotation to optimize joint mechanics.

content.title: THA Templating Template the Femur
content.definition: To template the femur, one should choose an implant that fills the medullary canal, determine the insertion depth by placing the new femoral head center of rotation level with the acetabular center of rotation (superior placement lengthens the limb, inferior placement shortens it), mark the femoral neck resection level (using the lesser trochanter posteriorly or the saddle point anteriorly), and restore offset by selecting the correct stem, neck-shaft angle, or neck length.
content.summary: Templating the femur is the radiographic process of selecting the appropriately sized femoral implant to fill the medullary canal, while simultaneously determining the optimal insertion depth and offset to manage limb length inequality and restore native biomechanics.

content.title: THA Implant Fixation Introduction
content.definition: The introduction to implant fixation highlights two main methods, cement fixation (using PMMA) and biologic (cementless) fixation (relying on bone in/on-growth), which are fundamental to total hip arthroplasty (THA). The history shows cemented fixation, popularized by Charnley in the 1950s, faced failures ("cement disease") that drove the shift toward cementless techniques, with the AML implant (1983) being the first FDA-approved microporous surface for bone ingrowth, leading to the current trend where 93% of THA in the US were cementless by 2012.
content.summary: This introductory section describes the two primary methods of implant fixation used in total hip arthroplasty (THA): cemented fixation, which uses polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) as grout, and biologic (cementless) fixation, which relies on bone ingrowth or ongrowth. It provides an overview of the history, mechanisms, indications, techniques, and associated complications for both fixation types.

content.title: THA Implant Fixation Indications
content.definition: The Dorr classification provides guidance for fixation choice by using a ratio of canal diameters to categorize femoral bone quality (Type A/B for uncemented, Type C for cemented, often called "stovepipe femur").
content.summary: Indications refer to the specific patient factors and anatomical characteristics, often guided by systems like the Dorr classification, that determine whether cemented or biologic (cementless) fixation is chosen for the components of a total hip arthroplasty. These choices are typically based on age, bone stock quality, and conditions like irradiated bone or a stovepipe femur.

content.title: THA Implant Fixation Cement Fixation
content.definition: Cement fixation acts as a grout to produce an interlocking fit and is primarily indicated for elderly patients, those with irradiated bone (where ingrowth is limited), and patients with a Dorr C "stovepipe" femur, although its use in the acetabulum is controversial due to higher failure rates. Cementing technique has evolved through three generations, from hand-mixing to modern methods utilizing vacuum-mixing, cement pressurization, and pulsatile lavage to optimize fixation by minimizing cement porosity, ensuring a cement mantle greater than 2 mm, using a stiff, smooth stem, and avoiding mantle defects and malpositioning. Radiographic analysis of cemented stems uses the Barrack and Harris grading system (Grade A to D) to assess the completeness of cement filling and the presence of radiolucencies at the cement-bone interface, with Grade A representing a complete fill ("white-out") and Grade D indicating gross radiolucencies and failure.
content.summary: Cement fixation is a method of securing prosthetic implants, typically using polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) cement, which acts as a grout to create a mechanical, interlocking fit between the component and the surrounding bone. This technique provides immediate stability and is often favored for patients with osteopenic bone or a "stovepipe" (Dorr C) femur.

content.title: THA Implant Fixation Biologic Fixation
content.definition: Biologic fixation relies on ingrowth (bone into the porous structure) or ongrowth (bone onto the grit-blasted surface) and is indicated for younger patients and older patients with good bone stock, as well as for acetabular fixation in most situations. Biologic fixation technique is achieved through a press-fit or line-to-line method, and is optimized by maintaining a porous size of 50-300 µm, a porosity of 40-50%, and a micromotion less than 150 µm (to prevent fibrous ingrowth), with fixation achieved either through porous coating (proximal or extensively coated) or grit-blasted metallic surfaces, sometimes augmented with hydroxyapatite (HA). Radiographic analysis of cementless stems assesses for signs of stable fixation, including the presence of spot-welds, absence of radiolucent lines around the porous area, and lack of subsidence or migration on serial radiographs.
content.summary: Biologic fixation, also known as cementless fixation, is a technique used in arthroplasty where the bone achieves mechanical stability by growing directly onto the surface (ongrowth) or into the porous structure (ingrowth) of the implant. This process requires maintaining specific parameters, such as appropriate pore size and minimal micromotion, to ensure lasting fixation.

content.title: THA Implant Fixation Complications of Implant Fixation
content.definition: Complications of implant fixation include aseptic loosening (due to poor fixation, mechanical loss, or particle-induced osteolysis, presenting as groin/buttock or thigh pain), stress shielding (proximal bone loss due to stiff or extensively coated stems), and intraoperative fractures (more common with press-fit stems and often treated by adding screws/cables or revising to a longer stem).
content.summary: Complications associated with joint replacement implant fixation include aseptic loosening, defined as the loss of fixation over time due to mechanical factors or particle-induced osteolysis, and stress shielding, which is proximal femoral bone loss caused by a stiff, well-fixed stem. Other immediate complications involve intraoperative fractures, particularly when using press-fit techniques.

content.title: THA Approaches Direct Anterior Approach
content.definition: The Direct Anterior Approach is an increasingly popular approach that uses the interval between the tensor fascia lata and sartorius, offers a decreased dislocation rate and avoids abductor violation (compared to anterolateral/direct lateral), but has a steep learning curve, challenging femoral exposure, increased infection risk in obese patients, and risks lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) paresthesias.
content.summary: The Direct Anterior Approach is an increasingly popular surgical technique for hip arthroplasty that accesses the joint through the internervous plane between the tensor fascia lata and the sartorius muscle. This approach avoids violation of the abductor mechanism and is associated with a decreased postoperative dislocation rate compared to the posterior approach.

content.title: THA Approaches Anterolateral Approach
content.definition: The Anterolateral Approach uses the interval between the tensor fascia lata and gluteus medius, offers a lower dislocation rate than the posterior approach, but is less commonly used for arthroplasty because it violates the abductor mechanism, potentially leading to a postoperative limp.
content.summary: The anterolateral approach to hip arthroplasty utilizes the interval between the tensor fascia lata and the gluteus medius. While offering a lower dislocation rate than the posterior approach, it is less commonly used due to violation of the abductor mechanism, which can result in a postoperative limp.

content.title: THA Approaches Direct Lateral Approach
content.definition: The Direct Lateral Approach has no true interval, instead splitting the gluteus medius and vastus lateralis, and while it offers a lower dislocation rate than the posterior approach and good exposure, it violates the abductor mechanism (leading to a limp) and is commonly associated with heterotopic ossification.
content.summary: The Direct Lateral Approach is a less commonly used surgical exposure for hip arthroplasty characterized by splitting the gluteus medius and vastus lateralis muscles, as it follows no true intermuscular interval. This approach violates the abductor mechanism, which often leads to disadvantages like postoperative limp and common heterotopic ossification.

content.title: THA Approaches Posterolateral Approach
content.definition: The Posterolateral Approach is the most common approach for arthroplasty due to its excellent exposure of both the femur and acetabulum and preservation of the abductor mechanism, though it carries a higher dislocation risk than anterior exposures, which is reduced by repair of the capsule and short external rotators.
content.summary: The posterolateral approach is the most frequently used surgical method for both primary and revision total hip arthroplasty, providing excellent exposure of the femur and acetabulum without violating the abductor mechanism. While offering excellent accessibility, it is associated with a potentially higher rate of postoperative dislocation compared to anterior approaches.

content.title: THA Approaches Extensile Approaches
content.definition: Extensile Approaches, primarily the Trochanteric Osteotomy (standard, slide, or extended), are complex techniques reserved for difficult primary and revision cases that provide excellent acetabular exposure and facilitate component removal, but are associated with complications including non-union, abductor weakness, heterotopic ossification, and fracture of the osteotomy fragment (most common).
content.summary: Extensile approaches are specialized surgical techniques, typically involving a trochanteric osteotomy, employed during difficult primary or revision hip arthroplasty. These approaches provide maximal exposure necessary for complex tasks such as component removal and excellent acetabular visualization.

content.title: THA Stability Techniques Summary
content.definition: The stability of total hip arthroplasty (THA) is determined by four major variables: component design, component position, soft-tissue tensioning, and soft tissue function.
content.summary: This summary outlines the four key variables				component design, component position, soft-tissue tensioning, and soft tissue function				that critically influence the overall stability of a Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA) implant.

content.title: THA Stability Techniques Component Design
content.definition: Component design features that increase stability include large femoral heads (which increase the head-neck ratio and jump distance prior to dislocation), elevated rim liners (which increase stability when placed posteriorly), and lateralized liners (which increase soft tissue tension but may increase loosening risk).
content.summary: Component design in Total Hip Arthroplasty refers to the specific physical characteristics and geometries of the femoral and acetabular prostheses that optimize stability. Key design features, such as large femoral heads (to increase jump distance and head-neck ratio) and specialized acetabular liners (to increase offset), are employed to maximize the range of motion and enhance soft-tissue tensioning, thereby minimizing dislocation risk.

content.title: THA Stability Techniques Component Position
content.definition: Component position recommendations for the acetabulum are 5 to 25 degrees of anteversion and 30 to 50 degrees of abduction, though the approach matters (posterior should err toward more anteversion, anterior toward less), and the femoral stem should have 10 to 15 degrees of anteversion, with a combined version of the cup and stem recommended to be around 37 degrees.
content.summary: Component position in total hip arthroplasty refers to the specific surgical orientation of the prosthetic components, including the acetabular cup (anteversion and abduction) and the femoral stem (anteversion). Achieving optimal component position is critical for maximizing joint stability, preventing impingement, and minimizing the risk of postoperative dislocation.

content.title: THA Stability Techniques Soft Tissue Tensioning
content.definition: Soft tissue tensioning is primarily optimized by ensuring abductor integrity (especially the gluteus medius) and restoring femoral offset, defined as the perpendicular distance from the femoral head center to the femoral axis, because increased offset decreases impingement, reduces joint reaction force, and increases stability without increasing leg length.
content.summary: Soft tissue tensioning is a critical variable for Total Hip Arthroplasty stability, achieved by ensuring abductor integrity and optimizing the length and tone of the periarticular musculature. This is primarily accomplished by restoring or increasing the femoral offset, which enhances stability and decreases joint reaction forces without increasing leg length.

content.title: THA Stability Techniques Soft Tissue Function
content.definition: Soft tissue function is controlled by the central nervous system (e.g., stroke, Parkinson's), the peripheral nervous system (e.g., spinal stenosis, radiculopathy), and local soft tissue integrity (e.g., trauma, deconditioning, infection), with insufficiency of the abductor mechanism often leading to a Trendelenburg gait.
content.summary: Soft tissue function is a primary determinant of total hip arthroplasty stability, requiring the coordinated integrity of the central and peripheral nervous systems to control musculature, alongside adequate local soft tissue health.

content.title: THA Acetabular Screw Fixation Acetabular Screw Placement and Safe Zones
content.definition: The acetabulum is divided into four quadrants by a line from the ASIS to the center and a perpendicular line, identifying the posterior-superior as the safe "target zone" (though the sciatic nerve is at risk with hip center elevation) and the anterior quadrants (anterior-inferior and anterior-superior) as the "danger/death zones" due to nearby obturator and external iliac vessels.
content.summary: Acetabular safe zones utilize a quadrant system (target, caution, danger, death) defined by specific anatomical lines to categorize the acetabulum, guiding supplemental screw placement to minimize the risk of injury to vital neurovascular structures, including the sciatic nerve, obturator vessels, and external iliac vessels.

content.title: THA Acetabular Screw Fixation Avoiding Prosthesis Impingement
content.definition: Prosthesis impingement can occur when the anterior acetabular rim is left proud (potentially causing iliopsoas tendon impingement) or from component mismatch, where medializing the cup increases bone-on-bone impingement by decreasing offset, and factors like a small femoral head or skirts (a low head-to-neck ratio) increase the overall risk of metal-on-metal impingement by limiting the range of motion.
content.summary: Avoiding prosthesis impingement requires meticulous component placement and selection to prevent unintended mechanical contact between prosthetic parts, bone, or adjacent soft tissues like the iliopsoas tendon. This is achieved by optimizing cup position (avoiding excessive medialization or lateralization) and ensuring favorable femoral head-to-neck ratios.

content.title: THA Postoperative Inpatient Management Introduction
content.definition: Postoperative inpatient management is a coordinated effort involving the orthopedic team, hospitalist, therapists, case manager, nursing staff, and the patient/family, and is divided into preoperative teaching, inpatient acute care (hospital), inpatient extended care (rehab/SNF), and outpatient home care.
content.summary: Postoperative inpatient management is a coordinated effort involving a multidisciplinary team (e.g., physicians, nurses, therapists, case managers) spanning phases from preoperative teaching through acute inpatient hospitalization to extended care. This overview specifically details the key components of acute hospital care, including multimodal pain control, medical management of complications, and initiation of physical and occupational therapy.

content.title: THA Postoperative Inpatient Management Inpatient Acute Care (Hospital)
content.definition:
Inpatient acute care focuses on immediate recovery, utilizing multimodal pain management, closely managing the surgical stress response, and initiating physical and occupational therapy starting on the day of surgery to achieve discharge criteria.

Pain management utilizes a multimodal approach starting preoperatively with medications like NSAIDs and acetaminophen, moving to regional anesthesia and periarticular injections intraoperatively, and relying on multimodal oral drug therapy postoperatively as the gold standard.

Medical management addresses the surgical stress response which can exacerbate underlying conditions, requiring evaluation and treatment for issues like hypertension (often due to pain or anxiety), hypotension (most commonly from volume depletion), hypoxia (managed with incentive spirometry and oxygen), decreased urine output (due to hypovolemia or urinary retention), altered mental status, chest pain (evaluating for cardiac origin), and postoperative fever (cytokine-mediated on days one to two, infectious after day three).

Physical therapy should start on the day of surgery to decrease length of stay and improve function, focusing on bed supported Range of Motion exercises (e.g., ankle pumps, gluteal sets), gait training (using a walker), and learning proper stair technique (up with the nonoperative leg, down with the operative leg).

Occupational therapy focuses on regaining independence in Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) and safe transfers using assistive devices like raised toilet seats and shower seats, progressing the patient toward gait normalization and functional independence.

Discharge home criteria require the patient to achieve independent ambulation with an assistive device (50 to 100 feet), independent transfers, independent ADLs, the ability to climb two stairs with supervision, adherence to dislocation precautions, and confirmation of appropriate home assistance.
content.summary: Inpatient acute care in the hospital refers to the immediate postoperative management phase requiring coordinated efforts from a multidisciplinary team. Key goals include aggressive multimodal pain control, management of medical complications arising from surgical stress, initiation of physical therapy, and achieving criteria necessary for safe discharge.

content.title: THA Postoperative Inpatient Management Inpatient Extended Care (Rehab)
content.definition: Inpatient extended care involves discharge to an inpatient rehab facility or a skilled nursing facility (SNF)/subacute rehab (SAR), with SNF/SAR being the complement to acute care for patients who do not meet home discharge criteria and do not qualify for inpatient rehab (which is intensive, requiring three hours of therapy/day, and rarely utilized after total joint arthroplasty).
content.summary: Inpatient Extended Care is a phase of postoperative management following acute hospitalization, encompassing care provided in either a Skilled Nursing Facility (SNF)/subacute rehab (SAR) or an intensive inpatient rehabilitation facility. This setting provides continued therapy and medical oversight for patients who require additional support before achieving safe discharge home.

content.title: THA Rehabilitation Introduction
content.definition: Rehabilitation for joint replacement is a coordinated process involving the orthopaedic surgeon, therapists (physical and occupational), case manager, nursing staff, and the patient/family, broken down into phases: preoperative teaching, inpatient acute care, inpatient extended care, and outpatient home care.
content.summary: Rehabilitation involves a coordinated effort among a multidisciplinary team, including the orthopaedic surgeon, therapists, and nursing staff, as well as the patient and family. This comprehensive care process is structured into sequential phases, beginning with preoperative teaching and progressing through inpatient acute care, extended care, and subsequent outpatient services.

content.title: THA Rehabilitation Preoperative Teaching
content.definition: While preoperative physical therapy has not shown improved outcomes, hip precautions should be discussed before surgery, with the specific restrictions (avoiding flexion past 90 degrees, internal rotation, and adduction for posterolateral; avoiding extension, external rotation, and adduction for anterolateral or direct anterior) depending on the surgical approach.
content.summary: Preoperative teaching is a phase of coordinated rehabilitation care occurring before surgery. It is essential for educating the patient on specific postoperative hip precautions, such as restrictions on flexion, rotation, and adduction, which are determined by the surgical approach used.

content.title: THA Rehabilitation Inpatient Acute Care (Hospital)
content.definition: Inpatient acute care focuses on early mobilization and pain management through a multimodal approach that uses preoperative NSAIDs/opioids, intraoperative regional anesthesia/periarticular injections, and postoperative oral drug therapy (the gold standard). Pain management uses a multimodal approach utilizing preoperative medications (NSAIDs/opioids), intraoperative methods (regional anesthesia/periarticular injections), and postoperative oral drug therapy as the gold standard to reduce pain. Physical therapy goals follow a progression from sitting upright to gait training and ambulation with a walker, eventually leading to safe transfers, gait normalization, and functional independence. Discharge home criteria require the patient to achieve independent ambulation with an assistive device, independent transfers and ADLs, the ability to manage stairs with supervision, and confirmation of appropriate home assistance.
content.summary: Inpatient acute care refers to the initial phase of hospital stay following surgery, where the primary goals are multimodal pain management and intensive physical therapy. During this phase, the patient works toward achieving functional milestones, such as independent ambulation and transfers, required for discharge home or to an extended care facility.

content.title: THA Rehabilitation Inpatient Extended Care (Rehab)
content.definition: Inpatient Extended Care is a continuation of care where earlier discharge to rehab from the hospital is associated with improved outcomes, with the discharge criteria to home being similar to those from the hospital.
content.summary: Inpatient extended care is a phase of post-acute recovery, often delivered in a rehabilitation or skilled nursing facility (SNF) setting, that follows the initial acute hospital stay. This setting provides coordinated therapeutic intervention, and earlier discharge from the hospital to this level of care is associated with improved patient outcomes.

content.title: THA Rehabilitation Outpatient Care
content.definition: Outpatient Care involves guidelines for returning to activities, with low-impact exercises like golf preferred (golf handicap shows minimal change after THA but increases after TKA), driving typically safe after 3-4 weeks (reaction time returns to baseline at 4-6 weeks), and return to work recommended within a month for non-manual labor jobs.
content.summary: Outpatient care is the final phase of rehabilitation following discharge from acute or extended inpatient facilities, focusing on the patient's maximal functional recovery and safe integration back into normal life. This phase addresses key milestones such as returning to driving, work, and appropriate sports activities.

content.title: THA Periprosthetic Fracture Summary
content.definition: THA Periprosthetic Fractures are a growing complication of total hip arthroplasty (THA) that require diagnosis via plain radiographs of the affected hip and ipsilateral femur, with treatment (nonoperative or operative) dictated by the fracture location, implant stability, and available bone stock.
content.summary: Periprosthetic fractures are an increasingly common complication of total hip arthroplasty (THA), typically diagnosed using plain radiographs of the affected hip and femur. Treatment involves individualized nonoperative or operative approaches determined by the fracture location, the stability of the implant, and the quality of the patient's existing bone stock.

content.title: THA Periprosthetic Fracture Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of intraoperative fractures is higher in primary uncemented hip replacements (3.5%) compared to cemented arthroplasties (0.4%), while postoperative fractures occur in 0.1% of cases, most commonly at the stem tip.
content.summary: Epidemiology outlines the incidence of THA periprosthetic fractures, reporting higher rates for intraoperative fractures (up to 3.5% in uncemented arthroplasties) compared to postoperative fractures (around 0.1%), which commonly occur at the stem tip.

content.title: THA Periprosthetic Fracture Etiology
content.definition: Etiology involves a wide array of intraoperative and postoperative factors, ranging from bone preparation issues and implant sizing mismatch to low-energy trauma in the postoperative setting.
content.summary:
Etiology is the study of the cause or set of causes for a disease or condition. Clinically, it identifies the underlying factors 


such as poor bone quality, technical error, or component instability 


that lead to a specific complication like a periprosthetic fracture.

content.title: THA Periprosthetic Fracture Intraoperative Acetabular Fractures
content.definition: The treatment for intraoperative acetabular fractures is determined by implant stability: observation and protected weight-bearing for stable implants, or acetabular revision with supplemental screws (or a jumbo cup) for unstable implants, with ORIF and revision required if the posterior column is compromised.
content.summary: Intraoperative acetabular fractures are complications that occur during total hip arthroplasty, typically when excessive force is applied during the impaction of the acetabular component. Treatment strategies depend primarily on the intraoperative assessment of component stability.

search_keywords: Types A B C THA fracture

content.title: THA Periprosthetic Fracture Intraoperative Femur Fractures
content.definition: Intraoperative femur fractures are classified using the Vancouver classification (Types A, B, C, with subtypes 1 (perforation), 2 (crack), 3 (unstable displaced)), and suspicion for fracture should be raised by a change in resistance during stem insertion.
content.summary: Intraoperative femur fractures are complications occurring during total hip arthroplasty, typically caused by technical errors during bone preparation, rasping, or prosthetic insertion, with incidences ranging from 0.1% in primary THA to over 20% in revision THA. These fractures are classified using the Vancouver system and require immediate intraoperative management, often involving cabling, stem revision, or plate fixation based on stability and location.

content.title: THA Periprosthetic Fracture Postoperative Femur fracture
content.definition: Postoperative femur fractures are classified using the Vancouver classification (Types AG (greater trochanter), AL (lesser trochanter), B1 (well-fixed stem), B2 (loose stem/good bone), B3 (loose stem/poor bone), and C (far distal)), which guides treatment based on stem stability, fracture location, and bone quality.
content.summary: A postoperative femur fracture is a periprosthetic complication occurring after total hip arthroplasty, often triggered by low-energy trauma. These fractures are classified using the Vancouver system, which assesses the location relative to the femoral stem, the stability of the implant, and the quality of the surrounding bone to determine the appropriate operative or nonoperative treatment.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Summary
content.definition: Periprosthetic fractures involving hip hemiarthroplasty are rare, low-energy fractures occurring near femoral stems in geriatric, osteoporotic patients, which cause pain and immobility and are treated with open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) or revision hip arthroplasty, depending on fracture type and implant stability.
content.summary: Periprosthetic fractures involving hip hemiarthroplasty are rare complications that occur near the femoral stem, usually affecting elderly, osteoporotic patients following low-energy trauma. Management is often dictated by the Vancouver classification and typically involves operative treatment via open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) or revision arthroplasty.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of these fractures is low, ranging from approximately 1% to 4% of all hemiarthroplasties, but is significantly higher in uncemented stems (1% to 7.4%) compared to cemented stems (0.2% to 1.3%), and the primary risk factors are increasing age, female gender, and osteoporosis.
content.summary: Epidemiology details the incidence and distribution of periprosthetic fractures following hip hemiarthroplasty, which occur in approximately 1% to 4% of all procedures. Key risk factors for these fractures include increasing age, female gender, underlying osteoporosis, and the use of uncemented femoral stems.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Etiology
content.definition: The etiology is typically a ground level fall in patients with predisposing conditions like osteoporosis or implant-related issues such as stress-shielding, osteolysis, or subsidence.
content.summary: The etiology of periprosthetic hip hemiarthroplasty fractures is typically a low-energy mechanism, such as a ground-level fall, often occurring in patients with associated predisposing factors like osteoporosis, stress-shielding, or osteolysis around the implant.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Classification
content.definition: The Vancouver Classification is used to categorize the fractures by location (AG/AL for trochanteric, B for around stem, C for distal) and implant stability (B1 well-fixed, B2 loose, B3 loose with poor bone), which dictates the appropriate treatment strategy.
content.summary: The standard method for categorizing periprosthetic fractures involving hip hemiarthroplasty is the Vancouver Classification system. This system organizes fractures based on their anatomical location relative to the femoral stem and implant stability (Types AG, AL, B1, B2, B3, C), which subsequently determines the recommended treatment approach.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Presentation
content.definition: The presentation typically follows a ground level fall and includes pain and inability to bear weight, with physical examination showing a shortened, externally rotated limb and pain with the log roll, while startup pain or constitutional symptoms may suggest antecedent loosening or infection.
content.summary: Periprosthetic fractures typically present acutely following a low-energy trauma, such as a ground-level fall, resulting in severe pain and the inability to bear weight on the affected limb. Physical examination often reveals a shortened, externally rotated limb with pain upon log roll, and historical clues may suggest antecedent implant loosening or infection.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Imaging
content.definition: Imaging requires AP and lateral femoral radiographs to fully evaluate the fracture pattern, displacement, and implant position, with an AP pelvis needed for surgical planning and a CT scan sometimes used to better assess implant stability and detailed fracture morphology, particularly the cement mantle.
content.summary: Imaging, primarily involving AP and lateral femoral radiographs and computed tomography (CT), is essential for diagnosing periprosthetic fractures. Radiographs evaluate the fracture pattern, displacement, and implant position, while CT scans provide detailed morphology and assess implant stability and cement mantle integrity.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is nonoperative for nondisplaced trochanteric fractures (Vancouver AG less than $2\text{ cm}$ and AL fractures) using protected weightbearing, and operative for displaced and unstable fractures, involving open reduction and internal fixation (for B1 and C fractures), revision arthroplasty with a long stem (for B2 fractures), or proximal femoral replacement (for B3 fractures with poor bone stock).
content.summary: Treatment protocols for periprosthetic fractures following hip hemiarthroplasty are primarily guided by the Vancouver Classification, which assesses fracture location and implant stability. Options range from nonoperative management for minimally displaced, stable fractures to surgical interventions, including open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) for stable stems, or revision arthroplasty for loose components.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Complications
content.definition: Complications include a high rate of infection, dislocation, refracture, stem subsidence, implant failure, and trochanteric escape, with surgical revision carrying up to a 25% complication rate and increased blood loss compared to isolated ORIF.
content.summary: Complications are potential adverse outcomes associated with the injury or subsequent surgical treatment of periprosthetic fractures around a hip hemiarthroplasty. These issues commonly include surgical site infection, implant failure, stem subsidence, dislocation, refracture, and trochanteric escape.

content.title: Hip Hemiarthroplasty Periprosthetic Fracture Prognosis
content.definition: The prognosis is grim, with high mortality (approximately 25% at 1 year) and high morbidity associated with these fractures in comorbid elderly patients, making early mobilization critical and surgical delay associated with increased mortality.
content.summary: The prognosis for patients experiencing periprosthetic hip fractures after hemiarthroplasty is poor, characterized by high morbidity and significant mortality, approximately 25% at one year and up to 50% at two years. Key prognostic factors influencing outcome include early mobilization to prevent complications and avoiding surgical delay, which is associated with increased mortality.

content.title: THA Dislocation Summary
content.definition: THA dislocation is a common complication caused by patient noncompliance, implant malposition, or soft-tissue deficiency, diagnosed by plain radiographs and primarily treated with closed reduction, though revision surgery is required for irreducible or recurrent instability.
content.summary: THA Dislocation is a common complication following total hip arthroplasty, frequently caused by implant malposition, soft tissue deficiency, or patient noncompliance with postoperative restrictions. While initial treatment is closed reduction, surgical management, including possible revision THA, is necessary for irreducible dislocations, recurrent instability, or component malposition.

content.title: THA Dislocation Epidemiology
content.definition: THA dislocation has an incidence of 1%-3%, with the majority (70%) occurring within the first month, and most (75%-90%) being posterior dislocations.
content.summary: The epidemiology of total hip arthroplasty (THA) dislocation demonstrates an incidence rate of 1-3%. These dislocations are overwhelmingly posterior in nature (75-90%) and occur predominantly early, with 70% happening within the first month after the procedure.

content.title: THA Dislocation Etiology
content.definition: Dislocation occurs through mechanisms like posterior (flexion, internal rotation, adduction) or anterior (extension, external rotation) and is strongly influenced by risk factors such as prior hip surgery, posterior approach (if short external rotators are not repaired), component malposition (e.g., excessive retroversion causing posterior dislocation), and polyethylene wear (a common cause of late instability).
content.summary: The etiology of total hip arthroplasty (THA) dislocation involves specific mechanical movements that provoke displacement (e.g., flexion, adduction, and internal rotation for posterior dislocation) combined with numerous predisposing risk factors. These risk factors include prior hip surgery, component malpositioning (such as excessive anteversion or retroversion), patient comorbidities, and late causes like polyethylene wear.

content.title: THA Dislocation Presentation
content.definition: Patients typically present with a history detailing an activity that places the hip in a provocative position (such as hip flexion, adduction, and internal rotation for posterior dislocation), often associated with simple actions like tying a shoe or sitting on a low seat.
content.summary: THA dislocation typically presents following a history of provocative positioning, such as hip flexion, adduction, and internal rotation, often occurring during activities like shoe tying or sitting in a low chair. Diagnosis is confirmed using AP and cross-table lateral radiographs, which visualize the dislocation and assist in evaluating the alignment of the prosthetic components.

content.title: THA Dislocation Imaging
content.definition: Diagnosis is confirmed using AP and cross-table lateral radiographs to visualize the dislocation, while an eccentric femoral head position suggests polyethylene wear, and a CT scan is utilized to precisely assess for implant malpositioning (e.g., retroversion or vertical cup position).
content.summary: Imaging, primarily using AP and cross-table lateral plain radiographs, is essential for confirming hip dislocation following total hip arthroplasty (THA) and identifying key component malalignment parameters like inclination and anteversion. CT scans are utilized as an adjunct study to precisely evaluate subtle implant malpositioning contributing to joint instability.

content.title: THA Dislocation Treatment
content.definition: Treatment for early dislocations involves closed reduction and immobilization (successful in two-thirds of cases), while operative management is required for recurrent instability, implant malalignment, or extensive polyethylene wear, necessitating a polyethylene exchange or revision THA.
content.summary: Management of THA dislocation primarily begins with nonoperative closed reduction and temporary immobilization, which is successful for most early dislocations. If the dislocation is irreducible or recurrent, or if underlying factors like component malalignment or polyethylene wear are present, surgical treatment, typically revision total hip arthroplasty, is necessary.

content.title: THA Dislocation Technique
content.definition: Revision THA techniques focus on preventing future instability by realigning components (correcting version/inclination), using larger heads to increase the head-neck ratio and offset, or converting to specialized implants like a constrained liner (for soft-tissue deficiency) or a dual mobility (DM) implant (which provides a larger range of motion and effective head size).
content.summary: Techniques, in the context of recurrent total hip arthroplasty (THA) dislocation, are the operative methods used during revision THA or conversion procedures aimed at restoring stability and preventing future episodes. These interventions include component realignment, head enlargement, increasing femoral offset, and utilizing constrained or dual mobility implants to address underlying factors like malalignment or soft tissue deficiency.

content.title: THA Sciatic Nerve Palsy Summary
content.definition: Sciatic nerve palsy is an uncommon but potentially devastating complication after total hip arthroplasty (THA) that can lead to persistent foot drop, requiring clinical diagnosis of postoperative numbness and weakness in the nerve's distribution.
content.summary: Sciatic Nerve Palsy following Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA) is an uncommon but potentially devastating complication characterized by post-operative numbness, paresthesias, and weakness, frequently resulting in persistent foot drop due to injury to the peroneal division. Treatment involves immediate adjustment of hip position to decrease nerve tension, followed by observation and AFO use for persistent symptoms.

content.title: THA Sciatic Nerve Palsy epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of sciatic nerve palsy is uncommon (0-3%), but it is the most common cause of medical malpractice litigation following THA, with the peroneal division of the sciatic nerve being the most frequently affected (80% of cases) as the nerve travels closest to the acetabulum at the level of the ischium.
content.summary: Epidemiology describes the incidence and anatomical distribution of sciatic nerve palsy following total hip arthroplasty (THA). It is an uncommon complication, occurring in 0-3% of cases, and the peroneal division of the sciatic nerve is affected most frequently.

content.title: THA Sciatic Nerve Palsy Etiology
content.definition: The causes of sciatic nerve palsy include direct trauma, stretch (especially with limb lengthening in developmental dysplasia of the hip), compression (often by hematoma), heat from cement polymerization, and pre-existing lumbar stenosis, though the cause remains unknown in 40% of cases.
content.summary: The etiology of sciatic nerve palsy following THA involves direct causes like mechanical trauma, stretch, compression from hematoma, or heat from cement polymerization, as well as several contributing risk factors. Major risk factors include surgical complexity, such as revision surgery or limb lengthening, and underlying patient conditions like developmental dysplasia of the hip or pre-existing lumbar stenosis.

content.title: THA Sciatic Nerve Palsy Presentation
content.definition: The presentation is characterized by immediate post-operative complaints of numbness, paresthesias, or weakness in the sciatic nerve distribution.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of sciatic nerve palsy following total hip arthroplasty involves immediate post-operative complaints of sensory disturbances, such as numbness and paresthesias, or motor deficits, characterized by weakness along the distribution of the affected nerve.

content.title: THA Sciatic Nerve Palsy Imaging
content.definition: Imaging with a post-operative CT or Ultrasound may be helpful if a hematoma is suspected as the cause of nerve compression.
content.summary: Post-operative imaging, specifically CT and ultrasound, may be utilized to help diagnose sciatic nerve palsy following THA if a compressive hematoma is suspected as the etiology. Electrodiagnostic studies like EMGs are also used later to confirm the level of injury and guide patient prognosis.

content.title: THA Sciatic Nerve Palsy Studies
content.definition: Studies utilizing EMGs (Electromyograms) are used post-operatively to confirm the level of injury and aid in discussions with the patient regarding the prognosis for recovery.
content.summary: Electromyography (EMG) is the primary study utilized post-operatively following sciatic nerve palsy after THA to confirm the precise level of nerve injury. This information is essential for accurate prognosis determination and guiding patient discussions regarding potential recovery.

content.title: THA Sciatic Nerve Palsy Treatment
content.definition: The immediate treatment for post-operative palsy is to place the hip in extension and the knee in flexion to decrease tension on the nerve, followed by immediate evacuation of any suspected hematoma, with a long-term AFO orthosis used as the first line of treatment for persistent foot drop.
content.summary: The immediate post-operative treatment for sciatic nerve palsy involves placing the hip in extension and the knee in flexion to decrease nerve tension, along with potential immediate hematoma evacuation if indicated. For persistent foot drop, management focuses on observation for recovery and the use of an ankle-foot orthosis (AFO).

content.title: THA Sciatic Nerve Palsy Prognosis
content.definition: The prognosis for recovery is guarded, as only 35% to 40% of patients recover full strength after experiencing a complete palsy.
content.summary: For patients experiencing a complete sciatic nerve palsy after total hip arthroplasty, the prognosis for full motor recovery is guarded, as only 35% to 40% typically achieve return of complete strength.

content.title: THA Leg Length Discrepancy Summary
content.definition: THA Leg Length Discrepancy (LLD) is a common post-operative finding, either transient (due to muscle weakness) or permanent (due to limb lengthening), which is diagnosed clinically (ASIS to medial malleolus measurement) or radiographically, and is primarily treated with observation, with shoe lifts indicated if LLD persists beyond six months.
content.summary: Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA) Leg Length Discrepancy (LLD) is a frequent post-operative finding, potentially caused by intentional lengthening or muscle weakness, and is diagnosed by clinical measurement and pelvic radiographs. While often transient, persistent LLD after six months may necessitate nonoperative management with a shoe-lift.

content.title: THA Leg Length Discrepancy Epidemiology
content.definition: LLD is the second most common reason for litigation following total hip arthroplasty, trailing only nerve injury.
content.summary: In the context of total hip arthroplasty (THA), post-operative leg length discrepancy is a significant issue, representing the second most common reason for litigation following the procedure, surpassed only by nerve injury.

content.title: THA Leg Length Discrepancy Etiology
content.definition: LLD is caused by either contractures leading to pelvic obliquity (creating apparent LLD) or true intentional limb lengthening, though abductor weakness can also create a sensation of LLD that often resolves spontaneously within three to six months, highlighting the critical role of preoperative planning in prevention.
content.summary: Etiology in post-THA leg length discrepancy primarily involves muscular contractures (abduction or adduction) that create pelvic obliquity, resulting in an apparent difference in limb length. The condition may also be caused by abductor muscle weakness, which often resolves post-operatively but can create a temporary perception of limb lengthening.

content.title: THA Leg Length Discrepancy Presentation
content.definition: The clinical presentation involves the patient's subjective sensation of LLD (even if anatomically equal), requiring physical examination to determine the true limb length (measured from ASIS to medial malleolus) versus the apparent limb length (affected by soft-tissue contractures and pelvic obliquity).
content.summary: The clinical presentation of leg length discrepancy (LLD) following total hip arthroplasty (THA) includes the patient's subjective feeling of limb inequality, even if anatomically equal, and objective findings. Objective assessment relies on physical examination, specifically measuring the true limb length from the anterior superior iliac spine to the medial malleolus, supported by AP radiographs to confirm the degree of discrepancy.

content.title: THA Leg Length Discrepancy Imaging
content.definition: Radiographic imaging, typically using an AP view, is essential for confirming and quantifying LLD, allowing comparison of bony landmarks and assessment of changes in neck length (not offset) that contribute to the discrepancy.
content.summary: Imaging, typically utilizing AP pelvic radiographs, is essential for objectively confirming and quantifying post-total hip arthroplasty leg length discrepancy and assessing associated structural findings, such as increased neck length.

content.title: THA Leg Length Discrepancy Treatment
content.definition: The primary treatment is nonoperative, involving observation for six months as perceived LLD often resolves with muscle relaxation, after which a shoe lift is the typical intervention, with revision THA being a rare option reserved for significant, persistent LLD that affects quality of life, recognizing the associated increased risk of dislocation, especially when shortening the limb.
content.summary: Treatment for leg length discrepancy following total hip arthroplasty typically begins with observation, as most cases resolve spontaneously with muscle relaxation within six months. If a significant discrepancy persists, management includes shoe-lifts, reserving surgical revision only for rare, severe, chronic cases that significantly affect quality of life.

content.title: THA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Summary
content.definition: THA Vascular Injury and Bleeding is a rare, potentially devastating complication, usually caused by errant retractor or screw placement, which requires prompt diagnosis via visual inspection or hemodynamic status change, followed by immediate vessel recognition, hemostasis, and vascular consultation.
content.summary: Vascular injury and bleeding complicating total hip arthroplasty (THA) is a rare but potentially devastating event, typically resulting from errant screw or retractor placement. Diagnosis relies on recognizing significant arterial bleeding and hemodynamic instability, necessitating prompt surgical hemostasis and immediate vascular consultation.

content.title: THA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Epidemiology
content.definition: With an incidence of 0.1%-0.2%, the main risk factors are acetabular screw placement, prior vascular bypass or revision surgery, and the mechanism is typically puncture or laceration, often from an errant retractor or acetabular screw.
content.summary: Epidemiology defines the incidence and distribution of total hip arthroplasty complications, such as vascular injury and postoperative anemia. It also identifies primary patient and surgical characteristics that serve as key risk factors for these adverse events.

content.title: THA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Anatomy
content.definition: Key vessels at risk include the superior and inferior gluteal vessels (posterior quadrants), the obturator vessels (anterior-inferior), the external iliac and femoral vessels (anterior-superior), the medial femoral circumflex artery (under the quadratus femoris), and the ascending branch of the lateral femoral circumflex artery (during the direct anterior approach).
content.summary: This section details the location and course of major vascular structures, including the external iliac, common femoral, obturator, and circumflex arteries, relative to the acetabulum and surrounding musculature. Knowledge of this anatomy is essential for minimizing vascular injury during total hip arthroplasty, especially regarding retractor and screw placement.

content.title: THA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Presentation
content.definition: The presentation is characterized by variable degrees of hemodynamic instability, pulsatile intra-operative bleeding, possible abdominal distension, and neurovascular signs such as loss of peripheral limb pulses.
content.summary: Presentation encompasses the observable clinical manifestations and diagnostic findings used to identify a complication, ranging from acute intra-operative signs like pulsatile bleeding and hemodynamic instability to later findings such as pallor, dyspnea, tachycardia, and results from imaging or laboratory tests. This collection of symptoms and objective data is crucial for timely diagnosis and initiation of appropriate treatment.

content.title: THA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Imaging
content.definition: Imaging includes radiographs to check for an extra-osseous acetabular screw, and angiography for stable patients, which delineates the specific injured vessels and allows for immediate endovascular embolization.
content.summary: Imaging studies are used in suspected total hip arthroplasty vascular injury, primarily using plain radiographs to identify extra-osseous hardware like misplaced screws. Angiography is critical for hemodynamically stable patients, as it precisely delineates the injured vessels and allows for immediate endovascular embolization.

content.title: THA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Treatment
content.definition: Treatment requires prompt diagnosis and is operative, involving angiography with endovascular embolization for hemodynamically stable patients, or emergent retroperitoneal exploration for hemodynamically unstable patients with pulsatile bleeding.
content.summary: Treatment for complications following total hip arthroplasty (THA) involves prompt intervention for rare but serious vascular injuries, utilizing methods like emergent retroperitoneal exploration or endovascular embolization to achieve hemostasis. Additionally, management includes the prevention of postoperative anemia through measures like tranexamic acid and restrictive blood transfusion based on strict hemoglobin thresholds and patient symptoms.

content.title: THA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Postoperative Anemia
content.definition: Postoperative anemia management aims to prevent the need for transfusion (a complication associated with increased prosthetic joint infection risk) by using tranexamic acid and managing low preoperative hemoglobin (the best predictor), with transfusion indicated only for specific, symptomatic hemoglobin thresholds. Even with treatment, the prognosis for vascular injury is poor, with an associated mortality rate of 9%, an amputation rate of 12%, and a permanent disability rate of 17%.
content.summary: Postoperative anemia is defined as a reduction in hemoglobin concentration following a surgical procedure, frequently manifesting as symptoms like dyspnea, syncope, and tachycardia, and often requiring blood transfusion for treatment.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Summary
content.definition: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency is a serious complication that causes hip instability, gait abnormalities, and pain due to factors like repair failure, muscle atrophy, implant issues (loosening/malposition), nerve injury, or greater trochanteric disruption.
content.summary: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency is a serious complication resulting from primary or revision total hip arthroplasty, characterized by the failure or disruption of the abductor mechanism leading to hip instability, gait abnormalities, and pain.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of this deficiency is highly variable (0.08%-22% in the first 12 months), is more common in females, and increases with age.
content.summary: Epidemiology describes the occurrence and distribution of THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency, noting an incidence rate that ranges from 0.08% to 22% within the first 12 months post-surgery. The condition is demographically more common in females and its incidence increases with patient age.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Etiology
content.definition: The etiology includes failure of the abductor repair (common in anterolateral/direct lateral approaches), iatrogenic superior gluteal nerve (SGN) injury, decreased femoral offset (by >5 mm), and periprosthetic greater trochanter fracture or escape.
content.summary: The etiology of Postoperative Abductor Deficiency following total hip arthroplasty (THA) is typically multifactorial, involving surgical technique issues such as failed abductor repair or iatrogenic injury to the superior gluteal nerve. Biomechanical factors, including decreased femoral offset or shortened prosthetic neck length, along with mechanical failures like greater trochanteric fracture or escape, also contribute significantly to this condition.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Anatomy
content.definition: The abductor muscle group consists of the gluteus medius and minimus (the main abductors), and the tensor fascia lata (TFL), all innervated by the superior gluteal nerve (SGN), which travels approximately 5 cm proximal to the greater trochanter.
content.summary:
The relevant anatomy involves the hip abductor muscle group



gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fascia lata



which originate on the ilium and insert near the greater trochanter to facilitate hip abduction. This complex is entirely innervated by the superior gluteal nerve (L4-S1), a structure running approximately 5 cm proximal to the greater trochanter.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Classification
content.definition: The Basic Classification System for Unstable THA identifies abductor deficiency as Type III, while the Advanced System includes Abductor-Trochanteric Complex defects (Type III) and the Goutallier Classification grades the severity of fatty degeneration in the gluteal muscles.
content.summary: Classification systems for THA postoperative abductor deficiency categorize the various etiologies of instability and assess the integrity of the abductor musculature. These systems, including the Basic and Advanced classifications for Unstable THA and the Goutallier grading for fatty atrophy, help identify component issues, impingement, or abductor status to guide treatment.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Presentation
content.definition: The presentation often involves pain over the greater trochanter, instability, and characteristic gait abnormalities such as a Trendelenburg gait (contralateral hip droops during stance) or an abductor lurch.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of postoperative abductor deficiency following total hip arthroplasty (THA) is characterized by hip pain, instability, and gait abnormalities. Key physical exam findings include a positive Trendelenburg test and the presence of a Trendelenburg lurch.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Imaging
content.definition: Imaging is primarily done with plain radiographs to evaluate component position, offset, and trochanteric fractures, while MRI is the gold standard for visualizing abductor tears, advanced fatty atrophy (Goutallier Grade 3 or 4), and superior gluteal nerve status.
content.summary: Imaging, including standard radiographs and CT, is utilized to evaluate implant positioning, offset, osteolysis, and potential greater trochanteric fractures. MRI is considered the gold standard for visualizing abductor tears and assessing the fatty atrophy and integrity of the gluteus medius and minimus tendons.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Studies
content.definition: Studies include routine labs (WBC, CRP, ESR) and arthrocentesis to rule out infection, and electrophysiologic studies (EMG) to confirm and quantify a superior gluteal nerve injury.
content.summary: In the context of THA postoperative abductor deficiency, diagnostic studies primarily involve laboratory tests (like WBC, CRP, and ESR) to rule out concurrent infection, and electrophysiologic studies such as EMG to confirm and quantify superior gluteal nerve injury.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Treatment
content.definition: Treatment depends on the etiology but includes nonoperative physical therapy (for nerve palsy or non-displaced fractures) and operative management like component revision, constrained liners, abductor muscle repair, or gluteus maximus transfer.
content.summary: Treatment for THA postoperative abductor deficiency is highly dependent on the underlying etiology, ranging from nonoperative management, such as physical therapy for partial tears or nerve palsy, to various surgical interventions. Operative options include component revision to optimize abductor tension, direct tendon repair, abductor muscle complex reconstruction using transfers or grafts, or conversion to a constrained liner.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Techniques
content.definition: Surgical techniques include component revision (to restore offset/neck length), conversion to a constrained liner or dual mobility construct, direct abductor muscle repair, gluteus maximus transfer, and greater trochanter advancement to restore muscle tension.
content.summary: Techniques for treating THA postoperative abductor deficiency include nonoperative management (physical therapy, fracture care), component revision to optimize hip mechanics, conversion to a constrained liner for stability, and specific reconstructive surgeries like direct abductor repair, muscle transfers, or allograft use.

content.title: THA Postoperative Abductor Deficiency Complications
content.definition: Complications include greater trochanteric escape (nonunion), heterotopic ossification (more common in direct lateral approach), superior gluteal nerve injury, recurrent instability, abductor repair failure, and persistent pain.
content.summary: Complications following total hip arthroplasty (THA) and subsequent abductor deficiency repair include mechanical failures, such as recurrent instability, hardware failure, and greater trochanteric escape. Other common adverse outcomes are biological issues like heterotopic ossification, persistent pain, and iatrogenic injury to the superior gluteal nerve.

content.title: THA Aseptic Loosening Summary
content.definition: THA Aseptic Loosening is a macrophage-induced inflammatory response that results in bone loss and implant loosening in the absence of an infection, typically diagnosed with plain radiographs and treated by revision arthroplasty.
content.summary: THA Aseptic Loosening is a macrophage-induced inflammatory response that results in progressive bone loss and mechanical failure of the implant components in the absence of an infection. Diagnosis is typically made using plain radiographs showing lucency or component migration, and treatment usually involves revision arthroplasty.

content.title: THA Aseptic Loosening Etiology
content.definition: The process of aseptic loosening involves prosthesis micromotion, leading to particulate debris formation, which subsequently triggers macrophage-activated osteolysis.
content.summary: The etiology of THA aseptic loosening is a mechanistic process beginning with prosthesis micromotion, which generates particulate debris; this debris subsequently activates macrophages, triggering osteolysis and ultimate implant failure.

content.title: THA Aseptic Loosening Presentation
content.definition: Aseptic loosening presents clinically as pain (groin, thigh, or knee pain), often activity-related, with a physical exam showing minimal pain on range of motion but increased pain with weight bearing.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of total hip arthroplasty (THA) aseptic loosening typically involves activity-related pain in the hip or thigh, coupled with minimal physical exam findings. Diagnosis is primarily based on plain radiographs showing lucency, component migration, or wear, while serum inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) remain normal.

content.title: THA Aseptic Loosening Studies
content.definition: Radiographs are the primary imaging modality, though they underestimate bone loss, showing findings like lucency > 2 mm at the implant-bone interface, component migration, or polyethylene liner wear. Serum labs for aseptic loosening are typically normal, with both the ESR and CRP being within normal limits, thus helping to rule out prosthetic joint infection.
content.summary: In the workup for suspected THA aseptic loosening, serum laboratory studies, such as ESR and CRP, are utilized primarily to rule out septic loosening (infection). The findings are typically characterized by normal levels of both ESR and CRP in the absence of infection.

content.title: THA Aseptic Loosening Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is nonoperative observation for stable implants with minimal symptoms, but revision THA is indicated for painful loosening, evidence of osteolysis, or extensive osteolysis that compromises future surgery.
content.summary: Definitive treatment for THA aseptic loosening, indicated for symptomatic pain or extensive osteolysis, is revision total hip arthroplasty (THA) with exchange of all loose components, though stable implants with minimal symptoms may be managed with observation.

content.title: THA Iliopsoas Impingement Summary
content.definition: THA Iliopsoas Impingement is an under-recognized cause of recurrent groin pain, typically caused by a malpositioned acetabular component, diagnosed clinically with specific provocative tests and imaging (CT/diagnostic injection), and treated with either arthroscopic tenotomy or acetabular component revision.
content.summary: THA Iliopsoas Impingement is an under-recognized etiology of recurrent groin pain after total hip replacement, typically resulting from a malpositioned acetabular component or excessive hardware. Diagnosis is based on clinical suspicion, often confirmed by CT identifying anterior cup overhang and a positive response to diagnostic injection, with treatment ranging from arthroscopic tenotomy to acetabular component revision.

content.title: THA Iliopsoas Impingement Etiology
content.definition: The impingement is typically caused by mechanical irritants such as a malpositioned acetabular component (anterior overhang), retained cement, excessive screw length, or a pre-existing limb length discrepancy.
content.summary: Etiology is the determination or study of the underlying causes or set of factors responsible for the onset of a disease or pathological condition. In the context of total hip arthroplasty impingement, common etiologic factors include a malpositioned acetabular component, retained cement, or excessive screw length.
