content.title: THA Iliopsoas Impingement Anatomy
content.definition: The iliopsoas muscle or tendon is the anatomical structure involved, impinging against the anterior rim of the acetabular component.
content.summary: The iliopsoas muscle and tendon is the key anatomical structure involved in THA iliopsoas impingement. This musculotendinous unit passes anteriorly across the hip joint and is subject to irritation or impingement by retained cement or a malpositioned acetabular component.

content.title: THA Iliopsoas Impingement Presentation
content.definition: Symptoms, which occur an average of 20 months post-procedure, include persistent groin pain and sometimes a subtle limp or tenderness, often reproduced by provocative tests like resisted seated hip flexion or a straight leg raise.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of THA Iliopsoas Impingement is characterized by recurrent groin pain, which often develops around 20 months after total hip replacement. Physical examination typically reveals groin tenderness and reproduction or exacerbation of pain with provocative tests such as resisted seated hip flexion or straight leg raise.

content.title: THA Iliopsoas Impingement Imaging
content.definition: Imaging requires routine radiographs (AP pelvis and hip views) and often a CT scan to assess prosthesis position and rule out other causes, as MRI is typically not valuable due to artifact.
content.summary: Standard imaging for THA iliopsoas impingement includes AP pelvis and hip radiographs to evaluate component position. A CT scan is particularly helpful in assessing the prosthesis position, identifying anterior cup overhang, and ruling out alternative etiologies, while MRI is usually not valuable due to artifact.

content.title: THA Iliopsoas Impingement Studies
content.definition: A diagnostic cortisone injection into the iliopsoas tendon sheath is the most helpful study for confirming the diagnosis of iliopsoas impingement.
content.summary: Diagnostic studies for THA iliopsoas impingement primarily involve a diagnostic cortisone injection into the iliopsoas sheath, which is helpful in confirming the clinical suspicion of the condition.

content.title: THA Iliopsoas Impingement Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is rarely nonoperative, generally proceeding to arthroscopic iliopsoas tenotomy for cases with normal component position, or acetabular component revision when excessive anterior cup overhang is the clear cause.
content.summary: Treatment for THA Iliopsoas Impingement is primarily operative; arthroscopic iliopsoas tenotomy is performed when acetabular component position is normal. Revision of the acetabular component is indicated when excessive anterior cup overhang is identified as the etiology.

content.title: THA Trunnionosis Summary
content.definition: Trunnionosis, also known as Mechanically Assisted Crevice Corrosion (MACC), is a complication of hip arthroplasty caused by metal ion release from corrosion at the femoral head-neck junction morse taper, leading to hip pain and adverse local tissue reactions (ALTRs).
content.summary: Trunnionosis, also referred to as Mechanically Assisted Crevice Corrosion (MACC), is a complication of total hip arthroplasty resulting from metal ion release caused by corrosion at the femoral head-neck interface Morse taper. This process leads to hip pain, adverse local tissue reactions (ALTRs) or pseudotumors, and is often diagnosed by substantially elevated serum cobalt levels compared to chromium levels.

content.title: THA Trunnionosis Epidemiology
content.definition: Risk factors include modular designs, large cobalt chrome femoral heads (which increase torsional forces), shorter taper length (less interference fit), metal-on-metal components, and mixed alloy components, with no pathognomonic serum metal ion level, though Co > 1 ng/ml is suggestive.
content.summary: The epidemiology of trunnionosis primarily describes the risk factors associated with this complication, including the use of modular designs, large cobalt chrome femoral heads, specific taper dimensions, and mixed alloy components. Clinically, serum cobalt levels exceeding 1 ng/ml are an important epidemiological consideration for mechanically assisted crevice corrosion (MACC).

content.title: THA Trunnionosis Etiology
content.definition: The pathophysiology of trunnionosis involves fretting corrosion (micromotion between the morse taper) and galvanic corrosion (between dissimilar metals), with the resulting metal debris potentially causing an Adverse Local Tissue Reaction (ALTR), such as an aseptic lymphocyte-dominant vasculitis association lesion (ALVAL/pseudotumor).
content.summary: The etiology of trunnionosis, also known as mechanically assisted crevice corrosion (MACC), encompasses different forms of corrosion occurring at the femoral head-neck taper, including galvanic, fretting, and crevice corrosion. These corrosive processes cause the release of metal ions, leading to adverse local tissue reactions.

content.title: THA Trunnionosis Presentation
content.definition: Presentation typically involves pain and a limp, with a physical exam occasionally revealing a palpable pseudocyst or abduction weakness due to soft tissue destruction from the ALTR.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of trunnionosis is most commonly characterized by patient complaints of hip pain and limp. Physical examination may reveal abduction weakness or, less frequently, a palpable pseudocyst resulting from an adverse local tissue reaction (ALTR).

content.title: THA Trunnionosis Imaging
content.definition: Imaging studies (AP/lateral radiographs) may be normal, but MARS MRI (Metal Artifact Reduction Sequence) is essential to assess for periarticular pseudotumors and soft tissue destruction, while Judet views or CT may be used to look for advanced osteolysis.
content.summary: While initial AP and lateral hip radiographs may be normal, advanced imaging modalities are crucial for trunnionosis; Judet views or CT scans evaluate for osteolysis, and Metal Artifact Reduction Sequence MRI (MARS MRI) detects periarticular pseudotumors and associated soft tissue destruction.

content.title: THA Trunnionosis Studies
content.definition: Routine studies include ESR, CRP, and WBC to rule out infection, aspiration (with manual cell count to avoid false elevation from debris), and metal ion levels, which characteristically show cobalt levels elevated substantially more than chromium (a ratio $\ge 2:1$).
content.summary: The laboratory evaluation for trunnionosis involves ruling out infection using ESR, CRP, and aspiration studies, and assessing for metal ion release. Critical findings include substantially elevated serum cobalt levels compared to chromium levels (a ratio of 2:1 or greater), which is indicative of head-neck junction corrosion.

content.title: THA Trunnionosis Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is observation for asymptomatic patients, while single stage revision THA is indicated for symptomatic patients with elevated serum cobalt (and a Co:Cr ratio at least 2:1) in the absence of infection, requiring an extensive synovectomy and inspection of the taper for possible stem retention or revision.
content.summary: Treatment for trunnionosis involves observation for asymptomatic patients, while surgical intervention is necessary for those experiencing a painful total hip arthroplasty (THA). Symptomatic patients, especially those with elevated serum cobalt levels, are typically managed with a single-stage revision THA, which includes extensive synovectomy and replacement of the modular components.

content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Summary
content.definition: A metal-on-metal (MoM) THA pseudotumor, also called aseptic lymphocyte-dominant vasculitis-associated lesion (ALVAL), is a mass-forming tissue reaction caused by metal wear, suspected with elevated serum metal ion levels and diagnosed with metal-subtraction MRI.
content.summary: A metal-on-metal (MoM) THA pseudotumor, also known as ALVAL, is a mass-forming soft tissue reaction caused by inflammatory response to metal wear debris and/or hypersensitivity in patients with MoM hip replacements. It is diagnosed by elevated serum cobalt and chromium levels alongside characteristic findings on MARS MRI, and is usually treated with revision arthroplasty if symptomatic.

content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of pseudotumor is 10%-15% of MoM THAs, accounting for 45%-50% of MoM revision surgeries, with risk factors including elevated cobalt/chromium levels, female gender, and a high acetabulum inclination angle (>55°).
content.summary: A metal-on-metal (MoM) THA pseudotumor has an incidence of 10 content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Etiology
content.definition: The pathophysiology is theorized to result from either hypersensitivity to metal ions or a localized high wear debris burden, with the reaction characterized by the presence of both macrophages and a predominant infiltration of lymphocytes.
content.summary: The formation of metal-on-metal THA pseudotumors is attributed to two proposed mechanisms: a hypersensitivity reaction to metal ions or a local tissue response induced by the presence of high wear debris.

content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Presentation
content.definition: Presentation is variable, as patients may be asymptomatic or complain of gradual or sudden groin pain, sometimes accompanied by a Trendelenburg gait or a palpable soft tissue mass.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of a metal-on-metal pseudotumor is highly variable, often being asymptomatic, but commonly involves gradual or sudden onset of groin pain and potentially a Trendelenburg gait. Physical examination may reveal palpable soft tissue masses or localized groin pain induced by hip flexion, internal rotation, and adduction.

content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Imaging
content.definition: Imaging is typically performed using MARS MRI (Metal Artifact Reduction Sequence), which is indicated when radiographs are normal but metal ion levels are elevated, and shows the pseudotumor as a fluid collection or solid mass in the periprosthetic soft tissues (generally hyperintense on T2 images).
content.summary: Diagnosis of MoM pseudotumors relies on initial plain radiographs to confirm the implant and exclude fracture, followed by specialized MRI using Metal Artifact Reduction Sequences (MARS) to identify the characteristic periprosthetic fluid collection or solid mass, especially when serum metal ions are elevated.

content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Studies
content.definition: Studies include ruling out infection with ESR/CRP/WBC and hip aspiration (which yields characteristic "dishwater fluid" and requires a manual cell count due to debris interference), and measuring serum metal ion levels, with values greater than 7 ppb being an indication for advanced imaging.
content.summary: Diagnostic studies for metal-on-metal pseudotumors include serum laboratory tests, most notably cobalt and chromium ion levels, along with WBC, ESR, and CRP. Invasive hip aspiration is also performed to analyze synovial fluid, differentiate metallosis from periprosthetic infection, and obtain accurate cell counts.

content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is observation for asymptomatic patients with low metal ion levels, but revision THA to ceramic-on-polyethylene components is the definitive operative treatment for a painful pseudotumor with elevated metal ions, though the complexity of the revision depends on the extent of bone loss and soft tissue destruction.
content.summary: Treatment for MoM THA pseudotumors depends on the patient's symptoms and serum metal ion levels; asymptomatic individuals with low ions are managed with observation. Definitive treatment for painful pseudotumors, elevated metal ions, and corresponding MRI findings is typically surgical revision using ceramic-on-polyethylene components.

content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Techniques
content.definition: Revision THA techniques involve addressing bone loss, soft tissue damage, and abductor deficiency (potentially requiring a constrained liner or gluteus maximus transfer), often performed as a single or two-stage procedure depending on the bone loss and infection status.
content.summary: Techniques for managing MoM THA pseudotumors are divided into nonoperative observation, involving serial monitoring of asymptomatic patients with radiographs, and operative revision total hip arthroplasty. Surgical revision involves converting to ceramic-on-polyethylene components, with the specific approach and staging tailored to the extent of soft tissue destruction and bone loss.

content.title: THA Pseudotumor (Metal on Metal Reactions) Complications
content.definition: Complications include a high rate of repeat revision THA (14%-20% within 5 years), often due to instability, infection, or persistent metal reaction, and rare instances of pseudotumor-induced femoral nerve palsy.
content.summary: Complications following the operative management (revision total hip arthroplasty) of metal-on-metal pseudotumors include a high rate of repeat revision THA, occurring in 14-20% of patients within five years. Additionally, rare but severe outcomes such as pseudotumor-induced femoral nerve palsy have been noted.

content.title: THA Other Complications Introduction
content.definition: This topic covers several common but distinct postoperative complications of total hip arthroplasty (THA), including heterotopic ossification, squeaking, and the management of postoperative anemia.
content.summary: This introductory section outlines various complications associated with Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA), serving as an index to the subsequent detailed discussions. Topics listed include heterotopic ossification, squeaking of prostheses, periprosthetic fractures, and management of postoperative anemia.

content.title: THA Other Complications Heterotopic Ossification
content.definition: Heterotopic Ossification (HO) is a frequent radiographic complication that rarely causes functional limitations, with risk factors including prolonged surgical time and specific conditions (like ankylosing spondylitis), and is treated by surgical excision only for severe loss of motion, with prophylaxis involving perioperative NSAIDs or radiation.
content.summary: Heterotopic ossification is the formation of mature bone in soft tissues, commonly recognized as a frequent radiographic complication following hip replacement procedures. Although it often does not cause functional limitations, severe cases that result in significant loss of motion may require surgical excision.

content.title: THA Other Complications Squeaking
content.definition: Squeaking is a high-pitched sound occurring during hip movement, most notably with ceramic-on-ceramic and metal-on-metal bearings, with the main risks being impingement, edge loading, component malposition, and the loss of fluid film lubrication.
content.summary: Squeaking is defined as a high-pitched audible sound occurring during hip movement, primarily reported as a complication following total hip arthroplasty, particularly with ceramic-on-ceramic bearing surfaces.

content.title: THA Other Complications Postoperative Anemia
content.definition: Postoperative Anemia is commonly predicted by low preoperative hemoglobin levels and is managed proactively with tranexamic acid (TXA) and conservatively with postoperative transfusions only when the patient's hemoglobin level drops to 7-8 or if the patient is symptomatic.
content.summary: Postoperative anemia refers to the development of a reduced hemoglobin concentration following a surgical procedure. Its primary clinical importance is that low preoperative hemoglobin levels are the best predictor for requiring a postoperative blood transfusion, which is often indicated when hemoglobin drops to 7-8 g/dL.

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Summary
content.definition: THA Revision is most commonly performed to address mechanical failure, fracture, instability, or infection of a prior THA, with treatment dictated by the failure's etiology, prior surgery, and patient demands.
content.summary: Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA) Revision is a surgical procedure performed to address the failure of a prior hip replacement, most commonly due to complications such as aseptic loosening, instability, periprosthetic fracture, or infection. Successful management requires accurate diagnosis using imaging and lab work, followed by appropriate operative treatment tailored to the underlying etiology and the degree of associated bone loss.

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of THA revision is projected to increase over 100% by 2030, with mechanical failure being the most common overall cause, while infection and periprosthetic fracture are key causes of early failure (under 2 years).
content.summary: The epidemiology of Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA) revision indicates a projected incidence increase of over 100% by 2030 in the United States, primarily affecting patients around 65-70 years old with roughly equal gender distribution. Mechanical failure, infection, and periprosthetic fracture are the most common etiologies driving these revisions.

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Etiology
content.definition: Indications for revision broadly include component failure, osteolysis, aseptic loosening, hip instability, periprosthetic joint infection (PJI), fracture, and complications like trunnionosis or adverse local tissue reaction (ALTR).
content.summary: Etiology refers to the underlying cause or mechanism responsible for total hip arthroplasty (THA) failure, which dictates the necessity and approach for revision surgery. Common etiologies include mechanical failure, aseptic loosening, instability, periprosthetic fracture, and periprosthetic joint infection (PJI).

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Classification of Bone Loss
content.definition: Bone loss in revision is categorized by both the AAOS Classification (Types I-V) and the Paprosky Classification (Types I-IV), which describe patterns of segmental and cavitary deficiencies in the acetabulum and femur to guide surgical reconstruction.
content.summary: Classification of bone loss involves using standardized systems, such as the AAOS and Paprosky methods, to categorize the severity, location, and type of osseous defects in the acetabulum and femur, which dictates the surgical approach during revision total hip arthroplasty.

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Presentation
content.definition: Presentation includes patient reports of groin, thigh, or start-up pain, with night pain suggesting infection, while physical exam may reveal external rotation of the extremity (suggesting femoral subsidence) or restricted hip motion.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA) failure encompasses patient-reported symptoms, such as specific patterns of pain, physical examination findings like gait abnormalities or restricted range of motion, and diagnostic evaluation using imaging and laboratory studies. These components are critical for determining the underlying etiology, such as aseptic loosening or infection, that necessitates THA revision.

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Imaging
content.definition: Imaging requires radiographs (AP pelvis, orthogonal views, full-length femur) to assess component stability and position, but CT scans are crucial for determining the extent of osteolysis and precise component position, while angiography may be needed for specific Paprosky defects.
content.summary: Imaging studies, including standard radiographs (AP pelvis, orthogonal views) and CT scans, are crucial for determining the diagnosis and etiology of total hip arthroplasty failure. These modalities are used to assess component position, evaluate bone loss (osteolysis), and classify the extent of bony defects.

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Studies
content.definition: Laboratory studies are essential to rule out infection (ESR, CRP, aspiration/cultures) and to diagnose metal-related failures by measuring serum metal ion levels (Cobalt/Chromium) for trunnionosis and pseudotumor evaluation.
content.summary: Studies relevant to THA revision encompass specific serum laboratory tests, such as ESR, CRP, CBC, and metal ion levels (cobalt and chromium), alongside joint aspiration, to evaluate potential causes of failure like periprosthetic joint infection or adverse local tissue reaction (trunnionosis).

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Treatment
content.definition: Treatment for bone loss involves a spectrum of operative options: femoral revision utilizes extensively coated stems, impaction bone grafting, or proximal femoral allografts for severe defects, while acetabular revision employs porous-coated cups, metallic augments, or reconstruction cages depending on the degree of bone loss and stability.
content.summary: Treatment for failed total hip arthroplasty (THA) is dictated by the etiology of failure, previous surgery, and patient activity demands. Management includes nonoperative strategies for pain of non-arthroplasty origin, and complex operative revisions (femoral, acetabular, or combined) categorized by the severity and location of bone loss.

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Surgical Techniques
content.definition: Surgical techniques for femoral revision emphasize bypassing the defect by two cortical diameters, often utilizing Extended Trochanteric Osteotomy (ETO) for stem removal, while acetabular techniques involve gentle reaming, filling defects with graft, and securing components with augments or cages to restore the center of rotation.
content.summary: Surgical techniques in total hip arthroplasty revision refer to the detailed operative methods used for component replacement, management of bone loss, and treatment of complications like instability or periprosthetic fracture. These procedures range from simple liner exchanges to complex reconstructions involving specialized implants, augments, or custom cages, guided by the specific classification of acetabular and femoral bone defects.

content.title: THA Revision Techniques Prognosis
content.definition: The prognosis for revision THA is characterized by lower mid-term and long-term survival rates compared to primary THA, with significantly higher rates of complications such as dislocation, infection, nerve palsy, and abductor deficiency.
content.summary: The prognosis for total hip arthroplasty (THA) revision is less favorable than for primary THA, characterized by lower mid-term and long-term implant survival rates. Patients undergoing revision also experience higher rates of complications, such as dislocation, infection, nerve palsy, and fractures.

content.title: Knee Biomechanics Introduction
content.definition: The knee joint is comprised of two distinct articulations: the tibiofemoral joint and the patellofemoral joint.
content.summary: The knee is a complex joint comprised of two primary articulations: the tibiofemoral joint and the patellofemoral joint. These joints work together to transmit body weight, increase the lever arm of the extensor mechanism, and provide dynamic and static stability throughout a wide range of motion.

content.title: Knee Biomechanics Patellofemoral Articulation
content.definition: The patellofemoral articulation functions to transmit the tensile forces of the quadriceps to the patellar tendon, increasing the extensor mechanism's lever arm, with joint reaction forces reaching up to 7x body weight during squatting and its stability conferred primarily by the medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL).
content.summary: The patellofemoral articulation is the sliding joint between the patella and the femur, which functions primarily to transmit the tensile forces generated by the quadriceps muscles to the patellar tendon. This mechanism significantly increases the lever arm of the knee's extensor mechanism, enabling efficient knee extension and bearing joint reaction forces up to seven times body weight.

content.title: Knee Biomechanics Tibiofemoral Articulation
content.definition: The tibiofemoral articulation is primarily responsible for the transmission of body weight, exhibiting a range of motion from 0 degrees to 155 degrees of flexion, and its stability is maintained by the crucial cruciate ligaments (ACL/PCL, which are the primary static restraints to anterior and posterior translation, respectively) and collateral ligaments (LCL/MCL).
content.summary: The tibiofemoral articulation is the major load-bearing joint of the knee, connecting the distal femur and the proximal tibia. Its primary function is the transmission of body weight while facilitating sagittal plane motion and complex rotational mechanics, such as the screw home mechanism.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee Introduction
content.definition: The knee is a diarthrodial joint allowing simultaneous rotation and translation, with its ligaments providing stability and acting as primary and secondary restraints to movement.
content.summary: The knee is a complex diarthrodial joint characterized by simultaneous rotation and translation. This introduction establishes the foundational anatomy, detailing the layered soft tissue structures and the specific biomechanical functions of the major stabilizing ligaments, including the ACL, PCL, and collateral ligaments.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee ACL
content.definition: The Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) is an extrasynovial, intracapsular ligament with two main bundles (AM tight in flexion, PL tight in extension) that functions as the primary static restraint to anterolateral displacement of the tibia on the femur, with a native tensile strength of 2,200 N.
content.summary: The Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) is a crucial intracapsular structure of the knee, consisting of anteromedial and posterolateral bundles, whose primary biomechanical function is to prevent anterior translation and anterolateral displacement of the tibia relative to the femur.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee PCL
content.definition: The Posterior Cruciate Ligament (PCL) is the primary static restraint to posterior translation of the tibia on the femur, especially at 90° of flexion, and works synergistically with the PLC to resist posterolateral rotatory instability.
content.summary: The Posterior Cruciate Ligament (PCL) is a strong, intracapsular ligament of the knee joint that serves as the primary restraint against posterior displacement and translation of the tibia relative to the femur. It is composed of two bundles (anterolateral and posteromedial) and works with the posterolateral corner to ensure rotational stability.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee LCL (lateral collateral ligament or fibular collateral ligament)
content.definition: The Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL) is a strong, cord-like structure that runs superficial to the popliteus tendon, tightens in extension, and functions as the primary restraint to varus angulation at 30 degrees of flexion.
content.summary: The Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL), or fibular collateral ligament, is a cord-like structure extending from the lateral femoral condyle to the fibula. Its primary function is to resist varus angulation, working synergistically with other structures to restrain axial rotation.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee PLC (posterolateral corner)
content.definition: The Posterolateral Corner (PLC) is a synergistic complex, including the LCL, popliteus muscle/tendon, and popliteofibular ligament, that primarily works with the PCL to control external rotation and posterior translation of the tibia.
content.summary: The PLC, or posterolateral corner, is a complex anatomical region of the knee that includes the lateral collateral ligament, popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. It functions synergistically with the PCL to stabilize the knee by primarily resisting posterolateral rotation, varus angulation, and posterior tibial displacement.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee MCL
content.definition: The Medial Collateral Ligament (MCL) is the primary stabilizer to valgus stress at all angles of flexion, consisting of a superficial portion (tibial collateral ligament) and a deep portion (medial capsular ligament), and works with the ACL to restrain axial rotation.
content.summary: The Medial Collateral Ligament (MCL) is the primary static stabilizer of the knee against valgus angulation and works with the Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) to provide restraint against axial rotation. It consists of superficial and deep portions, with the superficial component being the dominant restraint to valgus stress across all angles of flexion.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee Posteromedial corner
content.definition: The Posteromedial Corner lies deep to the MCL, is formed by the insertion of the semimembranosus and the posterior oblique ligament, and is crucial for rotatory stability and resisting valgus load in full extension.
content.summary: The posteromedial corner is an anatomical region of the knee located deep to the medial collateral ligament (MCL) that is critical for rotatory stability. It is formed by the insertion of the semimembranosus, the posterior oblique ligament, the oblique popliteal ligament, and the posterior capsule, and resists valgus stress and tibial internal rotation, particularly in full extension.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee Medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL)
content.definition: The Medial Patellofemoral Ligament (MPFL) is the primary passive restraint to lateral translation of the patella from 0 degrees to 30 degrees of knee flexion (providing 60% of total restraining force), lies in the second layer of the medial soft tissue, and is the ligament most commonly torn off its femoral attachment during patellar dislocation.
content.summary: The Medial Patellofemoral Ligament (MPFL) is a critical ligament in the second layer of the medial knee soft tissue complex that connects the medial femoral condyle to the superomedial border of the patella. Its main function is to provide restraint against lateral translation of the patella, primarily between 0 and 30 degrees of knee flexion.

content.title: Ligaments of the Knee Anterolateral Ligament
content.definition: The Anterolateral Ligament (ALL) lies in the third layer with the LCL, attaches between the lateral femoral epicondyle and the tibia (midway between Gerdy's tubercle and the fibula head), and contributes significantly to rotational stability, with its avulsion fracture known as a Segond's fracture often associated with ACL rupture.
content.summary: The Anterolateral Ligament (ALL) is a lateral knee structure found in Layer 3, originating at the lateral femoral epicondyle and inserting on the tibia between Gerdy's tubercle and the head of the fibula. Its primary function is to provide essential rotational stability to the joint.

content.title: Knee Physical Exam - Adult Inspection
content.definition: Inspection assesses for skin and soft tissue abnormalities, bony alignment (varus/valgus, length, contractures), and characteristic gait patterns such as a varus thrust (LCL/PLC insufficiency), antalgic (painful), or flexed knee gait.
content.summary: Inspection is the initial phase of the physical examination involving the visual assessment of the patient's anatomy and movement. For the knee, this includes observing the skin, soft tissues, and bony structures for discoloration, swelling, deformity, and symmetry, and evaluating gait patterns for abnormalities.

content.title: Knee Physical Exam - Adult Palpation
content.definition: Palpation identifies pain and pathology in bony structures (patella, tibial tubercle, joint line), soft tissues (patellar/quadriceps tendons, collateral ligaments, pes anserine bursa), and the popliteal fossa, while assessing for intra-articular effusion using patella ballotting or milking techniques.
content.summary: Palpation is a physical examination technique utilizing touch to assess anatomical structures for tenderness, swelling, and mobility. In the knee assessment, it is used to specifically locate joint line pain, identify soft tissue injuries, and evaluate effusions or patellar characteristics.

content.title: Knee Physical Exam - Adult Neurovascular
content.definition: Neurovascular evaluation checks motor function (knee flexion/extension, foot plantarflexion/dorsiflexion via the sciatic/femoral/tibial/peroneal nerves), assesses sensory distribution, palpates the popliteal/pedis/posterior tibial pulses, and tests the patellar reflex (L4).
content.summary: The neurovascular examination systematically assesses the functional integrity of the nervous system (motor function, sensory perception, and reflexes) and the circulatory system (peripheral pulses) of a limb. This assessment is crucial for detecting nerve injury or vascular compromise following trauma or other musculoskeletal conditions.

content.title: Knee Physical Exam - Adult ROM
content.definition: Range of Motion (ROM) of the knee is measured for flexion (125°-135°), extension (0°-10° hyperextension), and rotation (10°-15° internal/external tibial rotation).
content.summary: Range of motion (ROM) is a fundamental component of the physical exam that quantifies the total degree of movement available at a joint. For the knee, this assessment includes the specific measurement of flexion, extension, and internal and external tibial rotation.

content.title: Knee Physical Exam - Adult Special Tests
content.definition: Special Tests are used to assess the ACL (Lachman's, most sensitive, Anterior Drawer, Pivot Shift), PCL (Posterior Drawer, Posterior Sag, Quadriceps Active), Collateral Ligaments (Valgus/Varus Stress at 0° and 30° flexion), Meniscus (Joint Line Tenderness, most sensitive, McMurray's, Apley's Compression), Patellofemoral Joint (Patella Apprehension, J-sign, Patella Grind), and the Extensor Mechanism (Straight Leg Raise).
content.summary: In orthopedic examination, special tests are specific, targeted maneuvers designed to assess the integrity, stability, and function of particular joint structures, such as ligaments and menisci. These standardized procedures help identify underlying pathology by reproducing signs of instability or pain specific to the structure being evaluated.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Introduction
content.definition: This section outlines the two main articulations of the knee (tibiofemoral and patellofemoral), the key bone structures and ligament/muscle attachments, the various standard and specialized radiographic views, and the normal and pathological findings used for evaluation, especially in the context of total knee arthroplasty (TKA).
content.summary: This introductory section establishes the foundational knowledge for the knee joint, reviewing its essential anatomy, including osteology and soft tissue attachments, and detailing various standard and specialized radiographic views used for imaging the joint. It sets the stage for subsequent clinical discussions on topics such as osteoarthritis, surgical templating, and total knee arthroplasty complications.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Osteology
content.definition: The Osteology section details the gross anatomy of the distal femur, proximal tibia, and patella from anterior/posterior and lateral perspectives.
content.summary: Osteology in this context refers to the comprehensive study of the bony anatomy of the knee, specifically the distal femur, proximal tibia, and patella, and their associated attachments. It also details the specialized radiographic views and techniques required for evaluating structure, alignment, and pathology for accurate clinical assessment and surgical planning.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Attachments
content.definition: The Attachments section lists the muscles, ligaments, and tendons that attach to the knee bones, again from multiple standard radiographic views.
content.summary: Attachments, in the context of knee osteology, are the specific anatomical locations on the distal femur, proximal tibia, and patella where muscles, ligaments, and tendons originate or insert. These crucial connection points are typically analyzed using various views (anterior, posterior, and lateral) to understand the structural organization of the joint.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation AP/PA View
content.definition: The AP/PA View is a standard film used to check fracture, joint alignment, and osteoarthritis (OA), taken with the knee extended and internally rotated 3-5 degrees, with the PA view generally preferred for better joint space evaluation.
content.summary: The Anteroposterior (AP) or Posteroanterior (PA) view is a standard radiographic projection of the knee used primarily to evaluate for fractures, joint alignment, and osteoarthritis. It is performed with the patient positioned supine (AP) or prone (PA), the knee extended, and the beam aimed 1.5 cm distal to the apex of the patella.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Weightbearing AP/PA (Rosenberg) view
content.definition: The Weightbearing AP/PA (Rosenberg) view is a specialized projection, typically taken with the knees flexed 45 degrees (Rosenberg) or extended (AP), that is preferred for detecting early joint space narrowing in OA due to the physiological location of cartilage wear under load.
content.summary: The Weightbearing AP/PA (Rosenberg) view is a specialized knee radiograph performed with the patient standing and the knees flexed 45 degrees, typically taken bilaterally. This view is highly sensitive for detecting joint space narrowing and is preferred for evaluating osteoarthritis (OA) compared to standard non-flexed weightbearing views.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Full-length weightbearing AP (PCA) view
content.definition: The Full-length weightbearing AP (PCA) view uses three spliced films of the erect lower extremities to determine the overall mechanical axis of the limb, which is critical for preoperative OA planning and distributing weight-bearing loads equally to ensure implant longevity.
content.summary: The full-length weightbearing AP (PCA) view is a specialized radiographic assessment that captures the entire mechanical axis of the bilateral lower extremities while the patient is standing, usually requiring three separate films to be spliced together. This view is essential for quantifying limb deformity, measuring leg length, and planning surgical correction for conditions like osteoarthritis.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Lateral View
content.definition: The Lateral View is taken with the knee flexed 20 to 30 degrees (rolled lateral) or extended (horizontal ray) to visualize the suprapatellar fat pad, assess patella alta/baja, and check for trochlear dysplasia, with superimposition of the posterior femoral condyles indicating proper rotation.
content.summary: The Lateral View is a standard radiographic projection of the knee, typically obtained with the patient rolled laterally and the knee flexed 20-30°, or using a horizontal beam for specific injuries like acute patella fractures. This view is crucial for evaluating fractures, joint effusions, trochlear dysplasia, and assessing patellar height (alta/baja).

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Oblique View
content.definition: The Oblique View is taken with the leg internally/externally rotated 45 degrees to better visualize fractures of the patella/femoral condyle, OCD lesions, and pathology within the intercondylar fossa.
content.summary: The Oblique View is a supplementary radiographic projection of the knee obtained by rotating the leg 45 degrees internally or externally from the standard position. This view is primarily indicated for evaluating subtle fractures of the femoral condyle or patella, osteochondral defects, and pathology like loose bodies within the intercondylar fossa.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Tangential View
content.definition: The Tangential View (e.g., Sunrise, Hughston, Merchant) uses varying degrees of knee flexion to provide an en face view of the patellofemoral joint, which is essential for assessing patellar malalignment or tilt, trochlear groove depth, and vertical patella fractures.
content.summary: The tangential view is a specialized radiographic projection of the knee, often employing techniques like Sunrise or Merchant, which requires significant knee flexion to visualize the patellofemoral joint. This view is essential for evaluating patellar alignment, patellar tilt, and the depth of the trochlear groove by projecting the patella clearly away from the femoral condyles.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Intercondylar View
content.definition: The Intercondylar View (e.g., Beclere, Tunnel) uses a flexed knee position and specific beam angulations to provide an unobstructed view of the intercondylar fossa, which is necessary for diagnosing OCD lesions, loose bodies, and congenital slipped patella.
content.summary: The Intercondylar View is a specialized knee radiographic projection, employing techniques such as the Camp Coventry or Beclere methods, which require knee flexion and specific beam angles. This view is used to optimally visualize the intercondylar fossa for diagnosing related pathology, including loose bodies, osteochondritis dissecans (OCD), and intercondylar widening.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Normal Findings
content.definition: Normal Findings include common osseous normal variants such as the fabella (sesamoid in the lateral head of the gastrocnemius), cyamella (in the popliteus tendon), and bipartite/tripartite patella, which should be differentiated from true pathology.
content.summary: Normal findings in knee imaging include the expected anatomy and common benign variations, such as accessory sesamoid bones (fabella and cyamella), the cortical desmoid lucency, and developmental states like bipartite or tripartite patella.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation Knee Osteoarthritis
content.definition: The Kellgren and Lawrence Classification is the standard system used to grade knee OA severity from Grade 0 (normal) to Grade 4 (marked joint space narrowing and large osteophytes) based on weightbearing X-rays, which are most sensitive for detecting early joint space narrowing (JSN).
content.summary: Knee Osteoarthritis (OA) is a common degenerative condition of the knee joint characterized by the progressive loss of articular cartilage. Radiographically, it is defined by features such as joint space narrowing, the formation of osteophytes, and subchondral bone changes.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation TKA templating
content.definition: TKA Templating requires full-length views to assess the mechanical axis of the limb (neutral axis is key to minimize wear/loosening), determine the femoral resection angle (MAF - AAF), and establish the tibial bone cut (line perpendicular to MAT), ensuring the joint line is not elevated or lowered excessively (avoiding mid-flexion or flexion instability).
content.summary: TKA templating is the essential preoperative planning phase that utilizes specialized radiographs, such as the full-length scanogram, to accurately measure the limb's mechanical axis and coronal alignment. The process determines the appropriate component sizing and guides the necessary bone resection angles (femoral and tibial cuts) required to restore a neutral mechanical axis and ensure optimal joint kinematics.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation TKA periprosthetic fracture
content.definition: TKA Periprosthetic Fractures are classified by location (distal femur via Lewis & Rorabeck or SuSu; proximal tibia via Felix; patella via Goldberg), with treatment dictated by the fracture pattern and the stability of the implant.
content.summary: A periprosthetic fracture following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is a fracture occurring in the bone adjacent to the prosthetic components, typically involving the distal femur, proximal tibia, or patella. These fractures are categorized using specific classification systems based on their location, displacement, and the stability of the associated implant.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation TKA aseptic loosening
content.definition: TKA Aseptic Loosening is suspected on radiographs by a radiolucent area greater than 2 mm around the implant or cement and change in implant position (subsidence or varus/valgus migration), with lateral and oblique views being most helpful for identifying femoral osteolysis.
content.summary: Aseptic loosening in Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA) is the non-infectious failure of the prosthetic interface, typically characterized on imaging by a progressive radiolucent line exceeding 2 mm at the bone-prosthesis or cement-bone interface, component subsidence, or cement cracking.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation TKA instability
content.definition: TKA Instability is classified by its timing and plane—extension (varus/valgus), flexion (AP), or mid-flexion (malrotation)—and is evaluated with weightbearing and flexion lateral views to assess joint line symmetry, component sizing, and component positioning.
content.summary: TKA instability refers to abnormal laxity or excessive motion of the knee joint following total knee arthroplasty, which is classified based on the specific degree of knee flexion during which the instability occurs (extension, flexion, or mid-flexion gap).

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation TKA patellar maltracking
content.definition: TKA Patellar Maltracking is evaluated with AP (Q angle/femoral component position), lateral (femoral component offset), and tangential views (subluxation/tilt) to identify findings like a laterally subluxed patella or anterior placement of the femoral component.
content.summary: TKA patellar maltracking is the abnormal movement or misalignment of the patella relative to the femoral component, most commonly presenting as lateral subluxation or tilt. This mechanical complication is often caused by technical errors during surgery, such as increased Q angle, anterior placement of the femoral component, or asymmetric patellar resection.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation TKA patellar prosthesis loosening
content.definition: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening is primarily diagnosed on lateral and tangential views by observing a loss of the radiolucent space (contact between patella bone and femoral component) or a dissociated patellar component.
content.summary: TKA patellar prosthesis loosening is the mechanical failure of the implant-bone interface, which is radiographically identified by findings such as the dissociation of the patellar component or the loss of the normal radiolucent space causing direct contact between the native patella and the femoral component.

content.title: Adult Knee Radiographic Evaluation TKA heterotopic ossification
content.definition: TKA Heterotopic Ossification (HO) is classified by Furia and Pellegrini and appears as ossification along the anterior distal aspect of the femur and within the quadriceps mechanism (typically visible 3-4 weeks post-op), with excision reserved for cases causing snapping, tracking difficulties, or instability.
content.summary: Heterotopic ossification following Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA) is the abnormal formation of new bone, typically observed along the anterior distal aspect of the femur and within the quadriceps mechanism. This process is usually visible on radiographs within 3-4 weeks post-operatively and is classified based on the extent and continuity of the ossification.

content.title: Knee Osteoarthritis Summary
content.definition: Knee Osteoarthritis (OA) is a progressive, degenerative joint disease causing articular cartilage loss, diagnosed primarily with plain radiographs, and initially treated with nonoperative measures like NSAIDs and physical therapy, with knee arthroplasty indicated for severe, symptomatic disease.
content.summary: Knee osteoarthritis is a common degenerative disease of the knee joint characterized by the progressive loss of articular cartilage, leading to symptoms like pain, stiffness, and joint instability. Diagnosis relies primarily on plain radiographs, and treatment progresses from nonoperative modalities like NSAIDs and rehabilitation to eventual knee arthroplasty for severe, symptomatic disease.

content.title: Knee Osteoarthritis Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of symptomatic knee OA (240 per 100,000 per year) is significantly higher than hip OA (88 per 100,000 per year), and risk factors include large body mass, articular trauma, female gender, and increased age, while African American males are the least likely to receive a total joint replacement compared to whites and Hispanics.
content.summary: Epidemiology encompasses the study of disease frequency and distribution, specifically detailing the incidence of knee osteoarthritis and identifying associated factors, such as modifiable risks (e.g., articular trauma, high body mass) and non-modifiable risks (e.g., increased age, female gender).

content.title: Knee Osteoarthritis Etiology
content.definition: The pathophysiology involves degradation of the articular cartilage (loss of proteoglycans and collagen disorganization), an imbalance of matrix metalloproteases (MMPs) and their inhibitors, and inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6) secreted by the synovium, leading to bone changes (subchondral sclerosis/cysts) and osteophyte formation (mediated by Indian hedgehog signaling).
content.summary: Etiology refers to the underlying causes and mechanisms responsible for the initiation and progression of a disease. For knee osteoarthritis, the etiology is multifactorial, encompassing structural degeneration (pathoanatomy), cellular processes involving proteolytic enzymes and inflammatory cytokines, and genetic factors leading to progressive articular cartilage loss.

content.title: Knee Osteoarthritis Classification
content.definition: The Kellgren and Lawrence Classification is the standard system used to grade knee OA severity from Grade 0 (normal) to Grade 4 (marked joint space narrowing, large osteophytes, and severe sclerosis) based on AP weight-bearing radiographs.
content.summary: Classification of knee osteoarthritis is typically performed using the Kellgren & Lawrence grading system, which assigns scores from Grade 0 to Grade 4 based on findings from AP weightbearing radiographs. This system assesses the severity of degenerative changes primarily by evaluating the presence and extent of joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation.

content.title: Knee Osteoarthritis Presentation
content.definition: The presentation includes function-limiting knee pain, stiffness, activity-induced swelling, and mechanical symptoms (locking/catching), with physical exam often showing a flexion contracture, limited range of motion (flexion <110°), and a characteristic antalgic gait with an increased adductor moment.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of knee osteoarthritis is characterized by symptoms such as function-limiting pain, stiffness, and mechanical symptoms like locking or catching. Physical examination typically reveals an antalgic gait, joint effusion, and restricted range of motion, often including a fixed flexion contracture.

content.title: Knee Osteoarthritis Imaging
content.definition: Imaging relies on weight-bearing views (with the 45-degree semi-flexed PA view being most sensitive for early joint space narrowing), which are used to assess the pattern (medial, lateral, or patellofemoral) and characteristic features of OA (joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis/cysts).
content.summary: Imaging for knee osteoarthritis relies primarily on weight-bearing radiographs to confirm the diagnosis and classify the severity of the degenerative disease, often using the Kellgren & Lawrence system. Key radiographic findings include joint space narrowing, the presence of osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, and cysts.

content.title: Knee Osteoarthritis Studies
content.definition: Histology of the affected joint reveals the loss of superficial chondrocytes, fissuring, breakdown of the tidemark, and eburnation of the subchondral bone, reflecting the underlying cartilage destruction.
content.summary: Studies, as discussed in the context of knee osteoarthritis, refer primarily to the histological examination of joint tissues. These analyses reveal characteristic pathological features, including the loss of superficial chondrocytes, fissuring of cartilage, breakdown of the tidemark, and eburnation of the subchondral bone.

content.title: Knee Osteoarthritis Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is primarily nonoperative, with strong evidence supporting the use of oral and topical NSAIDs, tramadol, rehabilitation/exercise, and weight loss (for BMI > 25), while operative treatment includes total knee arthroplasty (TKA) for end-stage OA, unicompartmental arthroplasty (UKA) for isolated disease, or high-tibial osteotomy (HTO) for younger patients with medial unicompartmental OA.
content.summary: Treatment for knee osteoarthritis begins with nonoperative measures such as NSAIDs, physical rehabilitation, and weight loss for symptomatic patients. If symptoms progress despite these efforts and the disease becomes severe, operative interventions like total knee arthroplasty are indicated.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Summary
content.definition: Adult Limb Deformity is a lower extremity condition resulting from various causes (trauma, metabolic disorders, arthritis) that alters the bone's anatomic axis, is diagnosed clinically and with full limb-length radiographs, and is treated with osteotomy or arthroplasty to correct the mechanical axis.
content.summary: Adult Limb Deformity is a lower extremity condition resulting from causes such as fracture malunion, trauma, or metabolic bone disorders, characterized by significant mechanical axis deviation. Diagnosis requires clinical evaluation and full limb length radiographs, with definitive treatment often involving corrective osteotomy of the femur and/or tibia.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of limb deformity varies by underlying cause, affects all ages (trauma/growth abnormalities in younger patients), and typically affects the lower extremity (femur, tibia, or both), with no gender predilection.
content.summary: Epidemiology describes the frequency and distribution of adult limb deformity, noting that incidence varies depending on the underlying cause and that there is no specific gender predilection. Younger patients are often affected due to trauma or childhood growth abnormalities, while key risk factors include family history and prior trauma.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Etiology
content.definition: The etiology involves an underlying condition (malunion, metabolic bone disease, childhood abnormality) that alters the anatomic axis, causing mechanical axis deviation (MAD) and subsequent abnormal joint contact pressures and ligamentous instability that lead to joint degeneration.
content.summary: The etiology of Adult Limb Deformity encompasses the underlying causes that alter the anatomic axis of the bone, resulting in significant mechanical axis deviation. These causes typically include acquired conditions like trauma, fracture malunion/nonunion, and advanced arthritis, as well as developmental or systemic issues such as childhood growth abnormalities and metabolic bone disorders.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Anatomy
content.definition: The anatomy of the femur and tibia is essential for correction, noting the 5°-7° difference between the femoral anatomic axis (AAF) and mechanical axis (MAF), the equality of the tibial axes, and the role of the collateral ligaments (MCL/LCL) in maintaining coronal plane stability.
content.summary: Anatomy, in the context of limb deformity correction, defines the essential osteological parameters and ligamentous structures of the lower extremity, including normal bone angles and mechanical axes, which are critical for accurate diagnosis and precise surgical planning.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Classification
content.definition: Classification of adult limb deformity is descriptive, detailing the bone(s) affected, the position (varus/valgus, procurvatum/recurvatum), and the degree of angulation and translation.
content.summary: Classification of adult limb deformity is descriptive, characterizing the condition based on the specific bone or bones affected, the nature of the malalignment (e.g., varus/valgus or procurvatum/recurvatum), and the overall degree of the resulting deformity.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Presentation
content.definition: The presentation includes a history of prior trauma or bone disease, common symptoms of pain (medial for varus, lateral for valgus), subtle to obvious deformity, and physical exam findings of coronal/sagittal malalignment, rotational deformity (e.g., increased femoral anteversion), and ligamentous laxity.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of adult limb deformity includes patient history of trauma or bone disease, symptoms such as pain localized according to the deformity, visible structural changes, and abnormal gait. Diagnosis is established through a physical examination assessing alignment and ligamentous stability, confirmed by weight-bearing full-length radiographs used to calculate mechanical axis deviation and the center of rotation and angulation (CORA).

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Imaging
content.definition: Imaging relies on weight-bearing AP and lateral full-length lower extremity views (with the patellas facing anteriorly) to measure the mechanical axis deviation (MAD) and precise anatomic/mechanical angles (aLDFA, MPTA) to determine the CORA (center of rotation and angulation) which dictates the site and degree of correction, with CT used for assessing rotational malalignment.
content.summary: Imaging for adult limb deformity primarily relies on weight-bearing full-length lower extremity radiographs to assess the anatomic and mechanical axes. This process allows precise measurement of mechanical axis deviation (MAD) and the center of rotation and angulation (CORA), which are essential for accurate surgical planning.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Treatment
content.definition: Treatment prioritizes the correction of angulation, translation, length, and rotation in that order, with nonoperative bracing used for subtle or minimal deficits, and operative osteotomy (derotational, valgus/varus-producing femoral or tibial) or total knee arthroplasty (TKA) used for more severe or arthritic deformities.
content.summary: Treatment for adult limb deformity focuses on restoring neutral mechanical alignment by correcting underlying angular, translational, length, and rotational issues. This is primarily achieved through surgical methods, such as various osteotomies (femoral or tibial) or total knee arthroplasty, although nonoperative bracing may be used for subtle deformities or when surgery is contraindicated.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Techniques
content.definition: Surgical techniques involve osteotomies (e.g., medial opening wedge tibial, lateral closing wedge femoral) performed at the CORA to correct alignment, gradual limb lengthening using external fixators or intramedullary nails for LLD greater than 2 cm, and TKA to restore neutral mechanical alignment, sometimes requiring additional corrective extra-articular osteotomy for severe malalignment.
content.summary: In the context of adult limb deformity correction, techniques refer to the detailed surgical approaches and procedural methods, such as various osteotomies, internal fixation, and gradual distraction osteogenesis, used to restore neutral mechanical alignment and correct rotational or length discrepancies.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Complications
content.definition: Complications include deformity under/overcorrection (leading to unfavorable compartment loading), nonunion (with rates around 5% and increased risk with smoking, age, or obesity), ligamentous damage, patella baja (following tibial osteotomies), and infection.
content.summary: Complications following surgical correction of adult limb deformities, such as osteotomies, include technical errors like deformity undercorrection or overcorrection, and biological issues such as nonunion (reported rate around 5%). Other significant risks involve infection, patella baja following tibial procedures, and damage to surrounding ligaments or tendons.

content.title: Adult Limb Deformity & Correction Prognosis
content.definition: The prognosis for surgically corrected deformities shows excellent radiographic and clinical outcomes (>90% survival) within the first 5 years, though this drops to 50%-70% after 10 years, with 15%-25% of patients eventually requiring TKA.
content.summary: Following surgical correction, the prognosis is excellent in the short term, with radiographic and clinical success rates exceeding 90% within the first five years. However, long-term outcomes decline to 50                70% after 10 years, and unfavorable risk factors include older age, obesity, and insufficient correction.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Summary
content.definition: High Tibial Osteotomy (HTO) is a surgical procedure predominantly performed to correct varus deformities in young patients with unicompartmental osteoarthritis (OA) to prevent disease progression, but is contraindicated in cases of inflammatory arthritis, bicompartmental OA, or significant flexion contracture/ligamentous instability.
content.summary: High Tibial Osteotomy (HTO) is a surgical procedure performed primarily in young, active patients to correct angular deformities, such as varus or valgus, of the knee. The procedure aims to shift the mechanical axis of the lower extremity to unload the involved joint compartment and halt the progression of unicompartmental osteoarthritis.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Epidemiology
content.definition: The most common indication for HTO is primary or secondary medial knee arthrosis, with isolated lateral compartment OA being much less common.
content.summary: The epidemiology of indications for High Tibial Osteotomy reveals that primary or secondary medial knee arthrosis is the most common reason for the procedure, while isolated lateral compartment osteoarthritis is encountered significantly less often.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Etiology
content.definition: The etiology for HTO use is that angular deformity in the knee leads to abnormal distribution of weight-bearing stresses, which accelerates wear in the medial or lateral compartments, often necessitating the procedure (most commonly for varus deformities) to create a better mechanical environment.
content.summary: The underlying cause necessitating High Tibial Osteotomy (HTO) is an angular deformity (most often varus, less often valgus) in the knee. This malalignment results in an abnormal distribution of weight-bearing stresses, accelerating wear and leading to degeneration in the medial or lateral compartments.

search_keywords: Femoral head to ankle joint center

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Anatomy
content.definition: The mechanical axis of the lower extremity, assessed by a straight line from the center of the femoral head to the center of the ankle joint, should pass just medial to the medial tibial spine in a neutrally aligned leg.
content.summary: Anatomy relevant to HTO involves the mechanical axis of the lower extremity, which is defined by a straight line connecting the center of the femoral head to the center of the ankle joint. In a normally aligned knee, this axis passes slightly medial to the medial tibial spine.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Classification
content.definition: The classification of these deformities is descriptive, mainly focusing on Varus vs. Valgus alignment.
content.summary: Classification of knee deformity in the context of High Tibial Osteotomy (HTO) is based on the type of angular malalignment present. This system distinguishes between varus alignment, which is the most common indication, and less frequent valgus alignment.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Presentation
content.definition: The presentation includes pain on the medial or lateral side of the knee and objective knee malalignment on physical examination.
content.summary: Patients presenting for High Tibial Osteotomy typically report pain on the medial or lateral side of the knee, which correlates with observable knee malalignment on physical exam. This malalignment is quantified using standing hip-to-ankle radiographs to assess the deviation of the mechanical axis.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Imaging
content.definition: Imaging requires standing alignment hip-to-ankle films to visualize the overall knee malalignment using the mechanical axis line.
content.summary: Imaging, specifically standing alignment hip-to-ankle radiographs, is required to visualize the mechanical axis of the lower extremity and precisely determine the degree of knee malalignment. These films guide surgical planning by quantifying the necessary correction for varus or valgus deformities.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Studies
content.definition: Treatment is indicated for young, active patients typically less than 50 years with unicompartmental disease who are not obese or compromised, and have pain interfering with daily life, while being non-compliant or having inflammatory arthritis or bicompartmental OA are general contraindications.
content.summary: Although specific data is absent in this section of the text, "Studies" typically refers to the compilation of clinical research and literature providing the evidence base for High Tibial Osteotomy (HTO), including evaluations of efficacy, long-term survival rates, and reported complication frequencies.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Treatment
content.definition: The valgus-producing tibial osteotomy (most common) aims to unload the medial compartment and is typically overcorrected to 8 to 10 degrees of valgus, while the less common varus-producing tibial osteotomy is used for valgus knees with lateral compartment degeneration, with a deformity of greater than 12 degrees being a contraindication due to joint line obliquity.
content.summary: High Tibial Osteotomy (HTO) is the principal surgical treatment used in younger, active patients with unicompartmental osteoarthritis caused by angular knee deformities, primarily varus. The goal of HTO is to surgically correct tibial malalignment, thereby shifting the mechanical axis to unload the degenerating compartment and slow the progression of disease.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Technique
content.definition: Surgical techniques include the lateral closing wedge (more inherent stability, no required bone graft), medial opening wedge (preserves posterior slope, avoids the peroneal nerve), and focal dome osteotomy (corrects limb alignment with minimal bone translation), with the surgeon selecting the technique based on the required correction and potential complications.
content.summary: The surgical methods used for High Tibial Osteotomy primarily include the lateral closing wedge, the medial opening wedge, and the focal dome osteotomy. These techniques determine how the proximal tibia is cut and fixed (ORIF) to achieve the necessary angular correction and realign the mechanical axis of the limb.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Complications
content.definition: Complications include recurrence of deformity (60% failure rate after three years if undercorrected or if the patient is overweight), loss of posterior slope, patella baja (shortened patellar tendon), compartment syndrome, peroneal nerve palsy (more common with lateral osteotomies), and malunion/nonunion.
content.summary: Complications following High Tibial Osteotomy (HTO) are the adverse clinical events and structural failures resulting from the surgery, which may include recurrence of deformity, malunion or nonunion, loss of posterior slope, patella baja, and serious acute issues like compartment syndrome or peroneal nerve palsy.

content.title: High Tibial Osteotomy Prognosis
content.definition: The prognosis is good, with the success rate for varus-producing HTO being 87% at 10 years and valgus-producing HTO being 50%-85%, often providing good clinical results before eventual conversion to total knee arthroplasty.
content.summary: The prognosis following high tibial osteotomy varies based on the type of correction, demonstrating a 10-year success rate of 87% for varus-producing osteotomies and 50-85% for valgus-producing osteotomies. These procedures generally yield good clinical results, which allows for eventual conversion to total knee arthroplasty if necessary.

content.title: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty Summary
content.definition: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty (UKA) is a surgical option for arthritis isolated to a single compartment (medial, lateral, or patellofemoral), with progression of osteoarthritis and aseptic loosening being the most common reasons for conversion to a total knee arthroplasty (TKA).
content.summary: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty (UKA) is a surgical intervention reserved for patients with isolated osteoarthritis in one knee compartment (medial, lateral, or patellofemoral), offering advantages like faster rehabilitation and preserved kinematics compared to Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA). However, common reasons for conversion to TKA over time include aseptic loosening and the progression of osteoarthritis in the remaining compartments.

content.title: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty Epidemiology
content.definition: UKA is a niche procedure, performed in only 5% of knee arthroplasties, with the medial compartment being the most common site.
content.summary: Unicompartmental knee arthroplasty (UKA) represents approximately 5% of all indicated knee replacement surgeries. It is most frequently performed in the medial compartment, utilizing either historical fixed-bearing or newer mobile-bearing implants.

content.title: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty Types of implants
content.definition: Implants are categorized as fixed-bearing (the historical standard) or mobile-bearing, with the mobile-bearing design increasing conformity and contact to decrease wear, but being more technically demanding and carrying a risk of bearing dislocation.
content.summary: Implants used in Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty (UKA) are primarily categorized as fixed-bearing, the historical standard of care, or mobile-bearing. Mobile-bearing implants utilize weightbearing through the meniscus to increase conformity and decrease wear, though they are technically demanding and susceptible to bearing dislocation.

content.title: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty Advantages
content.definition: Compared to TKA, UKA offers faster rehabilitation, less blood loss, less morbidity, and lower rates of PJI/wound complications, while compared to osteotomy, it provides quicker recovery, better cosmesis, a higher initial success rate, and is easier to convert to a TKA.
content.summary: The main advantages of Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty (UKA) compared to Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA) include faster rehabilitation, reduced pain and morbidity, and preservation of normal knee kinematics. Relative to osteotomy, UKA offers improved cosmesis, higher initial success rates, and easier conversion to a total knee arthroplasty if needed.

content.title: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty Indications
content.definition: UKA is indicated as an alternative to TKA or osteotomy for unicompartmental disease, traditionally reserved for older, low-demand, thin patients but with a growing trend to expand indications, while absolute contraindications include inflammatory arthritis, ACL deficiency (for lateral UKA), fixed deformities (>10 degrees varus or >5 degrees valgus), and severely restricted motion.
content.summary: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty is indicated as an alternative to total knee arthroplasty or osteotomy for patients presenting with isolated unicompartmental disease. Although traditionally reserved for older, lower-demand patients, indications are currently expanding to include younger patients and those with more moderate arthrosis.

content.title: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty Technique
content.definition: Key technical tips include avoiding overcorrection (undercorrecting the mechanical axis by 2 to 3 degrees), removing osteophytes, and resecting minimal bone, while ensuring a 1 to 5 degrees valgus alignment to prevent overload of the unresurfaced compartment.
content.summary: The surgical technique for Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty (UKA) focuses on precise component placement and minimal bone resection, emphasizing the removal of osteophytes and avoidance of extensive soft tissue releases. Key goals include undercorrecting the mechanical axis by 2-3 degrees and achieving a final alignment of 1-5 degrees of valgus to prevent excess load on the unresurfaced compartment.

content.title: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty Complications
content.definition: Aseptic loosening is the most common cause of early failure (25%-45% at 5 years), with other complications including tibial stress fractures (associated with high activity/weight and guide pin placement errors) and intra-operative fractures.
content.summary: Complications following unicompartmental knee arthroplasty (UKA) are adverse outcomes that can lead to failure, with aseptic loosening being the most common cause of early failure. Other significant issues include tibial stress fractures, associated with high activity or technical errors, and intra-operative fractures from forceful implant impacting.

search_keywords: Fixed bearing vs mobile bearing UKA

content.title: Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty Outcomes
content.definition: Fixed-bearing designs show good 10-year survivorship (87%-96%), while mobile-bearing designs show excellent results (93% at 15 years), though overall revision rates are worse than TKA, with the main cause of late failure being the progression of osteoarthritis in the unresurfaced compartments.
content.summary: The clinical outcomes of UKA are reported primarily through long-term survivorship, showing excellent 15-year results for mobile-bearing implants but variable performance for fixed-bearing designs. Late failure is typically attributed to the progression of osteoarthritis in the remaining compartments or component loosening, often requiring conversion to Total Knee Arthroplasty.

content.title: Knee Arthrodesis Summary
content.definition: Knee Arthrodesis is the fusion of the tibiofemoral joint, most commonly performed as a salvage procedure for failed total knee arthroplasty (TKA) or complete loss of the extensor mechanism, with the goal of achieving an optimal position of 5 degrees valgus and 0 degrees to 15 degrees of flexion.
content.summary: Knee arthrodesis is the surgical fusion of the tibiofemoral joint, most commonly performed as a salvage procedure for failed total knee arthroplasty (TKA) or complete loss of the extensor mechanism. The procedure aims to achieve a stable, ankylosed joint, ideally positioned in 5-8 degrees of valgus and 0-15 degrees of flexion.

content.title: Knee Arthrodesis Indications
content.definition: The indications for knee arthrodesis include salvage for failed TKA (most common), loss of the extensor mechanism, painful ankylosis after infection or trauma, neuropathic arthropathy, and tumor resection.
content.summary: Indications for knee arthrodesis are the specific clinical conditions that warrant surgical fusion of the tibiofemoral joint, most commonly including salvage for failed total knee arthroplasty (TKA) or complete loss of the extensor mechanism. Other indications include painful ankylosis resulting from infection or trauma, neuropathic arthropathy, and tumor resection.

content.title: Knee Arthrodesis Contraindications
content.definition: Contraindications include the absolute contraindication of active infection, and relative contraindications such as bilateral knee arthrodesis, contralateral leg amputation, significant bone loss, and ipsilateral hip or ankle degenerative joint disease (DJD).
content.summary: Contraindications are patient factors or concurrent conditions that advise against performing a specific medical treatment or surgical procedure. They are categorized as absolute, where the procedure is forbidden (e.g., active infection), or relative, where risks must be carefully weighed against potential benefits.

content.title: Knee Arthrodesis Technique
