content.definition: The technique aims for an optimal position of 5°–8° valgus and 0°–15° of flexion (with some limb shortening being advantageous), typically achieved through rigid fixation methods like an intramedullary rod (one- or two-part), external fixation (allowing compression), or plate fixation.
content.summary: Technique in knee arthrodesis involves positioning the limb optimally at 5-8° valgus, 0-15° flexion, and 0-10° external rotation, followed by rigid surgical fixation utilizing methods such as intramedullary rods, external fixation, or plating to achieve permanent tibiofemoral fusion.

content.title: Knee Arthrodesis Complications
content.definition: Complications include nonunion, infection, increased degenerative changes in adjacent joints (low back, ipsilateral hip, and contralateral knee), and fractures in the supracondylar femur or proximal tibia due to increased stress in those regions.
content.summary: Complications following knee arthrodesis include failure of bony union, infection, and secondary degenerative changes in the ipsilateral hip or contralateral knee due to altered biomechanics. Patients may also experience low back pain or stress fractures, such as supracondylar femur or proximal tibial metaphysis fractures, which occur from increased stress after fusion.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design History
content.definition: The design of total knee arthroplasty (TKA) implants evolved from 19th century soft-tissue interposition to Gunston's cemented surface arthroplasty in the 1960s, leading to modern designs categorized as unconstrained (CR/PS) or constrained (non-hinged/hinged).
content.summary: The history of knee replacement surgery evolved from 19th-century soft tissue interposition to the introduction of early hinged prostheses and cemented surface arthroplasty in the 1950s and 1960s. This progression culminated in the 1970s with the development of the total condylar prosthesis, marking the first design to resurface all three compartments of the knee joint.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design Concepts in Prosthetic Design
content.definition: Concepts in Prosthetic Design are governed by the need for femoral rollback (posterior translation during flexion to increase ROM) and constraint (the ability to provide stability against laxity, with modularity providing options to augment standard implants), while fixation can be cemented or cementless (biologic).
content.summary: Concepts in prosthetic design are the fundamental mechanical and structural principles, including femoral rollback, constraint, modularity, and fixation, that govern the function, stability, and construction of total knee arthroplasty implants. These principles determine the implant's interaction with native tissues and its ability to provide varus-valgus and flexion-extension stability.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design Cruciate-Retaining (CR) Design
content.definition: The Cruciate-Retaining (CR) Design is a minimally constrained prosthesis that requires an intact PCL for stability in flexion, is indicated for arthritis with minimal laxity (<10° varus), and offers potential advantages in proprioception and reduced bone cut, but risks accelerated polyethylene wear if the PCL is tight.
content.summary: The Cruciate-Retaining (CR) design is a minimally constrained total knee arthroplasty that depends on an intact Posterior Cruciate Ligament (PCL) to provide stability during flexion. This design allows the native PCL to promote femoral rollback, aiming to reproduce the kinematics of the normal knee joint.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design Posterior Stabilized (PS) Design
content.definition: The Posterior Stabilized (PS) Design is a slightly more constrained prosthesis that requires sacrifice of the PCL and utilizes a tibial post-and-femoral cam mechanism to promote rollback, making it ideal for cases with a deficient PCL or inflammatory arthritis (which risks late PCL rupture), but it is disadvantaged by the risks of cam jump/dislocation and patellar "clunk" syndrome.
content.summary: The Posterior Stabilized (PS) design is a cruciate-substituting knee prosthesis that requires sacrifice of the PCL and is slightly more constrained than cruciate-retaining designs. It features a femoral cam that engages a tibial polyethylene post during flexion, providing stability and inducing posterior femoral translation.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design Constrained Nonhinged Design
content.definition: The Constrained Nonhinged Design provides varus-valgus and rotational stability without an axle by using a large tibial post and deep femoral box, and is indicated for moderate bone loss or ligamentous deficiencies (LCL/MCL attenuation) where standard implants would be unstable, but it requires more femoral bone resection and increases the risk of aseptic loosening due to greater constraint.
content.summary: The constrained nonhinged design is a total knee prosthesis that achieves substantial varus/valgus and rotational stability without a fixed mechanical axle linking the femoral and tibial components. This stability is accomplished through the articulation of a large tibial post within a deep femoral box.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design Constrained Hinged Design
content.definition: The Constrained Hinged Design is the most constrained prosthesis, featuring linked femoral and tibial components (often a rotating hinge to decrease constraint), and is reserved for global ligamentous deficiency, massive bone loss (e.g., tumor resection), or gross flexion/extension mismatch.
content.summary: The constrained hinged design is the most restrictive class of knee prostheses, characterized by mechanically linked femoral and tibial components, often utilizing a rotating hinge mechanism. This design is primarily indicated for managing global ligamentous deficiency, massive bone loss, or severe instability patterns unsuitable for less constrained implants.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design Mobile Bearing Design
content.definition: The Mobile Bearing Design is a minimally constrained prosthesis in which the polyethylene can rotate on the tibial baseplate, is theoretically indicated for young, active patients due to reduced polyethylene wear, but carries the specific risk of bearing spin-out if the flexion gap is loose.
content.summary: A Mobile Bearing Design is a minimally constrained total knee arthroplasty characterized by a polyethylene insert that is free to rotate and/or translate on the metal tibial baseplate. This design aims to increase the contact area between components, thereby reducing contact pressures and minimizing polyethylene wear.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design All-polyethylene base plates
content.definition: All-polyethylene base plates are a solid block of polyethylene (no metal tray) that offer lower cost and potentially decreased rates of osteolysis, but sacrifice the modularity and flexibility of hybrid designs.
content.summary: An all-polyethylene tibial base plate is a non-modular component constructed as a solid block of polyethylene, distinguishing it from designs that feature a metal tray with a separate plastic insert. This design offers cost reduction and decreased rates of osteolysis while maintaining functional outcomes equivalent to modular systems.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design Highly Congruent Liners
content.definition: Highly Congruent Liners are designed with a deep medial compartment concavity to mimic native knee kinematics by creating a medial pivot, but studies currently show equivalent outcomes and survivorship in short and mid-term follow-up compared to standard liners.
content.summary: Highly congruent liners are polyethylene inserts designed with a medial compartment concavity, which allows the lateral compartment to translate between flexion and extension, thereby creating a medial pivot kinematic pattern that may better replicate native knee motion.

content.title: TKA Prosthesis Design Patient Specific Instrumentation
content.definition: Patient Specific Instrumentation (PSI) uses patient-specific imaging to create femoral and tibial cutting blocks, offering the advantage of less peri-operative instrumentation, but has shown no obvious benefit in postoperative alignment, cost, outcomes, or patient satisfaction compared to standard instrumentation.
content.summary: Patient Specific Instrumentation (PSI) involves using custom-made femoral and tibial cutting blocks for total knee arthroplasty, which are fabricated based on preoperative imaging specific to the patient's anatomy. The primary advantage is reducing the amount of standard instrumentation required during the perioperative process.

content.title: TKA Templating Introduction
content.definition: TKA templating is the preoperative process of anticipating implant size and position, allowing the surgeon to predict necessary component inventory and provide a reliable starting point, with accuracy reaching up to 92%-100% (± one size).
content.summary: Templating is the crucial preoperative process of analyzing radiographs to accurately anticipate the necessary size and optimal position of prosthetic implants before surgery. This planning step is important for predicting the resources needed in the operating room and ensuring a reliable starting point for surgical placement.

content.title: TKA Templating Radiographic Views
content.definition: Necessary radiographic views for templating include the AP weight-bearing and lateral views (most important), with the full-length hip-to-ankle AP view being optional but useful for ruling out extra-articular deformity and planning cuts relative to the mechanical axis.
content.summary: Radiographic views are the specific X-ray projections, primarily the AP weight-bearing and lateral views of the knee, that are necessary for accurately performing preoperative templating during total knee arthroplasty planning. Optional views, such as the full-length hip-to-ankle view, are useful for assessing overall mechanical axis and ruling out extra-articular deformity.

content.title: TKA Templating Radiographic Analysis
content.definition: Radiographic analysis is a multi-step process that uses the hip-to-ankle view to determine the mechanical axis, estimates the coronal deformity, sets the femoral resection angle and tibial bone cut (perpendicular to the mechanical axis), and assesses the tibial slope and patellar height/tracking to optimize component alignment.
content.summary: Radiographic analysis is a standardized, stepwise process utilizing preoperative radiographs to meticulously measure and evaluate a patient's anatomical parameters, such as the mechanical axis, coronal deformity, and bony defects. This evaluation is essential for accurately templating implant size, position, and planned bone resection angles prior to total joint arthroplasty.

content.title: TKA Templating Template the Femur
content.definition: To template the femur, the surgeon selects the size on the lateral radiograph to restore posterior condylar offset and avoid notching, then uses the AP view to verify medial/lateral positioning and ensure there is no significant overhang (which may require downsizing).
content.summary: Templating the femur is the preoperative process of selecting the appropriate femoral implant size, typically using the lateral radiograph to restore posterior condylar offset and avoid anterior notching. This selection is then assessed on the AP view to confirm optimal medial/lateral positioning and ensure no component overhang.

content.title: TKA Templating Template the Tibia
content.definition: To template the tibia, the appropriate size is chosen on the lateral view to match the native tibial slope and avoid overhang, followed by an AP check to ensure correct medial/lateral positioning and prevent any overhang that would necessitate downsizing.
content.summary: Templating the tibia involves utilizing radiographs to select the appropriate implant size, typically starting with the lateral view to match the native tibial slope and prevent posterior overhang. Subsequently, the AP view is used to confirm optimal medial/lateral positioning while ensuring the component does not excessively overhang the bone.

content.title: TKA Approaches Introduction
content.definition: The surgical approach for total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is determined by factors like surgeon preference, prior incisions, patient anatomy (obesity, patella baja, deformity), and requires careful incision planning to ensure adequate skin bridge and preserve blood supply.
content.summary: The Introduction to total knee arthroplasty focuses on the critical initial steps, including the planning of incisions based on patient factors and the selection of the appropriate surgical approach to achieve adequate joint exposure. This process is dictated by factors such as surgeon preference, prior incisions, and the complexity of the deformity, utilizing methods ranging from standard medial approaches to extensile exposures.

content.title: TKA Approaches Standard Medial Parapatellar Approach
content.definition: The Standard Medial Parapatellar Approach is the most common technique, favored for its familiarity and excellent exposure even in complex cases, but it risks lateral patellar subluxation and potential compromise of patellar circulation if combined with a lateral release.
content.summary: The Standard Medial Parapatellar Approach is the most commonly used surgical technique for primary total knee arthroplasty, typically performed through a straight midline incision. It is familiar to most surgeons and provides excellent exposure, although potential disadvantages include failure of the medial capsular repair or development of lateral patellar subluxation.

search_keywords: Tibial Tubercle Osteotomy for TKA access

content.title: TKA Approaches Lateral Parapatellar Approach
content.definition: The Lateral Parapatellar Approach is useful for correcting a fixed valgus deformity because it provides direct lateral access and preserves the patellar blood supply, but it is technically demanding and makes medial patellar eversion difficult, sometimes requiring a tibial tubercle osteotomy.
content.summary: The Lateral Parapatellar Approach is a technically demanding surgical exposure useful for addressing fixed valgus deformities and lateral contractures, as it allows direct access to the lateral knee and preserves the patellar blood supply. Its primary drawback is the difficulty encountered when attempting medial eversion of the patella.

content.title: TKA Approaches Midvastus
content.definition: The Midvastus Approach is similar to the medial parapatellar but spares the VMO (Vastus Medialis Obliquus) insertion on the quadriceps tendon, potentially allowing for quicker rehabilitation and improved patellar tracking, though it is less extensile and difficult in obese patients or those with restricted ROM.
content.summary: The midvastus approach is a surgical technique for total knee arthroplasty similar to the medial parapatellar method, which spares the vastus medialis insertion on the quadriceps tendon. This technique avoids disrupting the extensor mechanism, potentially allowing for accelerated rehabilitation and quicker recovery.

content.title: TKA Approaches Subvastus Approach
content.definition: The Subvastus Approach is the least extensile approach, preserving both patellar vascularity and the extensor mechanism entirely, but its limited exposure makes it unsuitable for revision TKA, large quadriceps, or obese patients.
content.summary: The Subvastus approach involves lifting the muscle belly of the vastus medialis off the intermuscular septum, thereby preserving patellar vascularity and leaving the extensor mechanism completely intact. While minimally invasive, this technique is considered the least extensile of the standard total knee arthroplasty approaches.

content.title: TKA Approaches Minimally Invasive Surgical Approach
content.definition: Minimally Invasive Surgical Approaches require special instruments and are technically demanding, with current data showing no clinically significant improvement in patient-reported outcomes or quadriceps strength, and they risk component malposition (warranting conversion to a standard approach if jig placement is compromised).
content.summary: A minimally invasive surgical approach is a technically demanding technique often requiring specialized instrumentation for limited exposure and implant insertion. Despite its focus on soft-tissue sparing, current data shows no clinically significant improvement in patient outcomes and it carries the risk of component malposition.

content.title: TKA Approaches Extensile Exposures
content.definition: Extensile Exposures are reserved for challenging or revision cases, ranging from a Quadriceps Snip (minimal long-term consequence) to a V-Y Turndown (excellent exposure, but risks extensor lag and requires post-operative immobilization) or a Tibial Tubercle Osteotomy (excellent exposure that avoids extensor lag, but carries risks of avulsion fracture and non-union).
content.summary:
Extensile exposures are advanced surgical approaches

—including the quadriceps snip, V-Y turndown, and tibial tubercle osteotomy

—used in total knee arthroplasty, typically for complex cases or revisions, to maximize visualization and facilitate management of difficult exposures.

content.title: TKA Approaches Bilateral Total Knee Arthroplasty
content.definition: Bilateral Total Knee Arthroplasty can be performed simultaneously (two surgeons), sequentially (one surgeon under one anesthetic), or staged (separate anesthetics), and the use of antibiotic-loaded bone cement (ALBC) reduces deep infection in revision TKA, with no proven risk of increasing aseptic loosening in primary cases.
content.summary: Bilateral Total Knee Arthroplasty involves replacing both knees, categorized by the timing of the procedure. It can be performed simultaneously by two surgeons, sequentially by one surgeon under a single anesthetic, or staged under separate anesthetics days to a year apart.

content.title: TKA Axial Alignment Introduction
content.definition: TKA Alignment is a crucial aspect of total knee arthroplasty (TKA), focused on technical goals such as restoring the zero degrees mechanical alignment, restoring the joint line, balancing ligaments, and preserving the normal Q angle to ensure optimal function and patellar tracking.
content.summary: This section details the necessary anatomical landmarks, technical goals, and preoperative considerations required for successful Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA). The primary objectives include restoring neutral mechanical alignment, achieving proper ligament balance, and ensuring joint line preservation for optimal prosthetic function.

content.title: TKA Axial Alignment Preoperative Evaluation
content.definition: Preoperative evaluation requires standing AP and lateral radiographs of the knee to assess joint space, subluxation, ligament insufficiency (gapping), and bone defects, while full-length standing films are indicated for patients with deformity, or very tall/short stature to accurately determine the valgus cut angle (VCA).
content.summary: The preoperative evaluation utilizes a series of specific radiographic views, such as standing AP/lateral knee films and full-length alignment studies, to comprehensively assess the patient's existing joint space, ligament stability, bone defects, and required valgus cut angles. This process is crucial for detailed surgical planning and achieving optimal mechanical alignment goals.

content.title: TKA Axial Alignment Femoral Alignment
content.definition: Femoral alignment utilizes the anatomic axis (AAF) for intramedullary guiding, with the target valgus cut angle (VCA) (typically 5 to 7 degrees from the AAF) determined by the difference between the AAF and the mechanical axis (MAF), the latter ensuring even load sharing across the prosthesis.
content.summary: Femoral alignment is determined by the relationship between the Anatomic Axis, which bisects the medullary canal, and the Mechanical Axis, connecting the center of the femoral head to the intercondylar notch. To restore the neutral mechanical axis and ensure even load sharing during arthroplasty, the surgical femoral cut is made using the Valgus Cut Angle, which represents the typical 5-7 degree difference between these two axes.

content.title: TKA Axial Alignment Tibial alignment
content.definition: Tibial alignment requires the proximal tibia to be cut perpendicular to the mechanical axis (MAT), which is usually coincident with the anatomic axis (AAT) and can be guided intramedullary, but requires an extramedullary guide if a tibial deformity exists that separates the MAT and AAT.
content.summary: Tibial alignment ensures the proximal tibial cut is perpendicular to the mechanical axis, which extends from the center of the proximal tibia to the center of the talus. While the anatomic and mechanical axes are usually coincident, requiring intramedullary guides, extramedullary guides are used when significant tibial deformity exists.

content.title: TKA Axial Alignment Patellofemoral Alignment
content.definition: Patellofemoral alignment focuses on preserving a normal Q angle (11 degrees plus or minus 7 degrees), as any increase in the Q angle (caused by common errors like internal rotation of the femoral/tibial prosthesis or medialization of the femoral component) is the most common complication of TKA and leads to increased lateral patellar subluxation forces, pain, and accelerated wear.
content.summary: Patellofemoral alignment describes the mechanical relationship between the patella and the femoral trochlea, where proper tracking is fundamentally dependent on preserving a normal Q angle. Maintaining this alignment is critical in total knee arthroplasty (TKA) because an increased Q angle elevates lateral subluxation forces, leading to pain, accelerated wear, and potential dislocation.

content.title: TKA Axial Alignment Joint Line Preservation
content.definition: Joint line preservation is achieved by resecting only the thickness of the bone and cartilage that will be replaced by the prosthesis, as elevating the joint line (greater than eight millimeters) causes mid-flexion instability and patellofemoral tracking problems, while lowering the joint line causes flexion instability and a lack of full extension.
content.summary: Joint Line Preservation is the technical goal in total knee arthroplasty to restore the natural height of the knee joint by ensuring the prosthesis thickness matches the amount of bone and cartilage removed. This is critical for maintaining appropriate ligament tension and function, thereby preventing instability and patellofemoral tracking issues.

content.title: TKA Coronal Plane Balancing Introduction
content.definition: Ligament balancing is essential in total knee arthroplasty to obtain an optimum outcome, requiring the release of tight ligaments on the concave side and potential tightening of stretched ligaments on the convex side in both flexion and extension, though kinematic alignment allows placing implants in mild varus or valgus based on native anatomy.
content.summary: The introduction defines the fundamental necessity in knee arthroplasty of balancing both medial and lateral ligaments, which may be stretched or contracted over time, across the coronal and sagittal planes for an optimum outcome. This process requires releasing the tight ligaments on the concave side of the deformity and tightening the stretched ligaments on the convex side, ensuring stability in both flexion and extension.

content.title: TKA Coronal Plane Balancing Varus Deformity
content.definition: Varus deformity, characterized by a tight medial (concave) side and a stretched lateral (convex) side, requires a step-wise medial release (starting with the deep MCL and osteophytes) and lateral tightening with an appropriately sized prosthesis to balance both flexion and extension gaps.
content.summary: A varus deformity is an angular misalignment of the knee characterized by tightness and contraction of the medial soft tissue structures (concave side) and corresponding stretching or laxity of the lateral ligaments (convex side). Correction during arthroplasty involves precise bone cuts and strategic release of the tight medial ligaments to achieve proper balance.

content.title: TKA Coronal Plane Balancing Valgus Deformity (lateral side is concave/tight)
content.definition: Valgus deformity (where the lateral side is concave/tight) requires a step-wise lateral release (targeting the posterolateral capsule, IT band, and popliteus) and medial tightening with the prosthesis, while carefully avoiding internal rotation of the femoral component to prevent patellofemoral maltracking.
content.summary: A valgus deformity of the knee is defined by the lateral structures being tight (concave side) and the medial structures being stretched (convex side). Surgical correction requires releasing the tight lateral ligaments and subsequently tightening the lax medial ligaments to achieve proper joint balance.

content.title: TKA Coronal Plane Balancing Flexion / Contracture Deformity
content.definition: Flexion contracture deformity is addressed by releasing the tight posterior (concave) side, which involves removing posterior femoral and tibial osteophytes, releasing the posterior capsule, and possibly releasing the gastrocnemius muscles (all releases performed at 90 degrees of flexion to protect the popliteal artery), and should never be addressed by removing more tibial bone (which raises the joint line and causes patella alta).
content.summary: A flexion/contracture deformity of the knee is a fixed limitation of full extension caused by tight posterior soft tissues and potentially posterior osteophytes, requiring sequential release of these structures to restore balance.

content.title: TKA Coronal Plane Balancing Complications
content.definition: The most significant complication is peroneal nerve palsy, which has the highest risk following the correction of both valgus and flexion contracture deformities, necessitating immediate knee flexion and observation, while coronal plane deformities greater than 20 degrees require an extra-articular osteotomy in addition to intra-articular balancing.
content.summary: Complications in knee surgery, particularly following the correction of severe valgus and flexion contracture deformities, include the significant risk of peroneal nerve palsy. Furthermore, large coronal plane deformities (>20 degrees) cannot be addressed by intra-articular balancing alone and may require an extra-articular osteotomy.

content.title: TKA Sagittal Plane Balancing Introduction
content.definition: The goal of TKA balancing is to obtain a flexion and extension gap that is equal through a complex process involving soft tissue release and bony resection, following the general rule to adjust the femur for asymmetric problems (distal cut affects extension gap, posterior cut affects flexion gap) and adjust the tibia for symmetric problems (tibia cut affects both gaps).
content.summary: The introduction outlines the fundamental objective of total knee arthroplasty balancing, which is to achieve a stable, symmetric tibiofemoral gap that is equal in both flexion and extension. Achieving this essential stability throughout the arc of motion typically requires precise adjustments to bony resection depths and appropriate soft tissue releases.

content.title: TKA Sagittal Plane Balancing Evaluation & Treatment
content.definition: Evaluation and treatment of knee gaps involves a systematic matrix: if the knee is tight in both flexion and extension, the treatment is to cut more proximal tibia; if loose in both, the solution is a thicker tibial polyethylene insert or metal augments; and discrepancies between the flexion and extension gaps require selective adjustments to the femoral bone cuts (e.g., cutting more distal femur for tight extension, upsizing the femoral component for loose flexion).
content.summary: Evaluation and Treatment in total knee arthroplasty involves systematically assessing the stability of the knee gaps in both flexion and extension using trial implants. This evaluation dictates specific corrective strategies, such as altering bone cuts, releasing soft tissues, or modifying component size, necessary to achieve optimal ligamentous balance throughout the arc of motion.

content.title: TKA Patellofemoral Alignment Introduction
content.definition: Abnormal patellar tracking is the most common complication of total knee arthroplasty (TKA), making the preservation of a normal Q angle the most important factor in surgical technique.
content.summary: Abnormal patellar tracking is the most common complication following Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA), fundamentally caused by an increase in the Q angle. This increase often results from surgical errors in component placement or rotation, leading to increased lateral subluxation forces, pain, accelerated wear, and potential dislocation.

content.title: TKA Patellofemoral Alignment Anatomy
content.definition: The Q angle is defined as the angle between the axis of the extensor mechanism (ASIS to patella center) and the axis of the patellar tendon (patella center to tibial tubercle), and its preservation is critical for proper patellar tracking.
content.summary: In the context of total knee arthroplasty (TKA), the crucial anatomical element is the Q angle, which measures the alignment of the extensor mechanism relative to the tibial tubercle. Achieving proper component placement relies on aligning the femoral prosthesis using reference axes, such as the transepicondylar and posterior condylar axes, to prevent increases in the Q angle and resultant patellar maltracking.

content.title: TKA Patellofemoral Alignment Imaging
content.definition: Component malrotation, which is the primary cause of patellar maltracking, is best diagnosed with a CT scan of the knee.
content.summary: Malrotation of prosthetic components following total knee arthroplasty, a key contributor to abnormal patellar tracking and increased Q angle, is best diagnosed using a CT scan of the knee.

content.title: TKA Patellofemoral Alignment Femoral Prosthesis
content.definition: Femoral component rotation must be set perpendicular to the transepicondylar axis to create a rectangular flexion gap, and internal rotation or medialization of the prosthesis must be strictly avoided as both actions increase the Q angle and the risk of maltracking.
content.summary: The femoral prosthesis is the implant component fixed to the distal femur during total knee arthroplasty, requiring precise rotational alignment, typically guided by the anteroposterior, transepicondylar, or posterior condylar axes. Errors in rotation, such as internal rotation or medialization, are known causes of increased Q angle and subsequent patellar maltracking.

content.title: TKA Patellofemoral Alignment Tibial Prosthesis
content.definition: The preferred rotation for the tibial component is neutral, centered over the medial third of the tibial tubercle, because any internal rotation or medialization of the component effectively increases the Q angle and the risk of maltracking.
content.summary: The tibial prosthesis is a component used in total knee arthroplasty whose optimal placement requires neutral rotation, typically achieved by centering it over the medial third of the tibial tubercle. Correct positioning is crucial, as internal rotation or medialization increases the Q angle, contributing to patellar maltracking complications.

content.title: TKA Patellofemoral Alignment Patellar Prosthesis
content.definition: The patellar prosthesis should be centered or medialized to help decrease the Q angle, as lateralization will increase the Q angle, and while resurfacing leads to less anterior knee pain and fewer revision rates compared to non-resurfacing, it is absolutely indicated only for inflammatory arthritis, patellar maltracking, or primary patellofemoral arthritis.
content.summary: The patellar prosthesis is the implant used to resurface the posterior surface of the patella during total knee arthroplasty (TKA). Proper placement, ideally centered or slightly medialized, is critical because lateralization increases the Q angle, leading to patellar maltracking complications.

content.title: TKA Kinematic Alignment Summary
content.definition: Kinematic Alignment (KA) is a surgical technique for total knee arthroplasty (TKA) that aims to restore the patient's native, pre-arthritic limb alignment and joint lines by making bone cuts to match the original femoral and tibial joint surfaces, thus acting as a ligament sparing approach that avoids unnecessary soft tissue releases.
content.summary: Kinematic Alignment (KA) is a surgical technique for total knee arthroplasty designed to restore the patient's unique pre-arthritic limb alignment, joint line obliquity, and biomechanics. It involves precise bone cuts calculated to resurface the femur and tibia based on the three native femoral kinematic axes, ensuring balanced ligament laxities without the need for soft tissue releases.

content.title: TKA Kinematic Alignment Anatomic Rationale
content.definition: The anatomic rationale for KA is that only about 15% of native knees have the 180° Hip-Knee-Ankle (HKA) axis and perpendicular joint line targeted by Mechanical Alignment (MA), meaning MA alters the natural anatomy and biomechanics of the majority (85%) of patients.
content.summary: The anatomic rationale for Kinematic Alignment recognizes that natural knee anatomy is individualized, extremely variable in HKA axis alignment and joint line obliquity across the population. This approach aims to restore the patient's native, pre-arthritic alignment because only a small minority of knees conform to the 180-degree HKA axis targeted by traditional mechanical alignment.

content.title: TKA Kinematic Alignment Foundational Principles
content.definition: The foundational principles of KA are based on the premise that TKA failure is not due to mild residual varus or valgus, but rather that arthritic deformity results from cartilage and tibial bone wear while ligaments and femoral bone are largely spared, allowing restoration of the three kinematic femoral axes to achieve a naturally "balanced" joint without releases.
content.summary: The foundational principles of Kinematic Alignment (KA) assert that arthritic deformity primarily results from cartilage loss and tibial bone wear, assuming minimal femoral bone wear and intact ligaments. These principles guide the surgical technique to restore the three native femoral kinematic axes and achieve appropriate knee balance solely by correcting tibial wear, thus avoiding soft tissue releases.

content.title: TKA Kinematic Alignment The Three Kinematic Axes of the Femur
content.definition: The three kinematic axes of the femur that define normal knee biomechanics are the flexion-extension axis, the internal-external rotation axis (a medial pivot facilitating lateral rollback), and the patellar tracking axis, all of which are either parallel or perpendicular to the native femoral joint line.
content.summary: The three kinematic axes of the femur—the flexion-extension axis, the internal-external rotation axis (medial pivot), and the patellar tracking axis—are fundamental anatomical references that define the normal biomechanics of the knee joint. These axes are crucial targets for restoration in kinematic alignment total knee arthroplasty, as they are inherently oriented parallel or perpendicular to the native femoral joint line.

content.title: TKA Kinematic Alignment Surgical Technique in Kinematic Alignment
content.definition: The surgical technique in KA involves computing the femoral resection targets based on the thickness of the component (component thickness - 1 mm saw cut - 2 mm cartilage) and then adjusting the tibial cut (varus/valgus and slope) to achieve a rectangular gap in extension and a trapezoidal gap in flexion (with progressive lateral laxity), without sacrificing or releasing the PCL or collateral ligaments.
content.summary: The surgical technique for Kinematic Alignment involves calculating femoral resection targets to resurface the femur and restore the native kinematic axes, followed by adjusting the tibial bone cut to optimize knee balance. This balancing process, which strictly avoids soft tissue releases, aims to create a rectangular, equally balanced gap in extension and a trapezoidal gap, looser laterally, in flexion.

content.title: TKA Kinematic Alignment Methods of Performing Kinematic Alignment
content.definition: Methods of performing Kinematic Alignment include the caliper-verified technique (which verifies cut thicknesses within plus or minus 0.5 mm of the KA target using minimal instrumentation), patient-specific cutting guides, navigation (which allows intra-operative adjustments to avoid recuts), and robotics (which also allows intra-operative adjustments), though the latter three are often expensive and closed-vendor systems.
content.summary: The methods used to perform kinematic alignment (KA) during total knee arthroplasty vary in complexity, ranging from the caliper-verified technique to advanced digital technologies. These approaches include patient-specific cutting guides, navigation systems, and robotics, all utilized to achieve the precise bone resections necessary to restore the patient's native, pre-arthritic kinematic axes.

content.title: TKA Kinematic Alignment Kinematic Alignment versus Mechanical Alignment
content.definition: Kinematic Alignment versus Mechanical Alignment differs significantly in that KA accepts a patient's residual varus or valgus alignment as representative of their native pre-arthritic state, which is not associated with hardware failure, and relies on internal rotation of the femoral component and a medial-pivot implant to optimize patellar tracking, rather than the 0° neutral alignment and 3° external rotation favored by MA.
content.summary: Kinematic Alignment (KA) is a total knee arthroplasty technique that aims to restore the patient's unique, pre-arthritic limb alignment and joint line obliquity based on the native kinematic axes of the femur. This differs fundamentally from traditional Mechanical Alignment (MA), which standardizes the knee to a neutral 180-degree Hip-Knee-Ankle axis, thereby altering the natural anatomy in the majority of patients.

content.title: TKA Kinematic Alignment Related Techniques
content.definition: Related Techniques include Restricted KA (which limits the magnitude of varus/valgus alignment with minor soft tissue releases used only to achieve pre-set limits), Functional Alignment (which adjusts bone resections intraoperatively for symmetric or asymmetric joint spaces without releases), and Patient-Specific Alignment (which makes a predetermined tibial cut followed by femoral gap balancing without releases).
content.summary: Related Techniques are modifications of Kinematic Alignment (KA) that adjust the surgical methodology, including Restricted KA which places limits on component positioning and Functional or Patient-Specific Alignment, which prioritize intraoperative soft tissue balance by adjusting bony resections.

content.title: TKA in Patella Baja (Prior HTO) Summary
content.definition: TKA in Patella Baja is a complication that occurs when performing a total knee arthroplasty (TKA) in a patient whose patella has been lowered relative to its normal position, often caused by a prior high tibial osteotomy (HTO).
content.summary: TKA in Patella Baja describes performing total knee arthroplasty in a patient whose patella is positioned abnormally low (patella infera), often a consequence of previous proximal tibial osteotomy. This condition is diagnosed radiographically by an Insall-Salvati ratio less than 0.8 and requires specialized surgical techniques, such as joint line modification or tibial tubercle transfer, during the arthroplasty.

content.title: TKA in Patella Baja (Prior HTO) Etiology
content.definition: The etiology of acquired patella baja is most commonly a proximal tibial osteotomy (the most frequent complication of opening-wedge HTO), where patellar tendon shortening or scarring lowers the patella, but it can also be caused by tibial tubercle transfers, trauma, or technical error during a primary TKA.
content.summary: The etiology of acquired patella baja is primarily linked to previous surgical interventions or trauma near the knee joint. Common causes include proximal tibial osteotomy, tibial tubercle transfer, trauma to the proximal tibia, and technical errors during total knee arthroplasty resulting in joint line elevation.

content.title: TKA in Patella Baja (Prior HTO) Presentation
content.definition: The presentation is characterized by anterior impingement knee pain and knee stiffness, with a physical exam revealing a mechanical block to full flexion due to patellar impingement on the tibia.
content.summary: Patients typically present with anterior impingement knee pain and stiffness, exhibiting a mechanical block or limited flexion upon physical examination. Radiographic diagnosis confirms patella baja, showing distal patellar positioning relative to the trochlear groove, quantified by an Insall-Salvati ratio of less than 0.8.

content.title: TKA in Patella Baja (Prior HTO) Imaging
content.definition: Imaging relies on the lateral view of the knee in 30 degrees of flexion to measure the Insall-Salvati ratio, with a ratio of <0.8 being consistent with patella baja.
content.summary: Imaging for patella baja typically involves AP and lateral radiographs of the knee, with the lateral view used to measure the Insall-Salvati ratio. A ratio of less than 0.8 is diagnostic for patella baja, indicating distal positioning of the patella relative to the trochlear groove.

content.title: TKA in Patella Baja (Prior HTO) Treatment
content.definition: Treatment for patella baja in the context of TKA is primarily operative (TKA itself) for severe impingement in older, arthritic patients, as nonoperative management is reserved for mild symptoms in younger patients.
content.summary: Treatment for TKA in patella baja ranges from nonoperative measures like physical therapy for mild symptoms to total knee arthroplasty (TKA) for severe impingement in older patients. Operative strategies during TKA focus on correcting the deformity by placing the patellar component superiorly, lowering the joint line, or potentially performing a tibial tubercle transfer.

content.title: TKA in Patella Baja (Prior HTO) Techniques
content.definition: Techniques to address patella baja during TKA include: placing the patellar component superiorly (for mild cases), lowering the joint line (via distal femoral augmentation or lowering the tibial cut) for moderate cases, or performing a cephalad tibial tubercle transfer (for moderate cases, though healing is unpredictable), with patellectomy being reserved for severe cases.
content.summary: Techniques are the specialized surgical methods employed during total knee arthroplasty to manage patella baja, aiming to restore proper biomechanics and prevent anterior impingement. These specific strategies include placing the patellar component superiorly, lowering the joint line, or performing a tibial tubercle transfer.

content.title: TKA Postoperative Inpatient Management Introduction
content.definition: Post-operative rehabilitation is a coordinated, multi-disciplinary effort involving the orthopedic team and therapists, broken down into distinct phases including inpatient management and addressing postoperative anemia.
content.summary: The Introduction outlines the coordinated, multidisciplinary framework required for patient rehabilitation, emphasizing systematic pain management (multimodal therapy), early physical therapy, and achieving specific functional and mobility goals necessary for safe discharge.

content.title: TKA Postoperative Inpatient Management Inpatient Management
content.definition: Inpatient management focuses on multimodal pain control (using preoperative NSAIDs/opioids, intraoperative regional blocks/periarticular injections, and postoperative oral regimens with NSAIDs/opioids/gabapentin) and initiating physical therapy on the day of surgery to achieve mobility goals like 90° knee flexion and ambulating 75–100 feet to meet discharge criteria.
content.summary: Inpatient management is the initial, coordinated phase of post-operative care involving a multidisciplinary team dedicated to optimizing patient recovery within the hospital setting. Key components include multimodal pain management, early initiation of physical therapy, and meeting functional milestones necessary for safe discharge, such as specific range of motion and ambulation requirements.

content.title: TKA Postoperative Inpatient Management Post-Operative Anemia
content.definition: Post-operative anemia is related to blood loss, which is minimized by intraoperative tourniquet use, while early discharge to rehab is associated with improved outcomes, and discharge criteria require the patient to achieve specific mobility milestones (e.g., 80-90 degrees active knee flexion and stair climbing).
content.summary: Post-operative anemia is defined as a reduction in the red blood cell count, hemoglobin, or hematocrit that occurs following a surgical procedure. This condition commonly results from intraoperative blood loss, affecting recovery time and potentially requiring clinical management.

hierarchy.subtopic: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management

content.title: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management Introduction
content.definition: Rehabilitation is a coordinated, multidisciplinary effort encompassing multiple phases of care, including inpatient extended care and outpatient care.
content.summary: Rehabilitation following orthopedic surgery is a structured, multidisciplinary process requiring coordination between the orthopedic surgeon, physical therapists, and nursing staff. Care is generally divided into inpatient extended care and outpatient phases, utilizing various modalities to restore function and guide the patient's safe return to daily and recreational activities.

references.chapter: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management

hierarchy.subtopic: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management

content.title: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management Inpatient Extended Care (Rehab)
content.definition: Inpatient extended care, which is associated with improved outcomes when initiated early after hospital discharge, requires patients to meet mobility and independence criteria that are similar to hospital discharge criteria for eventual return home.
content.summary: Inpatient extended care, often referred to as rehabilitation or SNF, is a critical post-acute phase of recovery requiring coordinated efforts from a multidisciplinary team, including therapists and nurses. Earlier discharge from the acute hospital to this setting has been associated with improved patient outcomes.

references.chapter: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management

hierarchy.subtopic: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management

content.title: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management Outpatient Care
content.definition: Outpatient care typically involves physical therapy 2-3 times per week, focusing on low-impact closed-chain exercises (like biking or elliptical) and modalities such as aquatic therapy or NMES, with driving generally recommended after 4 weeks for a right total knee.
content.summary: Outpatient care constitutes a key phase of rehabilitation following discharge from inpatient or extended care, characterized by structured physical therapy sessions typically performed 2-3 times per week. This care focuses on advancing strength and function using specific exercises and modalities to guide the patient's return to unassisted mobility and daily activities.

references.chapter: TKA Postoperative Rehabilitation and Outpatient Management

content.title: TKA Periprosthetic Fracture Summary
content.definition: TKA Periprosthetic Fractures are a complication of knee arthroplasty that may involve the distal femur, proximal tibia, or patella, diagnosed with plain radiographs and treated nonoperatively or operatively based on fracture location, implant stability, bone stock, and patient comorbidities.
content.summary: TKA Periprosthetic Fractures are a serious complication of total knee arthroplasty that may involve the distal femur, proximal tibia, or patella. Management is individualized, ranging from nonoperative bracing to complex operative procedures, based on fracture stability, implant integrity, available bone stock, and patient comorbidities.

content.title: TKA Periprosthetic Fracture Epidemiology
content.definition: The epidemiology shows these fractures affect the distal femur, proximal tibia, and patella, with intraoperative fractures most commonly involving the medial femoral condyle, and overall fracture risk increased by poor bone quality, rheumatoid arthritis, and neurological disorders like Parkinson's disease.
content.summary: The epidemiology of TKA periprosthetic fractures encompasses the frequency (incidence rates), anatomical distribution (distal femur, proximal tibia, patella), timing of occurrence, and the various patient and procedure-related risk factors associated with their development.

content.title: TKA Periprosthetic Fracture Etiology
content.definition: The etiology includes intraoperative fractures (like the common medial femoral condyle break) and postoperative fractures (such as those at the stem tip), with risk factors including stress-shielding, screw holes, local osteolysis, and technical factors like stiffness or neurological disorders.
content.summary: Etiology refers to the underlying causes and predisposing conditions that lead to TKA periprosthetic fractures. These factors are categorized by timing (intraoperative or postoperative) and include general patient risks, such as poor bone quality and underlying neurological disorders, alongside mechanical or surgical risks specific to the component location.

content.title: TKA Periprosthetic Fracture Distal Femur Periprosthetic Fractures
content.definition: Distal Femur Periprosthetic Fractures have an incidence of 0.3%-2.5%, are classified primarily by Lewis and Rorabeck (Types I-III) or Su and Associates (Types I-III) based on displacement and component stability/location, and are treated with nonoperative bracing for stable/non-displaced fractures or retrograde intramedullary nailing/ORIF for stable/displaced fractures, with long-stem revision or distal femoral replacement for loose implants.
content.summary: Distal femur periprosthetic fractures are complications of total knee arthroplasty (TKA), occurring in the distal portion of the femur, often involving the bone surrounding the existing femoral prosthetic component. These fractures are typically classified based on displacement, comminution, and the stability of the implant using systems like the Lewis and Rorabeck classification.

content.title: TKA Periprosthetic Fracture Tibial Periprosthetic Fractures
content.definition: Tibial Periprosthetic Fractures have an incidence of 0.4% - 1.7%, are classified by Felix and Associates (Types I-IV) based on location and relation to the stem, and are treated with nonoperative casting/bracing for nondisplaced or stable fractures, ORIF for unstable fractures with a stable prosthesis, or long-stem revision for displaced fractures with a loose tibial component.
content.summary: Tibial periprosthetic fractures are a specific complication of total knee arthroplasty (TKA) involving a fracture of the proximal tibia. These fractures are classified based on their location relative to the prosthesis, occurring at the tibial plateau, adjacent to the stem, or in the tibial shaft distal to the component.

content.title: TKA Periprosthetic Fracture Patellar Periprosthetic Fractures
content.definition: Patellar Periprosthetic Fractures have a high incidence in resurfaced patellae (0.2% - 21%) but are rare in unresurfaced patellae (0.05%), are classified by Goldberg or Ortiguera and Berry based on extensor mechanism involvement and component stability, and are treated nonoperatively for non-displaced fractures with an intact extensor mechanism or operatively (ORIF, partial patellectomy, or total patellectomy) for loose components or extensor mechanism disruption.
content.summary: Patellar periprosthetic fractures are a complication following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) that specifically involves the patella, with incidence significantly higher in resurfaced knees. These fractures are frequently associated with risk factors such as patellar osteonecrosis, asymmetric patellar resection, or specific implant design characteristics.

content.title: TKA Extensor Mechanism Rupture Summary
content.definition: TKA Extensor Mechanism Ruptures are traumatic periprosthetic injuries (patellar or quadriceps tendon injuries) following a TKA, diagnosed clinically by the patient's inability to perform a straight leg raise, with radiographs potentially showing patella alta or patella baja.
content.summary: TKA Extensor Mechanism Ruptures are traumatic periprosthetic injuries following total knee arthroplasty that involve tears of the patellar tendon or quadriceps tendon. Diagnosis is suggested by the clinical inability to perform a straight leg raise and confirmed radiographically, typically requiring surgical treatment via repair or reconstruction.

content.title: TKA Extensor Mechanism Rupture Epidemiology
content.definition: With an incidence ranging from 0.17 percent to 2.5 percent.
content.summary: The epidemiology of TKA Extensor Mechanism Ruptures focuses on the frequency of this traumatic periprosthetic injury, reporting an incidence rate ranging from 0.17% to 2.5%.

search_keywords: Patellar tendon rupture post total knee arthroplasty

content.title: TKA Extensor Mechanism Rupture Etiology
content.definition: The etiology involves intraoperative (tibial tubercle avulsion) or postoperative causes (manipulation, impingement, trauma), which result in either a patellar or quadriceps tendon injury.
content.summary: The etiology of TKA extensor mechanism ruptures is categorized into intraoperative causes, typically avulsion from the tibial tubercle, and postoperative factors. Postoperative causes commonly include direct trauma, manipulation of the knee, or impingement by the prosthetic components.

content.title: TKA Extensor Mechanism Rupture Presentation
content.definition: The presentation is characterized by complaints of knee pain and weakness and the key diagnostic finding on physical exam is the inability to perform a straight leg raise.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of TKA extensor mechanism rupture involves acute knee pain and weakness, characterized by the inability of the patient to perform a straight leg raise. Radiographic findings frequently show evidence of abnormal patellar positioning, such as patella alta or patella baja.

content.title: TKA Extensor Mechanism Rupture Imaging
content.definition: Imaging relies on radiographs to confirm the diagnosis and check for associated findings like patella alta or patella baja.
content.summary: Imaging, typically utilizing standard radiographs, is performed during the workup of TKA extensor mechanism ruptures to assess for characteristic displacement of the patella, which may manifest as patella alta or patella baja.

content.title: TKA Extensor Mechanism Rupture Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is typically surgical repair (direct suture/augmentation for complete lacerations with adequate bone stock) or reconstruction (using allograft or synthetic mesh for chronic injuries or deficient soft tissues), with nonoperative management (immobilizer) reserved for partial quadriceps tendon ruptures or patellar fractures where the extensor mechanism remains intact.
content.summary: Management for TKA extensor mechanism ruptures is typically surgical, ranging from direct repair to complex reconstruction, tailored to the chronicity of the injury and the adequacy of available patellar bone stock. Nonoperative care with knee immobilization is reserved strictly for partial tendon ruptures or patellar fractures where the extensor mechanism remains functionally intact.

content.title: TKA Stiffness Summary
content.definition: TKA Stiffness is a common complication resulting in poor functional outcomes, diagnosed clinically when flexion is less than 90° or a flexion contracture is 10°-15°, and treated by manipulation under anesthesia (MUA) within the first 12 weeks or arthroscopic lysis for late stiffness.
content.summary:
Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA) stiffness is a common complication resulting in poor functional outcomes, typically diagnosed clinically when the patient has less than 90 degrees of knee flexion or a flexion contracture of 10

–15 degrees.

content.title: TKA Stiffness Epidemiology
content.definition: With an incidence of 1.3%-12%, the most important risk factor for TKA stiffness is poor preoperative range of motion, along with factors like younger age, smoking, and technical errors such as malrotation or tight gaps. TKA stiffness is multifactorial, caused by preoperative patient factors (poor ROM, low pain tolerance, smoking), technical errors (overstuffing the patellofemoral joint, malrotation, tight flexion/extension gaps), and postoperative factors (delayed rehabilitation, infection, HO, or hamstring spasms).
content.summary: The incidence of TKA Stiffness ranges from 1.3% to 12% following total knee arthroplasty. Risk factors for this complication are diverse, including preoperative issues like poor range of motion, technical errors such as malrotation, and postoperative complications like infection or delayed rehabilitation.

content.title: TKA Stiffness Presentation
content.definition: Stiffness presents clinically with symptoms such as difficulty kneeling, and objectively as a significant loss of motion, defined as a flexion contracture of 10°-15° or an absolute flexion less than 90°.
content.summary: The presentation of TKA stiffness typically involves patient complaints of difficulty kneeling. Physical examination confirms the diagnosis by revealing restricted range of motion, defined as knee flexion measuring less than 90 degrees or a persistent flexion contracture of 10 to 15 degrees.

content.title: TKA Stiffness Imaging
content.definition: Both plain radiographs and CT scans are used primarily to rule out a mechanical cause for the stiffness, such as prosthesis malposition or malalignment.
content.summary: Imaging, typically utilizing radiographs and CT scans, is an important step in the workup of TKA stiffness; its primary purpose is to rule out technical issues like prosthesis malposition or mechanical misalignment as the cause of restricted range of motion.

content.title: TKA Stiffness Studies
content.definition: The only lab studies indicated are serum ESR and CRP to rule out a prosthetic joint infection as an underlying cause of the stiffness.
content.summary: In the diagnostic workup for TKA stiffness, "Studies" primarily refers to serum laboratory tests, such as erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). These labs are critical for ruling out periprosthetic joint infection, which is a major underlying cause of postoperative stiffness that must be excluded.

content.title: TKA Stiffness Treatment
content.definition: Acute stiffness (flexion < 90° within the first 12 weeks) is treated by manipulation under anesthesia (MUA), while persistent late stiffness is managed surgically with arthroscopic lysis of adhesions or revision TKA if a technical cause is identified.
content.summary: Treatment for TKA stiffness is guided by the timing of onset and severity of restricted range of motion, typically starting with manipulation under anesthesia (MUA) if performed within 12 weeks of surgery. For persistent late stiffness or cases where technical errors are identifiable, surgical options such as arthroscopic lysis of adhesions or revision total knee arthroplasty are utilized.

content.title: TKA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Summary
content.definition: TKA Vascular Injury and Bleeding are a rare but potentially devastating complication, most commonly caused by direct injury to the popliteal artery during surgery from errant posterior retractor placement or excessive hyperextension, with an incidence of 0.017%-0.2%.
content.summary: TKA Vascular Injury and Bleeding is a rare but potentially devastating complication of total knee arthroplasty, typically resulting from direct trauma to the popliteal artery during the procedure. Diagnosis is based on brisk intraoperative bleeding or postoperative ischemia, necessitating immediate recognition, hemostasis, and prompt vascular surgery consultation.

content.title: TKA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Epidemiology
content.definition: The epidemiology shows a low incidence (0.017%-0.2%), with risk factors primarily including revision surgery and peripheral vascular disease.
content.summary: The epidemiology of TKA vascular injury indicates its low incidence, estimated between 0.017% and 0.2%. Primary risk factors include revision surgery and peripheral vascular disease, while the etiology often involves direct laceration, blunt trauma from retractor placement, or thrombosis.

content.title: TKA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Etiology
content.definition: The etiology involves direct laceration during sharp posterior dissection, blunt trauma from retractor placement (especially if placed lateral to the PCL or more than 1 cm deep), or thrombosis, with injury risk increased by excessive hyperextension during bone cuts.
content.summary: Etiology is the study or determination of the underlying cause or causes responsible for a disease, medical condition, or complication. In the context of vascular injury during total knee arthroplasty, the etiologies include direct laceration, blunt trauma from retractor placement, and thrombosis of the popliteal artery.

content.title: TKA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Anatomy
content.definition: The popliteal artery is the vessel most at risk, as it is anchored by the adductor magnus (proximally) and the soleus tendon (distally), runs posterior to the knee capsule, and is a lateral structure at the level of the joint line, dividing distally into the anterior and posterior tibial arteries.
content.summary: The key anatomy related to vascular injury during TKA is the popliteal artery, which is a continuation of the superficial femoral artery that courses immediately posterior to the knee capsule and the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus. Understanding its lateral position at the joint line is critical, as excessive hyperextension brings the vessel closer to instruments, increasing the risk of laceration or thrombosis.

content.title: TKA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Presentation
content.definition: The presentation is characterized by the intraoperative findings of brisk bleeding and loss of distal pulses, or the postoperative signs of ischemia, sensory changes, loss of pulses, and skin mottling.
content.summary: The presentation of a vascular injury during or following TKA includes immediate intraoperative findings like brisk bleeding and loss of pulses, as well as delayed signs of acute limb ischemia postoperatively, such as skin mottling, sensory changes, and loss of distal pulses.

content.title: TKA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Imaging
content.definition: Intraoperative imaging (including angiogram) is performed under the guidance of an immediate vascular surgery consultation when brisk intraoperative bleeding or loss of distal pulses occurs. Postoperative imaging relies on a CT angiogram when signs of ischemia, loss of pulses, skin mottling, or sensory changes suggest a vascular injury.
content.summary: Imaging studies are essential for diagnosing vascular injury following TKA, utilizing intraoperative angiography to confirm active bleeding or pulse loss, and postoperative CT angiography to evaluate signs of distal ischemia or absent pulses.

content.title: TKA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Treatment
content.definition: Treatment requires immediate vascular surgery consultation upon suspicion of injury, with potential interventions including stent placement, bypass, endarterectomy, or prophylactic fasciotomy to avoid reperfusion compartment syndrome.
content.summary: Treatment for vascular injury during Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA) hinges on immediate recognition of the injured vessel, achieving hemostasis, and obtaining prompt vascular surgery consultation. Definitive repair modalities typically include stent placement, bypass grafting, or endarterectomy, sometimes requiring a prophylactic fasciotomy.

content.title: TKA Vascular Injury & Bleeding Complications
content.definition: Complications include compartment syndrome, amputation, and increased mortality and socioeconomic consequences (increased length of stay and cost).
content.summary: Complications are adverse events or secondary conditions that arise during the course of a disease or procedure, representing negative outcomes resulting from the primary injury or treatment. Following TKA vascular injury, these potentially devastating outcomes include compartment syndrome, amputation, and mortality, along with increased socioeconomic burdens.

content.title: TKA Peroneal Nerve Palsy Neurological Injury (Peroneal Nerve)
content.definition: TKA Peroneal Nerve Palsy is a rare (0.3%-2%) but potentially devastating complication most commonly seen after correcting valgus and/or flexion deformities, diagnosed clinically by decreased sensation and weakness in ankle dorsiflexion, with treatment consisting of immediate knee flexion, observation, and an AFO for persistent foot drop.
content.summary: Peroneal nerve injury is an uncommon (0.3%-2%) complication of total knee arthroplasty (TKA), frequently associated with the surgical correction of pre-existing valgus or flexion deformities. It is diagnosed clinically by decreased sensation in the peroneal distribution accompanied by weakness of ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop).

content.title: TKA Wound Complications Summary
content.definition: TKA Wound Complications result from systemic factors (like diabetes, obesity, smoking, and poor nutrition), local issues (prior incisions, knee deformity, poor blood supply), or technical/postoperative factors (large flaps, long tourniquet time, hematoma), and are diagnosed clinically by the presence of incisional breakdown or dehiscence.
content.summary: Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA) wound complications are characterized by incisional breakdown or dehiscence following surgery, often resulting from underlying patient systemic factors (such as diabetes or vascular disease), local knee characteristics, or technical issues. Diagnosis is clinical, and treatment depends primarily on the size and thickness of the wound and the limb's vascular status.

content.title: TKA Wound Complications Epidemiology
content.definition: The primary risk factors for wound complications include systemic factors such as diabetes mellitus, vascular disease, and morbid obesity (BMI > 40), as well as local surgical factors like crossing previous incisions at sharp angles or short incisions requiring excessive skin traction.
content.summary: The epidemiology of TKA wound complications focuses on identifying patient, local, and surgical risk factors that increase the incidence of incisional breakdown. Key determinants include systemic conditions like diabetes and morbid obesity, local factors such as poor blood supply, and surgical technical issues like large subcutaneous skin flaps or long tourniquet time.

content.title: TKA Wound Complications Presentation
content.definition: The presentation includes a history of a multiply-operated knee or prior infection, and physical exam findings of wound breakdown/dehiscence, erythema, warmth, drainage lasting beyond 7 days, or peri-incisional eschar.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of a TKA wound complication includes relevant history (e.g., multiply operated knee, prior infection) and symptoms like persistent knee pain or drainage lasting beyond the early postoperative period (7 days). Physical examination reveals localized signs such as wound breakdown, erythema, warmth, drainage, or peri-incisional eschar.

content.title: TKA Wound Complications Imaging
content.definition: Imaging is primarily used to rule out deep infection via radiographs (looking for bone resorption) or radionuclide bone scans if serology is inconclusive, and angiography is reserved for assessing vascular flow if a gastrocnemius flap is planned.
content.summary: Imaging, including radiographs and bone scans, is employed during the diagnosis of TKA wound complications primarily to rule out or investigate suspected deep infection. Angiography is reserved for cases requiring advanced surgical treatment, such as rotational flaps, to confirm adequate blood flow through the sural arteries.

content.title: TKA Wound Complications Treatment
content.definition: Treatment depends on the wound's size and depth: small, superficial wounds may be managed with local wound care; however, full-thickness necrosis requires operative debridement and coverage using a rotational flap, such as the medial gastrocnemius flap for anterior and medial defects.
content.summary: Treatment for total knee arthroplasty wound complications is dictated by the wound's size, depth, and the limb's vascular status. Management strategies range from nonoperative local wound care for small, superficial defects to operative debridement and coverage with rotational flaps for cases involving full-thickness necrosis.

content.title: TKA Heterotopic Ossification Summary
content.definition: TKA Heterotopic Ossification (HO) is the complication of bone formation in the soft tissues after TKA, with the diagnosis confirmed by plain radiographs, and treatment being observation in most cases as it rarely causes functional problems.
content.summary: TKA Heterotopic Ossification (HO) is a complication following total knee arthroplasty characterized by the formation of bone in the soft tissues adjacent to the joint. Although diagnosed radiographically, HO rarely impacts clinical outcomes and is usually managed with observation, with surgical excision reserved for mechanical symptoms such as quadriceps snapping or patellofemoral instability.

search_keywords: Risk factors for HO after total knee replacement

content.title: TKA Heterotopic Ossification Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of HO ranges from 1% to 42%, but less than 1% of cases impact the clinical outcome, with risk factors including hypertrophic arthrosis, male gender, obesity, periosteal injury, and post-operative knee effusion or forced manipulation.
content.summary: The epidemiology of TKA Heterotopic Ossification (HO) shows a wide incidence range (1% to 42%), though less than 1% of cases significantly impact clinical outcome. Risk factors include male gender, obesity, hypertrophic arthrosis, and certain post-operative complications such as hematoma or forced manipulation for restricted motion.

content.title: TKA Heterotopic Ossification Etiology
content.definition: The etiology is defined as any bone formation in the soft tissues (excluding periosteum) that develops after surgery, with or without a precipitating event, and is distinguished from myositis ossificans (which develops after traumatic muscle injury).
content.summary: Heterotopic Ossification (HO) after TKA is characterized by the formation of new bone tissue within the soft tissues, outside of the periosteum, following the surgical procedure. This development may occur with or without a specific precipitating event and is distinct from myositis ossificans, which arises from traumatic muscle injury.

content.title: TKA Heterotopic Ossification Classification
content.definition: The Furia and Pellegrini classification system categorizes HO based on the size of the bone island and its contiguity with the anterior distal femur (Class I for localized islands, Class II for contiguous bone).
content.summary: The Furia and Pellegrini classification system is used to categorize heterotopic ossification (HO) following TKA, differentiating the lesions based on whether the bone island is localized to the suprapatellar soft tissues or is contiguous with the anterior distal femur, and further by size (less than or greater than 5 cm).

search_keywords: Loss of knee motion after TKA

content.title: TKA Heterotopic Ossification Presentation
content.definition: The presentation is rarely symptomatic, but when it is, it can include loss of motion, pain, quadriceps muscle snapping, and patellofemoral instability.
content.summary: TKA Heterotopic Ossification is often asymptomatic, but patients may rarely present with symptoms such as pain, loss of motion, quadriceps snapping, or patellofemoral instability. The condition is definitively diagnosed via radiographs showing ossification along the anterior distal femur and within the quadriceps mechanism, usually appearing 3 to 4 weeks post-operatively.

content.title: TKA Heterotopic Ossification Imaging
content.definition: Radiographic evaluation (AP/lateral views) shows ossification along the anterior distal aspect of the femur and within the quadriceps mechanism, typically becoming visible 3-4 weeks post-operatively and maturing within 1-2 years.
content.summary: Radiographic evaluation is the primary imaging modality used for diagnosing TKA Heterotopic Ossification, utilizing AP and lateral views of the knee. Findings include ossification along the anterior distal aspect of the femur and within the quadriceps mechanism, typically becoming visible 3 to 4 weeks post-operatively.

content.title: TKA Heterotopic Ossification Prevention
content.definition: Prevention is generally reserved for high-risk patients undergoing THA (using external beam radiation or NSAIDs), as there is no specific literature recommending these prophylaxis therapies for TKA patients.
content.summary: Prevention strategies for heterotopic ossification, such as external beam radiation therapy and pharmaceutical prophylaxis with NSAIDs, are typically employed for high-risk patients undergoing Total Hip Arthroplasty. Currently, there is insufficient literature to recommend these specific prophylactic measures for patients receiving Total Knee Arthroplasty.

content.title: TKA Heterotopic Ossification Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is observation for the majority of cases since HO rarely impacts the clinical outcome, but surgical excision is indicated when the ossification causes functional issues like quadriceps muscle snapping, patellofemoral tracking difficulties, or patellofemoral instability.
content.summary: Treatment for TKA Heterotopic Ossification (HO) is typically nonoperative observation, as the condition rarely impacts the clinical outcome or function of the knee. Surgical excision of the HO is indicated only in symptomatic cases exhibiting functional difficulties, such as quadriceps muscle snapping, patellofemoral tracking difficulties, or instability.

content.title: TKA Instability Summary
content.definition: TKA Instability is a common cause of early failure, accounting for 10%-20% of all revisions following total knee arthroplasty, and is diagnosed clinically by varus/valgus thrust or posterior sag, with treatment depending on the direction and severity of the instability relative to the prosthesis type.
content.summary: TKA Instability is a common cause of early failure following total knee arthroplasty, accounting for 10% to 20% of revisions. Diagnosis relies on clinical signs such as varus or valgus thrust during ambulation, positive posterior sag, and overall joint laxity, with treatment strategies varying based on the instability type and existing prosthesis.

content.title: TKA Instability Epidemiology
content.definition: TKA instability is a common cause of early failure, accounting for 10% to 20% of all revisions following total knee arthroplasty.
content.summary: Epidemiology describes the incidence and impact of TKA instability, which is a common cause of early failure following total knee arthroplasty. Specifically, instability accounts for approximately 10-20% of all subsequent revision procedures.

content.title: TKA Instability Etiology
content.definition: TKA instability is categorized into five distinct mechanical types: extension (varus-valgus), flexion (anteroposterior), mid-flexion, genu recurvatum, and global instability.
content.summary: The etiology of TKA instability is categorized into distinct mechanistic types, including extension (varus-valgus), flexion (anteroposterior), and mid-flexion patterns. These instabilities typically arise from technical surgical errors, such as improper component sizing, excessive bone resection leading to gap mismatch, or insufficient management of soft-tissue and ligamentous deformities.

content.title: TKA Instability Presentation
content.definition: Instability is clinically diagnosed by observing a varus/valgus thrust during ambulation or demonstrating overall laxity on examination, such as a positive posterior sag test with the knee flexed to ninety degrees.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of TKA instability involves gathering a detailed history—including surgical details and comorbidities—and noting symptoms of pain or subjective instability. Clinical diagnosis relies on physical examination findings such as observing a varus/valgus thrust during gait and demonstrating overall knee laxity or a positive posterior sag sign.

content.title: TKA Instability Imaging
content.definition: Radiographic assessment uses weight-bearing AP, full-length AP, and lateral views to identify component malpositioning and bone cuts, such as excessive femoral resection or increased tibial slope, while CT can provide information on component rotation.
content.summary: Imaging studies, primarily radiographs (weightbearing AP, full-length AP, lateral) and CT scans, are utilized to evaluate TKA instability by assessing mechanical alignment, component positioning, and rotation. These studies help identify specific findings, such as excessive resection or component mismatch, that differentiate the types of instability present.

content.title: TKA Instability Studies
content.definition: The primary studies for TKA instability involve serum labs (CBC, ESR, CRP) and knee aspiration to rule out infection as a potential cause of symptomatic laxity.
content.summary: Studies used in the evaluation of total knee arthroplasty instability include serum laboratory tests (CBC, ESR, CRP) and knee aspiration. These diagnostic measures are essential for ruling out periprosthetic joint infection as a potential cause of TKA failure or symptoms.

content.title: TKA Instability Extension (varus-valgus) Instability
content.definition: Symmetrical instability results from excessive distal femoral resection, while asymmetrical instability is caused by uncorrected coronal plane deformity (ligament failure). Treatment involves distal femoral augments or, if needed, a varus/valgus constrained device.
