content.summary: Extension (varus-valgus) instability is defined as varus or valgus laxity following total knee arthroplasty, classified as symmetrical when excessive distal femoral resection causes a mismatch between the flexion and extension gaps, or asymmetrical due to failure to correct the preoperative coronal plane deformity.

content.title: TKA Instability Flexion (anteroposterior) Instability
content.definition: This occurs when the flexion gap exceeds the extension gap, caused by over-resection of the posterior femoral condyles, undersizing the femoral component, or excessive tibial slope, requiring treatment with posterior augments, component upsizing, or conversion to a Posterior Stabilized (PS) prosthesis.
content.summary: Flexion (anteroposterior) instability is a complication of total knee arthroplasty characterized by a flexion gap that exceeds the extension gap. This mismatch leads to excessive anteroposterior laxity, which can result in knee dislocation, often termed "jumping the post" in posterior-stabilized implants.

content.title: TKA Instability Mid-flexion Instability
content.definition: This is a controversial type of instability involving malrotation when the knee is flexed between 45 degrees and 90 degrees, often associated with modifications to the joint line or femoral component design, and typically necessitates a full revision to restore appropriate gap balance.
content.summary: Mid-flexion instability is a controversial type of mechanical failure following total knee arthropllasty that presents as instability or malrotation when the knee is flexed between 45 and 90 degrees. This condition is typically associated with modification of the joint line, issues with femoral component design, or attenuation of the anterior medial collateral ligament.

content.title: TKA Instability Genu recurvatum
content.definition: Defined as a fixed valgus deformity combined with an iliotibial band contracture, this deformity is associated with conditions like poliomyelitis or rheumatoid arthritis and requires treatment with long-stemmed constrained implants (PS or varus/valgus constrained).
content.summary: Genu recurvatum is characterized by a fixed valgus deformity and iliotibial band contracture, often manifesting clinically as the knee locking in hyperextension during ambulation. This instability is frequently associated with underlying conditions such as poliomyelitis, rheumatoid arthritis, or Charcot arthropathy.

search_keywords: Severe TKA bone loss and instability

content.title: TKA Instability Global, multiply-operated instability
content.definition: This severe form of instability involves multidirectional ligamentous laxity in both flexion and extension, often accompanied by significant bone loss, requiring the use of a highly constrained hinged prosthesis or endoprosthetic replacement as salvage options.
content.summary: Global, multiply-operated instability is a severe form of knee laxity affecting multiple planes, specifically involving both flexion and extension gaps along with varus/valgus instability. This complex condition typically occurs in previously operated knees and may be associated with severe bone loss.

content.title: TKA Aseptic Loosening Summary
content.definition: TKA Aseptic Loosening is a macrophage-induced inflammatory response that results in bone loss and implant loosening in the absence of an infection, typically diagnosed with plain radiographs and treated by revision arthroplasty.
content.summary: Aseptic loosening of a Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA) is a sterile, macrophage-induced inflammatory response caused by particulate debris, leading to progressive bone loss (osteolysis) and subsequent implant instability. It is diagnosed primarily via plain radiographs showing progressive radiolucency (> 2 mm) at the bone-prosthesis interface, and definitive treatment usually involves revision arthroplasty.

content.title: TKA Aseptic Loosening Etiology
content.definition: The etiology involves a process where prosthesis micromotion and particulate debris (from wear, backside wear, or cement issues) trigger macrophage-activated osteolysis, which is exacerbated by malalignment and high patient activity levels.
content.summary: The etiology of aseptic total knee arthroplasty (TKA) loosening is primarily driven by the formation of particulate debris, which triggers macrophage-activated osteolysis and subsequent prosthesis micromotion. Contributing factors also include excessive polyethylene wear rates due to surgical or material factors, and mechanical failure at the cement-implant or cement-bone interfaces.

content.title: TKA Aseptic Loosening Presentation
content.definition: Aseptic loosening presents as local pain (around loose components) that is typically absent in early disease but aggravated by weight bearing and activity, with a physical exam often showing increased pain only during weight bearing.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of TKA aseptic loosening is often characterized by chronic, localized pain exacerbated by weight-bearing, although early disease may be painless. Diagnosis is primarily confirmed radiographically by identifying progressive radiolucent lines greater than 2 mm at the cement-bone or implant-bone interfaces, or by observing implant subsidence.

content.title: TKA Aseptic Loosening Studies
content.definition: Radiographs are the primary imaging modality for TKA aseptic loosening, showing characteristic signs like radiolucent lines > 2 mm at the implant interface, implant migration, or cement cracking, though CT or MRI may be necessary to assess the full extent of osteolysis for preoperative planning.
content.summary: The key laboratory findings used in the workup for aseptic total knee arthroplasty (TKA) loosening are normal serum levels of erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). This normalcy is critical in distinguishing aseptic loosening from a periprosthetic joint infection.

content.title: TKA Aseptic Loosening Differential
content.definition: To rule out periprosthetic joint infection, serum labs are critical, but in aseptic loosening, both ESR and CRP will be normal, confirming the non-infectious nature of the process.
content.summary: The differential diagnosis in the context of total knee arthroplasty (TKA) loosening centers critically on excluding a periprosthetic joint infection (PJI), as both conditions can present with pain and evidence of component loosening.

content.title: TKA Aseptic Loosening Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is nonoperative observation for stable, minimally symptomatic implants, but requires revision TKA when the patient experiences pain, evidence of osteolysis, or extensive osteolysis that compromises future surgery, using either bone graft or metal augments or wedges for defects greater than ten millimeters.
content.summary: Management of TKA aseptic loosening depends on severity, ranging from observation for stable implants with minimal symptoms to operative intervention. Definitive treatment, indicated for pain, osteolysis, or progressive instability, is surgical revision total knee arthroplasty, often utilizing bone grafts or metal augments to address associated bone defects.

content.title: TKA Patellar Maltracking Summary
content.definition: TKA Patellar Maltracking is the most common reason for secondary surgery after TKA, diagnosed clinically by anterior knee pain, subluxation, and limited flexion, with treatment depending on the underlying etiology.
content.summary: TKA Patellar Maltracking is a leading cause of patient dissatisfaction and the most frequent reason for secondary surgery following total knee arthroplasty. Diagnosis is typically clinical, marked by anterior knee pain, patellar subluxation, and limitation in knee flexion, often resulting from prosthetic design issues, component malrotation, or extensor mechanism imbalance.

content.title: TKA Patellar Maltracking Epidemiology
content.definition: Patellar maltracking is the most common reason for secondary surgery following total knee arthroplasty.
content.summary: Patellar maltracking is a common cause of patient dissatisfaction following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) and represents the most frequent indication for secondary surgery after the initial procedure.

content.title: TKA Patellar Maltracking Etiology
content.definition: Causes of maltracking are multifactorial, including prosthetic design issues, extensor mechanism imbalance, asymmetric patellar resection, malpositioning, and most critically, malrotation (especially internal rotation of the femoral component, which increases the Q angle).
content.summary: Etiology refers to the underlying causes of TKA Patellar Maltracking, which encompasses technical factors related to prosthetic design and implant placement. Specific etiologies include component malrotation, extensor mechanism imbalance, asymmetric patellar resection, and patellar malpositioning.

content.title: TKA Patellar Maltracking Presentation
content.definition: The clinical presentation involves patient complaints of peri-patellar pain, feelings of subluxation or frank dislocation, and limited flexion.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of TKA patellar maltracking is characterized by patient complaints of peri-patellar pain, feelings of subluxation, or frank dislocation, often coupled with physical findings such as limited knee flexion.

content.title: TKA Patellar Maltracking Imaging
content.definition: Imaging uses AP views to assess the Q angle and femoral component position, lateral views for femoral size and rotation, and tangential views to assess patellar subluxation and tilt. A CT scan is the best modality to assess rotational malalignment.
content.summary: Imaging is a critical diagnostic step for TKA patellar maltracking, primarily utilizing AP, lateral, and tangential radiographs to evaluate component placement, patellar subluxation, and Q angle. Computerized tomography (CT) scanning is also employed to accurately assess for rotational malalignment of the components.

content.title: TKA Patellar Maltracking Treatment
content.definition: Treatment requires appropriately addressing the underlying etiology of the maltracking.
content.summary: Treatment for TKA Patellar Maltracking may be nonoperative or operative, depending on the severity and specific cause of the condition. Effective management requires the treatment strategy to be precisely selected to address the underlying etiology, such as prosthetic malposition or extensor mechanism imbalance.

content.title: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening Summary
content.definition: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening is a complication following TKA that is more common with metal-backed patellar components, diagnosed by plain radiographs, and generally treated by revision arthroplasty of the patellar component.
content.summary: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening is a complication following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) where the patellar component loses adequate fixation, commonly presenting as persistent knee pain. Diagnosis is confirmed using plain radiographs that show signs such as component dissociation or thinning polyethylene, typically necessitating surgical revision of the component.

content.title: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence is 0.6%-5% of cases, with a higher occurrence in metal-backed patellae.
content.summary: Epidemiology is the medical study concerning the incidence, distribution, and control of a disease within a defined population. For TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening, this study reveals an incidence ranging from 0.6% to 5%, occurring more commonly with metal-backed patellar components.

content.title: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening Etiology
content.definition: The etiology involves factors that induce high shear stress, such as maltracking, overstuffing, high flexion (>100°), obesity, poor bone stock, and disruption of patellar perfusion from surgical releases, leading to secondary osteonecrosis and osteolysis.
content.summary: The etiology of TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening involves the primary causes and predisposing risk factors leading to component failure. Causes include mechanical issues like maltracking generating high shear forces and biological factors such as subclinical infection, while risks include obesity, poor bone stock, and inadequate implant fixation.

content.title: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening Presentation
content.definition: The presentation is persistent knee pain.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of TKA patellar prosthesis loosening is characterized primarily by persistent knee pain. Diagnosis is confirmed through radiographic imaging using lateral and tangential views, which typically reveal loss of the radiolucent space, thinning of the polyethylene component, or dissociation of the patellar implant.

content.title: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening Imaging
content.definition: Imaging relies on lateral and tangential views to look for findings such as the loss of the radiolucent space (bone-on-component contact), a dissociated patellar component (seen adjacent to the patella), or thinning of the polyethylene.
content.summary: Imaging utilized for diagnosing TKA patellar prosthesis loosening consists of plain radiographs, using specific lateral and tangential views of the knee. Radiographic signs include loss of the radiolucent joint space, thinning of the polyethylene liner, or dissociation of the patellar component.

content.title: TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is typically operative revision of the patellar component, with resection of the patellar component considered if revision fails.
content.summary: The standard treatment for TKA Patellar Prosthesis Loosening is operative revision arthroplasty of the patellar component; if this primary revision procedure fails, subsequent resection of the patellar component is performed.

content.title: Patellar Clunk Syndrome Summary
content.definition: Patellar Clunk Syndrome is a painful, palpable clunk at the patellofemoral articulation of a posterior stabilized TKA, caused by a fibrous nodule of scar tissue at the distal quadriceps tendon catching on the femoral component's box during knee extension.
content.summary: Patellar Clunk Syndrome is a complication of posterior stabilized total knee arthroplasty (TKA) resulting in a painful, palpable clunk felt during knee extension, usually around 40 degrees of flexion. This symptom is caused by a fibrous nodule of scar tissue catching on the intercondylar box of the femoral component.

content.title: Patellar Clunk Syndrome Epidemiology
content.definition: With a reported prevalence of 3.5%, the risk factors include preoperative patella baja or valgus alignment, previous knee surgery, and technical errors such as small or low-placed patellar components, or increased posterior femoral condylar offset of the femoral component.
content.summary: Epidemiology of Patellar Clunk Syndrome details its frequency, reporting a prevalence of approximately 3.5%, and identifies associated risk factors. These risks include preoperative factors like patella baja and specific component characteristics such as small patellar components or posterior stabilized femoral designs.

content.title: Patellar Clunk Syndrome Etiology
content.definition: The mechanism involves a fibrous nodule of scar tissue (whose cause is unknown) at the distal quadriceps tendon/superior patellar pole becoming entrapped within the femoral intercondylar box during flexion and being forced out of the notch during active knee extension, creating a painful snap typically felt between 30 degrees and 45 degrees.
content.summary: The etiology of Patellar Clunk Syndrome is the formation of a fibrous nodule of scar tissue at the posterior surface of the distal quadriceps tendon or superior patellar pole in a posterior stabilized total knee arthroplasty. This scarring is potentially initiated by direct trauma during patellar resection or impingement from an undersized patellar component, causing the nodule to catch on the femoral component box during extension.

content.title: Patellar Clunk Syndrome Presentation
content.definition: The syndrome typically presents an average of 12 months after TKA with complaints of patellofemoral pain and a painful, palpable "pop" or "catch" during physical exam as the knee is actively extended at approximately 40 degrees of flexion.
content.summary: Patients typically present an average of 12 months following total knee arthroplasty with patellofemoral pain and complaints of popping or catching in the knee. The key physical finding is a painful, palpable "pop" or "catch" elicited during knee extension, specifically around 40 degrees of flexion.

content.title: Patellar Clunk Syndrome Imaging
content.definition: Imaging with ultrasound (US) can show the suprapatellar fibrous tissue, while the patellar crunch syndrome is a related condition where scar accumulation around the patellar component causes a crunching sensation during motion.
content.summary: Imaging, specifically utilizing Ultrasound (US), is employed in the diagnosis of Patellar Clunk Syndrome by visualizing the characteristic suprapatellar fibrous tissue associated with the condition.

content.title: Patellar Clunk Syndrome Treatment
content.definition: Treatment is observation for patients with mild symptoms, but surgical resection (arthroscopic or open) of the fibrous nodule is indicated for those with severe symptoms, offering good outcomes and very low rates of recurrence.
content.summary: Treatment for Patellar Clunk Syndrome is guided by symptom severity, involving observation for patients with mild discomfort. For those experiencing severe symptoms, operative management is indicated, typically requiring arthroscopic or open resection of the fibrous nodule that causes the clunk.

content.title: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity Summary
content.definition: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity is a rare complication following TKA that results in persistent knee pain and stiffness due to a T-cell-mediated Type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction to metallic components (primarily nickel in cobalt-chromium alloys).
content.summary: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity is a rare complication of knee replacement characterized by chronic pain, stiffness, and effusion, resulting from a Type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction to metallic implant components, typically nickel. Diagnosis is challenging and often one of exclusion, with definitive treatment involving the exchange to hypoallergenic implants.

content.title: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity Epidemiology
content.definition: The incidence of this allergic reaction is rare.
content.summary: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity is characterized by a very low incidence, meaning it is considered a rare complication following total knee arthroplasty.

content.title: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity Etiology
content.definition: The etiology is a Type IV delayed-type cell-mediated hypersensitivity reaction to metal ions (like nickel) deposited in local tissue, with the hypersensitivity potentially initiating early implant loosening.
content.summary: The cause of TKA metal hypersensitivity is a Type IV, T-cell mediated, delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction, typically initiated by nickel ions released from cobalt-chromium implant alloys. These metal ions deposit in local tissue and react with proteins to trigger the immune response.

content.title: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity Presentation
content.definition: The presentation includes a patient history of atopic hypersensitivity (e.g., eczema), and symptoms of vague persistent pain, unremitting swelling/effusion, and stiffness, often without objective signs of infection or loosening.
content.summary: The clinical presentation of TKA metal hypersensitivity is characterized by vague, persistent pain, stiffness, and chronic, unremitting effusion in the affected knee. Patients may also present with a prior history of generalized atopic conditions like dermatitis or eczema.

content.title: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity Studies
content.definition: Studies for diagnosis are controversial but may include patch testing, lymphocyte transformation tests (LST), and T-lymphocyte-rich immunohistopathology, though the definitive diagnosis is often one of exclusion after ruling out infection and aseptic loosening.
content.summary: Studies used to evaluate TKA metal hypersensitivity include patch testing, the lymphocyte transformation test (LST), and T-lymphocyte rich immunohistopathology. A negative work-up for acute or chronic infection must also be completed as part of the evaluation to rule out other causes of persistent symptoms.

content.title: TKA Metal Hypersensitivity Treatment
content.definition: The treatment is operative implant exchange to a hypoallergenic femoral component with an all-polyethylene tibial component for patients with persistent, debilitating symptoms.
content.summary: The definitive treatment for TKA metal hypersensitivity is operative implant exchange, indicated when persistent symptoms significantly affect the patient's quality of life. This procedure involves replacing the existing components with a hypoallergenic femoral component and an all-polyethylene tibial component.

content.title: TKA Other Complications Introduction
content.definition: The topic of other TKA complications is categorized into separate topics covering neurovascular injuries (peroneal nerve palsy, vascular injury), extensor mechanism issues (patellar loosening, clunk, maltracking, rupture), and general complications (infection, fracture, metal hypersensitivity, wound complications, instability, and stiffness).
content.summary: This introductory section provides a navigational overview and index, listing the specific complications of Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA)—including neurovascular injuries, extensor mechanism failures, infection, and instability—that are discussed in detail in separate subsequent topics.

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Summary
content.definition: TKA Revision is most commonly performed to address mechanical failure, fracture, instability, or infection associated with a prior TKA, with diagnosis and treatment dependent on the failure's etiology and the patient's status.
content.summary: TKA revision surgery is necessitated by failure etiologies such as infection, aseptic loosening, or instability, requiring a thorough diagnostic assessment involving physical examination, labs, and radiographs. The procedure aims to extract the failed components, restore bone deficiencies using specialized reconstruction methods, and utilize appropriate constrained prostheses to achieve ligament balance and joint stability.

search_keywords: Most common reason for TKA revision

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Etiology
content.definition: The most common causes for TKA failure are infection (most common cause overall and for early failure) and aseptic component loosening (most common cause of late failure), followed by ligament/flexion instability and periprosthetic fracture.
content.summary: Etiology refers to the underlying cause or mechanism leading to the failure of a medical procedure, such as a primary Total Knee Arthroplasty. The most common etiologies requiring TKA revision are infection, aseptic component loosening, instability, and periprosthetic fracture.

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Presentation
content.definition: The presentation requires a comprehensive history of the original TKA and current comorbidities, with physical exam assessing for pain (new onset or persistent), stiffness, instability (thrust), gait abnormalities, and signs of infection (warmth, effusion, skin changes).
content.summary: The presentation of a failed TKA encompasses the patient's medical history, subjective symptoms (like pain, stiffness, and instability), and objective physical examination findings used to determine the etiology of failure. This evaluation is crucial for differentiating mechanical issues from infection, often relying on assessing the temporal course of symptoms, gait, range of motion, and ligamentous stability.

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Imaging
content.definition: Imaging requires serial weight-bearing radiographs (AP, lateral, skyline, full-length) to track implant movement and wear, supplemented by CT scans to assess component rotation and the severity/location of bony defects, while a bone scan can be used to rule out loosening if negative (but is non-specific if positive).
content.summary: Imaging is essential for diagnosing the etiology of total knee arthroplasty (TKA) failure and typically involves serial radiographs to track component stability and alignment. Specialized modalities such as computed tomography (CT) help assess component rotation and bone defects, while bone scans may be utilized to evaluate for loosening, infection, or stress fracture.

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Studies
content.definition: Studies are critical to rule out infection, requiring serum labs (CBC, ESR, CRP) and knee aspiration (for cell count and culture).
content.summary: In the workup for Total Knee Arthroplasty failure, "Studies" refers to the essential diagnostic tests used to identify the underlying etiology, most notably serum labs (CBC, ESR, CRP) and knee aspiration with cell count and culture, which are critical for ruling out periprosthetic joint infection.

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Technique - Prosthesis Selection
content.definition: Prosthesis selection for revision is guided by the degree of ligamentous deficiency: unconstrained (CR/PS) if ligaments are intact, constrained nonhinged for varus/valgus instability, and constrained hinged (rotating platform) or distal femoral replacement (salvage) for global ligament deficiency or massive bone loss.
content.summary: Prosthesis selection in TKA revision involves choosing the level of implant constraint (ranging from cruciate retaining to highly constrained hinged or replacement systems) necessary to restore stability, which is determined primarily by the integrity of the remaining knee ligaments and the extent of bone deficiency.

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Technique - General Steps
content.definition: The general steps for TKA revision involve surgical exposure (often extensile or with a tibial tubercle osteotomy), tibial side removal first to establish the joint line (1.5 - 2 cm above the fibula head), followed by the femoral side to match the tibia, balancing the flexion/extension and medial/lateral gaps, and addressing patellofemoral tracking.
content.summary: The general steps for total knee arthroplasty revision involve achieving extensile surgical exposure and meticulously removing the failed implants while preserving bone stock. Reconstruction then proceeds by establishing the correct joint line, balancing the flexion-extension gaps and medial/lateral gaps, and addressing patellofemoral tracking to ensure a stable outcome.

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Technique - Bone Defect Reconstruction
content.definition: Bone defect reconstruction is classified by the Anderson Orthopaedic Research Institute (AORI) system (Types 1-3) and addressed with long stems (cemented or press-fit) to promote load sharing, with metaphyseal sleeves/trabecular metal cones for cavitary defects >1 cm, and structural allografts/custom implants for massive loss (Type 3).
content.summary: Bone Defect Reconstruction is a crucial step in revision total knee arthroplasty aimed at restoring bone deficiencies and achieving stable implant fixation. This is accomplished by classifying the defect (e.g., AORI) and utilizing long stems for load sharing, combined with materials like augments, cement, metaphyseal sleeves, or structural allografts to fill the voids.

content.title: TKA Revision Techniques Complications
content.definition: Complications are more frequent than in primary TKA, including high rates of infection (up to 4%-7%), stiffness, neurovascular problems (peroneal nerve injury with valgus/flexion correction), skin necrosis (requiring flap coverage), and extensor mechanism disruption.
content.summary: Complications following revision total knee arthroplasty include increased risks of chronic pain, stiffness, and infection (upwards of 4-7%), which is double the rate of primary TKA. Other potential issues involve neurovascular injury, skin necrosis requiring complex coverage, and disruption of the extensor mechanism.

content.back: Osteolysis represents a histiocytic response to wear debris.

content.stem: Which of the following cytokines inhibits the process depicted in Figure A?

options.text: Receptor activator of nuclearfactor kappa (RANK) ligand

explanations.general:
Of the above cytokines, only inteferon gamma has been demonstrated to inhibit osteolysis, the process depicted in Figure A.

The cellular orchestrators of osteolysis in arthroplasty are macrophages, which secrete numerous mediators that incite a complex cascade after activation. This ultimately results in osteoclast maturation and bone resorption. Promoters of osteolysis include interleukin 1, interleukin 6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and RANK B ligand. Inhibitors of osteolysis include interferon gamma, osteoprotegrin, interleukin-4 and interleukin-10.

Talmo et al. reviewed the pathophysiology of osteolysis after total hip arthroplasty. They reported that along with targeting osteoclasts, several cytokines and inflammatory mediators, including TNF-alpha and interleukin 1 are critical participants in osteolysis and may represent key targets for pharmacologic intervention.

Holt et al. reviewed the cell signaling molecules implicated in osteolysis. They reported that the most important mediators were TNF-alpha and IL-1. They highlighted that several studies have reported these molecules to be present in high concentrations in the surrounding pseudomembranes in revision arthroplasty.

Figure A demonstrates retro-acetabular osteolysis after a total hip arthroplasty.

Incorrect Answers:
Answers 1, 2, 3, and 5: Interleukin 1, 6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and RANK B ligand are all promoters (not inhibitors) of osteolysis.

content.stem: A 64-year-old male underwent an uncomplicated right total hip arthroplasty (THA) eight years ago. He now reports the insidious onset of right groin pain that has been present for 10 months and recalcitrant to anti-inflammatory medications and physical therapy. You suspect he has a local soft tissue reaction caused by the main articulation that is mainly lymphocyte driven. Assuming physiologic range of motion and adequate positioning of the components, which of the following articulations does this patient most likely have?

explanations.general: The key finding is a **lymphocyte-driven local soft tissue reaction** (known as ALTR or pseudotumor), developing years after an uncomplicated THA. This reaction is uniquely associated with **Metal-on-Metal** (MoM) bearings due to the generation of metal ions and wear debris that trigger a delayed-type hypersensitivity response.

distractors.opt_A: Metal on Polyethylene is the standard articulation and typically causes osteolysis driven by macrophages reacting to polyethylene debris, not a lymphocyte-driven reaction.
distractors.opt_B: Ceramic on Polyethylene is a common, well-tolerated articulation. Wear debris is polyethylene, leading to macrophage-driven osteolysis.
distractors.opt_D: Ceramic on Ceramic is generally known for its very low wear rate, but ceramic failure (fracture) is a risk. It does not cause a lymphocyte-driven reaction.
distractors.opt_E: Polyethylene on Polyethylene is not a primary articulation used in modern THA; polyethylene is typically articulating with a metal or ceramic head.

content.stem: Compared to a cruciate retaining knee prosthesis, an anterior stabilized prosthesis has what effect on the contact area and what effect on the stability in PCL deficient knees?

options.text: No change in contact area; no change in stability

explanations.general: An anterior stabilized knee prosthesis is composed of a highly congruent polyethylene component with a large anterior lip, which prevents anterior translation of the femur on the tibia. The high conformity of the polyethylene component increases the contact area of the implant. Due to the anterior lip, it confers greater stability than a cruciate retaining prosthesis in a PCL deficient knee.

distractors.opt_A: Incorrect. The highly congruent components of an anterior stabilized knee **increase** the contact area to distribute stress and reduce wear.
distractors.opt_C: Incorrect. There is a definite change; the AS design deliberately increases conformity (contact area) and stability.
distractors.opt_D: Incorrect. The AS design increases both contact area and stability.
distractors.opt_E: Incorrect. The AS design increases both contact area and stability.

content.stem: Which of the following methods in the manufacturing process of polyethylene provides the **MOST** improvement in wear performance?

options.text: Irradiation in an oxygen rich environment

explanations.general: The most common method to improve polyethylene wear performance is **cross-linking**, which is achieved through high-dose **gamma irradiation**. Irradiating the polyethylene in an **inert gas** (such as argon or nitrogen) maximizes the creation of cross-links while minimizing the formation of free radicals, thus achieving the best wear performance.

distractors.opt_A: Ram bar extrusion is a manufacturing method for the bulk material, but it does not achieve the cross-linking necessary for a large improvement in wear performance.
distractors.opt_B: Sterilization in gas plasma does not create the necessary cross-linking bonds needed to dramatically improve wear characteristics.
distractors.opt_D: Irradiation in an oxygen-rich environment creates excessive **free radicals**, which can lead to oxidation and ultimately degrade the mechanical properties of the polyethylene, adversely affecting its long-term performance.
distractors.opt_E: Sterilization in ethylene oxide does not cause cross-linking and is primarily used for terminal sterilization, not for enhancing wear performance.

content.stem: A 71-year-old man presents to the clinic for the annual follow-up after undergoing a total knee replacement 7 years prior. He has been very satisfied with his knee overall until when he noticed his incisional scar began leaking fluid two months ago. His primary care physician has put him on antibiotics, which helped stop the drainage for a couple of weeks. However, one week after antibiotic completion he continues to endorse draining fluid from his incisional site. A clinical image of his knee is shown in Figure A. A cotton-tipped swab confirms the wound traces to the intra-articular space. Which of the following are the next best steps in management?

options.text: Obtain baseline labs, aspirate the knee, and wait for results to see if he has a periprosthetic infection
Order baseline labs, perform clinic wound care and prescribe oral antibiotics
Obtain baseline labs, admit the patient, and perform wound irrigation and débridement with polyethylene exchange
Obtain baseline labs, aspirate the knee, and bring him back for one stage revision
Obtain baseline labs, aspirate the knee, and bring him back for two staged revision

explanations.general: This patient has a **chronic PJI** (symptoms > 6 weeks) based on the presence of a sinus tract communicating with the joint (confirmed by the swab test) and the long duration. According to international consensus (MSIS) and major orthopedic societies (AAOS), the current gold standard for the treatment of chronic PJI is a **two-staged revision arthroplasty**. This involves prosthesis removal, cement spacer placement, and a subsequent second surgery for reimplantation after infection eradication is confirmed.

distractors.opt_A: Incorrect. The patient already meets the diagnostic criteria for chronic PJI (sinus tract present), so there is no need to 'wait to see' if he has an infection; the infection is confirmed.
distractors.opt_B: Incorrect. Performing wound care and prescribing antibiotics is insufficient for treating chronic PJI. The infected implant must be surgically addressed.
distractors.opt_C: Incorrect. Performing a simple debridement, antibiotics, and implant retention (**DAIR**) is reserved for **acute** PJI (usually symptoms < 3 weeks) in well-fixed components, or acute hematogenous infections. This patient has a chronic infection (7 years post-op, 2 months of symptoms).
distractors.opt_D: Incorrect. While one-stage revision is common in some European centers, two-staged revision is the accepted gold standard in the US for chronic PJI due to lower rates of recurrence.

content.stem: When investigating a periprosthetic hip or knee joint infection, which marker is most sensitive and specific for infection?

explanations.general: Synovial alpha-defensin has been shown to be 100% sensitive and 98% specific for periprosthetic joint infections. It is an antimicrobial peptide produced by neutrophils in synovial fluid, making it a highly specific marker directly related to the presence of acute infection.

distractors.opt_A: Intraoperative frozen section has a good sensitivity (86%) and specificity (90-95%), but is not as sensitive or specific as alpha-defensin. It is also an intraoperative procedure, not a standalone marker.
distractors.opt_B: Serum IL-6 is a useful inflammatory marker with high sensitivity (100%) but lower specificity (95%) than alpha-defensin.
distractors.opt_C: CRP has demonstrated sensitivity of 69–96% and specificity of 70–77% for PJI, but can be affected by renal clearance and other systemic conditions, making it less specific than alpha-defensin.
distractors.opt_D: Studies have compared synovial alpha-defensin to LE colorimetric strip testing and have shown alpha-defensin to be more sensitive and specific for PJI. The LE strip test typically has a sensitivity of 97-100% but lower specificity (69-81%).

content.stem: Which of the following patients requires preoperative noninvasive cardiac testing?

options.text: 52-year-old man scheduled to undergo elective lumbar decompression and fusion, with a history of hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, and deep venous thrombosis following treatment of an ankle fracture
67-year-old woman scheduled to undergo total knee arthroplasty, with a history of myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, and diabetes mellitus

68-year-old woman scheduled to undergo total hip arthroplasty for osteonecrosis, no known medical comorbidities, but has not seen a physician in more than 20 years and drinks 7 to 10 alcoholic beverages per week
72-year-old man with an intertrochanteric hip fracture, with a history of chronic renal failure, colon cancer, and obesity
81-year-old man with a fracture of the proximal femur, history of myocardial infarction, prostate adenocarcinoma, and hypothyroidism, and prior to the fracture he was able to climb a flight of stairs

explanations.general: Noninvasive cardiac testing is recommended in the presence of **three or more** of the following risk factors in a sedentary patient: history of coronary artery disease or myocardial infarction, history of heart failure, prior cerebrovascular accident, and diabetes mellitus or chronic renal disease. The patient in option B has **three major risk factors** (MI, CVA, and DM) and is undergoing elective surgery (THA), making cardiac workup necessary.

distractors.opt_A: This patient has only two major risk factors (HTN, hypercholesterolemia) and DVT is not one of the criteria for cardiac testing necessity.
distractors.opt_B: This patient has no known major risk factors; alcohol abuse and failure to see a physician are not the primary indications for noninvasive cardiac testing per the standard guidelines.
distractors.opt_C: This patient has two major risk factors (Chronic Renal Failure, Obesity), but the surgery is for a hip fracture (acute setting) and the number of risk factors does not meet the 'three or more' threshold for elective procedures.
distractors.opt_D: The key here is functional status. This patient had a history of MI but was able to climb a flight of stairs (a 4+ MET activity). If patients can perform activities > 4 METs (like climbing a flight of stairs), preoperative stress testing is usually unnecessary, even with a history of cardiac disease.
